AdrianM: Perhaps, if that's the case then what I have in mind might not be possible
RadianceCausality: what did you have in mind
AdrianM: That's unfortunately difficult to explain and I'm still not sure if it'll work...
AdrianM: But the basic idea is if we cast the probes into the scene and then each one of those probes casts 4 rays, those 4 rays could then be traced through each cascade level and then merged back down including the overlap of other rays that got traced at the same time.
AdrianM: We can use the pixels themselves to store the "progress" of each ray through each cascade as well as being a lookup structure for neighboring rays.
AdrianM: So in the worst case where you have a bunch of different far apart point there would be no reuse, it would just trace it's 4 rays (8 in 3D) and then merge back down those same 4 rays with nothing gained. In the best case where you have every point sampled the rays all overlap and merge down to give the same result as having calculated full holographic radiance cascades.
AdrianM: In theory using some kind of a hash function can map the starting positions of each sample to a ray path which will be unique and won't be redundant with any other ray.

RadianceCausality: might try to understand it later but not rn

chronicl: here is a thought I had when alexander first mentioned prefiltering directionally and conally to me before. 
I essentially had the idea of doing cone prefiltering split into the same four directions as we are doing holographic rc now and creating bigger cones by merging three cones in one direction (red) with one cone in the other direction (blue). I never conceptualized this further, because some things wouldn't have made sense for prefiltering, however, now for holographic rc where we are representing these cones with rays, I wonder if this idea could be useful.
the main difficulty is, how do we represent these cones and how would merging happen. the currenty way of doing this in holographic rc makes more sense to me then anything I've come up for this, but just wanted to throw it into the room
Alexander: 'conally' is now a word
chronicl: lol yea, I was about to put a (?) behind it
Alexander: sean conally
Alexander: merged cones should be assembled from parts that are marched in the same direction
Alexander: it's not like a blur kernel where only shape matters
Alexander: for example a filter shape might look like a box, but the box might be marched from the left, from the right, top or bottom
chronicl: I was thinking along the lines of bidirectionally tracing every ray you cast. bidirectionally meaning you march in one way, but accumulate two seperate colors, one for each direction, with different accumulation strategies
chronicl: so although the blue cone is facing left, it would also have information for it's rays if they were cast from end to start, which the right facing red cones could use
Alexander: possible yea
chronicl: honestly, that's probably a very good optimization for the current version of holographic rc. you are actually tracing a lot of rays twice, once from start to end and once from end to start when you are doing the opposite direction cascades
Alexander: one thing i should mention about prefiltering: only prefiltering along the marching axis is exact (and is equivalent to extensions). prefiltering tangentially can introduce some error:
Alexander: both segments prefiltered will result in transmittance 0.5, so after merging it'll be 0.25. however, if you were to properly calculate transmittance of their combination, it should be 0
Alexander: however, this only affects occlusion. transparent light sources with no occlusion are exact with prefiltering
chronicl: yep, that's the main reason I gave up on that idea, because I realized for proper occlusion you can't just generalize to four 90 degree cones
Alexander: also on cardinal direction prefiltering:
Alexander: left case will allow light to escape the enclosure if you prefilter it horizontally. right case will block it for any cardinal direction prefiltering
Alexander: this only matters on small scale, so maybe that's not important but it's just a thing to keep in mind that cardinal direction prefiltering can be potentially lossy
chronicl: makes sense

Alexander: the sharp lines inside penumbra gradient means there's too much spatial resolution and not enough angular resolution

RadianceCausality: 256x256 and 2x angular
Alexander: maybe what we should be optimizing is how much accuracy we can get at a fixed atlas resolution and while casting 1 ray per atlas sample
Alexander: compare what kind of scaling works best under those constraints
Alexander: once we establish what discretization works best, we can start looking for ways of how to populate/raymarch that the most efficient way
Alexander: so i think doing quality or quality:perf are both bad metrics, because quality is too easy to optimize by just increasing the atlas size and quality:perf depends too much on the way we raymarch (which should be made irrelevant by extensions eventually)
Alexander: having the same spatial resolution for all cascades imo makes no sense. it has to drop, because penumbras tend to widen and we should exploit that quality. that being said, i don't understand why dropping it 2x per cascade looks so bad. maybe we're doing it wrong.
RadianceCausality: Because it simply doesn't work?

Alexander: yes, but in practice that's a case that doesn't really matter
Alexander: a source that's too small and too far from an occluder is a tradeoff that's worth exploiting
Alexander: basically creating a penumbra that's slightly wider than it should be is an acceptable kind of artifact, most of the time. what's not acceptable is noise, striations and instabilities

RadianceCausality: Anyways

RadianceCausality: Because the bilinear interpolation makes it have noticable artifacts
Alexander: i think the problem is that you can't rely on bilinear interpolation to produce a result without aliasing
Alexander: so while it has a sufficient discretization frequency to produce a result that's detailed enough, bilinear filtering makes it noisy and muddy
Alexander: for example if we were to run one blur pass over each cascade, it'd look much more stable and less muddy, but also lose more detail
Alexander: on another note, i'm integrating cones instead of directions now. the results are very promising:
Alexander: crucially i noticed that integrating over one parameter (that defines both position and angle) results in better accuracy than integrating position and angle as a double integral
Alexander: so yes, the integral should be over a frustum, rather than an integral over an angle and then over area
Alexander: testing just one cascade. left: integrating over direction only, right: integrating over direction+position at the same time

AdrianM: So, I've been thinking about 3D. This approach really doesn't scale to 3D very well at all.
AdrianM: Normal RC has each cascade halving in size for the volumetric case, and constant for the screenspace case.
AdrianM: Holographic cascades has each cascades double in size for the volumetric case, and quadruple in size for the screenspace case.
AdrianM: It's especially annoying because the scaling in 2D works out really well, but for 3D I can't see any way to keep constant cascade sizes without giving up something as the scale increases.
Alexander: unfortunately i found no way to make holographic RC to work for screenspace at all
AdrianM: I believe that
Alexander: because "focus points" of rays have to sit on the lines of probes
Alexander: not because of memory, but because there's no way to interpolate them between lines without introducing error
AdrianM: Can you elaborate what you mean by "focus point" do you mean the point where the probe is projected onto the scene?
Alexander: it's hard to illustrate in mspaing. but imagine each probe has phi directions (in the plane of the screen) and in theta directions (depth-wise, perpendicular to the screen). so all of those directions intersect at the probe position, so probe is the focus point in some sense.
Alexander: so all theta rays have only focus points on the lines
Alexander: so there are no theta rays that we can use between the lines without interpolating over long distance
Alexander: sorry if that sounds confusing but it's specifically a 2.5d problem where we have 2d probes on 1d lines
Alexander: in full 3d there are 2d probes on a 2d plane, so it's not a problem
AdrianM: As I understand it you are projecting a bunch of probes onto the scene based on the camera. Each of these probes then acts as a normal 3D probe but interpolates from it's neighbors while considering depth value (so probes far apart in the Z direction have little influence)
Alexander: yes, that's fine. the problem comes from trying to retrieve different depth rays between lines of probes
AdrianM: I think that problem might actually be solvable, but the memory scaling I can't see a solution to
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: the orange probe will need to long-distance interpolate between the two lines, which is bad
Alexander: because we only want to interpolate short distances, like the distance between two probes on one line, but not between lines
Alexander: scaling is actually not an issue, because i fix the number of theta/depth rays in my screenspace implementation
Alexander: so it does not increase with cascade index
AdrianM: I assume you are scaling the ray/probe counts in a way that is decreasing the resolution as the scale increase.
AdrianM: In other words the shadows would get more blurry as the distance increases
Alexander: i scale phi and theta directions differently
Alexander: theta directions count stays constant, because in screenspace theta ray depth tests are pretty approximate anyway, so there's no point in pretending that they have a higher accuracy than what they actually do
AdrianM: Hmmm, are you tracing the rays in true 3D space or only in the space which the camera can see? For example can offscreen light sources still illuminate things on screen?
Alexander: the screenspace version only casts rays against the depth buffer
Alexander: spwi is the version that casts world rays. with spwi you probably can't get away with a constant number of theta rays.
AdrianM: Oh haha, okay that was not what I had in mind. SPWI is what I've been thinking about.
Alexander: we have pretty extreme performance considerations in poe, so it has to be really fast
Alexander: so stuff like spwi is out of question really
Alexander: it's also not about what's most accurate or what converges to the reference solution or what has fewer ringing artifacts on point sources, it's about what can fit in 2ms on a GTX950
AdrianM: That's interesting. Though I'm not bound by that limitation as I'm not working on a game engine. I'm just curious how far we can go with this.
Alexander: honestly you can't really apply the flatland "i still see some ringing" mentality to anything 2.5d or 3d. they're so much harder to make fast enough, that for any practical implementation minor stuff like ringing is likely going to be your last concern
Alexander: so stuff like bilinear fixes and long shadows are kind of out of the question on the current hardware anyway
AdrianM: I don't think that solution which will run at high framerates while leaving resources left for an entire game to run along side it is possible in 3D. I'd be shocked even to see something that can run in real time with decent quality.
Alexander: well it's just a matter what you consider to be "decent quality"
Alexander: for me it's a question of how well it can look given the limited resources it's realistically going to get
Alexander: the answer is certainly not "you can't run anything at all". there's some GI you can run for for the resources available. it's matter of how well you can make it look
AdrianM: Perhaps 3 dimensions is one dimension too many \:P
Alexander: imo there's no usecase to use 3d. its only practical advantage over 2.5d spwi is that you can do volumetric lighting effects like god rays
Alexander: if you only care about surface lighting, then it's mostly a waste
AdrianM: offscreen lights?
Alexander: spwi don't care where sources are
Alexander: spwi only gathers light on visible surfaces, but it can come from anywhere
AdrianM: Oh, I see
AdrianM: Interesting, you are only using it for ambient lighting and doing the direct lighting separately
Alexander: you can calculate lighting fully with rc, but it's more accurate to caclulate analytic lights analytically
Alexander: i want to find a robust way of integrating cones (instead of rays) that converges to the same result as having more probes/directions
AdrianM: Do you mean tracing cones over the scene, or a different way of interpolating from the parent cascade using cones?
Alexander: i mean how to cast multiple rays per probe and then how to merge probes so that the result converges to the same as if having more probes
Alexander: at least under certain conditions like having only transparent light sources
Alexander: cone tracing (as in, with prefiltering) is an approximation to casting multiple rays which is an approximation to actually casting cones, so i think this terminology is a bit ambiguous
Alexander: i guess my quesion is what should the analytical cones be in order to match the reference solution
AdrianM: If you are using multiple rays to approximate a cone then isn't that still going to work out to a similar cost? I suppose you'd save memory and the merging would be cheaper.
Alexander: on shadertoy memory is the primary limitation to having a higher quality result
AdrianM: Is that a limitation with shadertoy specifically? I believe the limit in webgl is a 4096x4096 texture.

Alexander: (i think)

Alexander: at least shadertoy has a 1024x1024 cubemap as its largest storage and in our webgl legit demo i couldn't create a texture larger than 2048
Alexander: you can but i'd really like to have a way to use less memory and not more if possible
AdrianM: If you are willing to use an SDF then there's a technique called Cone Marching which is similar to Ray Marching
Alexander: we also currently lack a good enough way of thinking about cones instead of a rays. especially when it comes to integrating over probe area and not just its directions.
AdrianM: Does this method not work for cones?
Alexander: "casting rays" is not even relevant to what i'm trying to figure out. might as well consider rays to be analytically exact and O(1) for this
Alexander: it only integrates angles
Alexander: and as i have recently shown, that does not converge to the infinite resolution case. to have that you have to also integrate probe positions
Alexander: somehow
Alexander: you can verify that by taking increasingly more numerical integration steps. it does not increase in quality past a certain point
AdrianM: The area marked in green in that diagram is the angle between the probe position and the current position you want to merge into. Am I misunderstanding what you mean by "integrate probe positions"?
Alexander: the area corresponding to one direction of one probe is not what's on the left, it's what's on the right:
Alexander: probes are not points, they are actually integrals over the area that they occupy
Alexander: which in case of circular rings is arc segments and in case of lines is line segments

Alexander: like caustics vs a focal point
AdrianM: It makes sense to me that there would be a focal point for vanilla RC and that focal point would be the center of the probe. But I'm not sure how that translates to quadrant based RC where all the probes are arranged on lines.

Alexander: actually i can calculate the distance..
AdrianM: Well that's very odd. Is there an optimal distance or does it continue to improve until the limit case where the rays are all parallel?
Alexander: looks like the focal plane is one line away.. but the focal points on that plane are different for each direction :thonk:
Alexander: the optimal distance is where the probe offset is equal to the angle offset
Alexander: so you offset the probe index by x and you offset direction index by x at the same time, where x is in [-0.5..0.5]
Alexander: and then you integrate for x [-0.5..0.5]
AdrianM: Honestly this feels like this is moving us closer to stochastic methods with tracing mutliple rays and averaging the result.
Alexander: "stochastic methods" are an approximate solution to the analytic rendering equation that's continuous
Alexander: i want to find the continuous equation for what each probe is supposed to actually contain
Alexander: (the answer to that question is definitely not one ray)
AdrianM: And I suppose you aren't interested in adaptive methods (such as sparse quadtrees) which have variable performance depending on scene complexity?
Alexander: btw this is also directly applicable to any TAA-like accumulation over time. but we need to know what to accumulate in order for that to converge to the right result.
Alexander: absolutely not
Alexander: first i want to understand what is the data that we're approximating in cascades
Alexander: before making it more complicated
Alexander: i guess just consider one cascade first
Alexander: how do we cast multiple rays per probe so that the result is identical to the analytical solution for all cells that don't contain sources?
Alexander: what is it that we need to average for that to work?
Alexander: yeah the first step is for sure to do that with one cascade i just now realized
AdrianM: That seems like it shouldn't be too hard to answer that question. Isn't it just the same as if the cascade had a large number of probes/rays?
Alexander: it's not, because it inherently contains also the way we gather light from a cascade
AdrianM: Just throw another integral over the intergral we already have and call it a day \:P
Alexander: well yes, just need to figure out which one and where
Alexander: so the problem is that depending on which point you gather radiance at, you actually want to have different integrals
Alexander: and i suspect that you can't do that exactly. but some arrangements will end up having greater error than others
Alexander: ~~maybe i fucked something up, checking~~
ok disregard, in my implementation the number of directions has too be a multiple of 4
Alexander: so here's a setup with one cascade, 10x10 planes/rings, 30 probes per ring, 8 directions per probe:
Alexander: now, if we increase that to 8*64 directions:
Alexander: so far so good, what one should expect
Alexander: however, if instead of increasing the number of directions by 64 times, we instead cast 64 sub-rays per direction in a cone (while keeping 8 directions), this is what we get:
Alexander: imo this is fundamentally the problem. our cones don't converge to the thing they're supposed to converge to
Alexander: maybe the gathering strategy needs to also change?.. will try a couple things
AdrianM: Hmmm, let me know when you get it figured out. I'm interested to see what you come up with.
Alexander: the most bruteforce, the most naive way (i'm just iterating over a whole bunch of directions when gathering with a small step):
Alexander: it definitely does not converge to the same thing. i still see 8 distinct rays that don't merge into continuous cones
Alexander: there has to be a way to do this
Alexander: if not exactly then at least better than this
Alexander: i think the order of business to figure out has to be like this:
1) fix the line/ring count to a low value so that it's easier to examine. like 8x8
2) set the number of probes within each ring to almost infinity, set the number of directions to a low value (like 8), figure out a way to make it converge to the right solution
3) set up the number of probes within each ring to a low value instead (like 4), set the number of directions to an almost infinity and make that work.

i have a feeling that if 2) and 3) work individually, then combining them should also work

RadianceCausality: I don't get what you're referring to

RadianceCausality: ??

RadianceCausality: pcg is being a piece of crap again
RadianceCausality: I'm computing the added noise as pcg(x + y << 16)
RadianceCausality: oh wait its precedence
Mytino: You're making me nervous about our plans of using pcg3d for all our game's procedural generation haha
Mytino: So no issue with pcg after all?
RadianceCausality: @Mytino no pcg3d is fucked anyways
RadianceCausality: this is 1d pcg
RadianceCausality: ```rs
pub fn pcg(v: Expr<u32>) -> Expr<u32> {
    let state = v * 747796405u32 + 2891336453u32;
    let word = ((state >> ((state >> 28u32) + 4u32)) ^ state) * 277803737u32;
    (word >> 22u32) ^ word
}
```
RadianceCausality: ```rs
pub fn pcg3d(v: Expr<Vec3<u32>>) -> Expr<Vec3<u32>> {
    let v = v.var();
    *v = v * 1664525u32 + 1013904223u32;

    *v.x += v.y * v.z;
    *v.y += v.z * v.x;
    *v.z += v.x * v.y;

    *v ^= v >> 16u32;

    *v.x += v.y * v.z;
    *v.y += v.z * v.x;
    *v.z += v.x * v.y;

    **v
}
```
Here's the bad pcg3d
RadianceCausality: if you don't change all 3 inputs much it has pretty obvious aliasing issues
RadianceCausality: anyways just use it and if its obviously bad switch to another rng
RadianceCausality: also just realized that you can replace `x < 0 || x >= size` with `x as u32 >= size` most of the time (where x is `i32`)

RadianceCausality: the casting literaly is a no-op
RadianceCausality: unfortunately the compiler can't optimize since size could be > 2^31

I have the impression it's likely one of the best choices for us with the speed/quality balance, looking at the testing done in "Hash Functions for GPU Rendering" https://www.jcgt.org/published/0009/03/02/

Alexander: i had this idea.. what if instead of storing radiance per direction we stored a sum table of radiance? so that radiance from direction i to j is not a sum, but is `r[j]-r[i]`

Alexander: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prefix_sum
Alexander: we're currently storing radiance for every direction i, the first row of that table. we could store the sum of all directions up to i, to make summing over a range of directions faster

Alexander: basically whenever you have nodes on the boundaries i call it an integer grid. you might also call it a grid of vertices. if boundaries fall exactly between nodes, i call it a half-integer grid, or a cell grid.

texture texels for example is a half-integer/cell grid. vertex grids are kinda more rare, because they're a bit more annoying to handle. not sure of a common example.
RadianceCausality: Actually how does that matter
Alexander: i currently set aside my frustum casts (even though the version casting frustums is the one that yields the best results so far), because I noticed I can't even cast cones properly. continuous cones don't result in anything that remotely resembles infinitely many directions.
Alexander: it's possible to wave this problem away and say that it only manifests when directions are undersampled, but i'm greatly bothered by it nonetheless
Alexander: note that the easiest way to transition from frustums to cones without losing accuracy is to increase the number of probes to infinity. this way frustums become very narrow and they should converge to the same results that cones do.
Alexander: this is similar thinking to how we developed the parallax fix, where we assumed that the number of directions is near-infinite.
chronicl: I read that from you before but didn't quite get what your test case is. Could you describe it again for one quadrant?

Alexander: again, this brings us back to this image
Alexander: this image is 4x4 lines/rings
Alexander: so it's a high cascade
Alexander: this is very close to what i'm testing
Alexander: and it exactly showcases this projection/gathering process: radiance is extracted from adjacent lines (in this case squares/rings)
Alexander: the exact mechanism of how this has to happen optimally is unclear, and considering it does not work for cones, i can't tell what it has to actually be. but there's a lot of ways to implement it that almost work or work in certain limits (such as the image above works for when you have enough probes and directions)
Alexander: note the sharp transitions from cell to cell, that's where one nearest line/ring switches to another. there's no interpolation between them
Alexander: you can also observe a similar image if you change `src_cascade_index = 0`  to some higher value (for example, `src_cascade_index = 5`) in `Image` tab in my shadertoy https://www.shadertoy.com/view/4ctczl in order to observe a higher cascade instead of merging all the way down
Alexander: the main idea is to show how much more information higher cascades store compared to vanilla RC grids and how much both angular and spatial resolution you can obtain from just one cascade in comparison, if you do this projection/gathering procedure
Alexander: one way to think about this projection/gathering is by shooting a lot of (almost infinity) uniformly distributed rays from the point of interest and interpolate each one of them at the point of intersection with the closest line. you interpolate bilinearly two nearby probes and their two nearby directions
Alexander: by iterating over all directions this way you can gather fluence at any point between the lines
chronicl: setting `src_cascade_index` to anything larger than 3 just results in a black screen, but I get what you mean now. when you said only one cascade I was thinking only c0, but you actually meant a high cascade, which makes sense
Alexander: you can increase the number of cascades and set the number accordingly
Alexander: you might have to change the number of probes in order for it to fit into the cubemap, which is kind of annoying
Alexander: so yes, imo the core problem with tracing cones is that we currently have no way of doing that projection step accurately if we trace cones instead of rays
Alexander: and that's essential for doing any kind of TAA or more advanced pre averaging schemes

AdrianM: I've been thinking about how we could use holographic rc for only calculating lighting on surfaces. I see a fundamental problem...

If we take a step back, what is the primary benefit of holographic rc? It's that it allows us to reuse rays from higher cascades. Let's look at a single ray (blue), you can see in the diagram on the left it gets to be reused by all the points along the green line.

Now imagine that we only care about lighting on surfaces. In this case you can see in the diagram on the right that the blue ray gets to be reused by only a single point (the surface it hits) and then by definition it blocks the light preventing it from being used by any other surfaces.

This means that the primary advantage offered by this approach is almost completely negated in the case where we only want to render surface lighting. That's fine for volumetric rendering but a pretty big limitation for almost anything else.
AdrianM: I don't see any possible way around this limitation, but I'd be glad to be proven wrong.
RadianceCausality: Yeah
If we put the HRC probes on surfaces, we'd have to extend them arbitrarily long and then you might as well just trace 1000 directions for every surface point and not do cascades
RadianceCausality: Normal RC can be put on surfaces, since then we still do halving the spatial resolution when increasing angular resolution
AdrianM: That's not entirely correct, you can imagine that a probe on a surface that is almost entirely occluded only has to trace a few rays nearby, not hypothetically thousands of rays that would be needed to approximate far away details.
RadianceCausality: Also if the total surface area is O(n) for a n\*n screen, then the number of total traces necessary is O(n\*n) which is one trace per pixel
RadianceCausality: Hmm yeah
But you can't really do reuse well, so that could be handled using a different scheme
AdrianM: My thoughts exactly
RadianceCausality: Also that might be arguable as "this isn't important"
AdrianM: If we use what we currently have for surface lighting then it will be constant time by that constant time will always be equivalent to the worst possible case
AdrianM: But honestly none of this gets us anywhere close to realtime 3D
RadianceCausality: Well surface probe normal RC seems to work
RadianceCausality: Which is why I want to try a surfel thing
Alexander: imo the way to use holographic RC on surfaces would be to create a grid of lines on the surface
Alexander: as opposed to treating surfaces as 2d holograms, surfaces should have 1d holographic lines on them
AdrianM: Can you elaborate what you envision? I don't see how that solves the problem stated above.
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: this is a patch of a 3d surface
Alexander: it's like a light map, it uses the surface uv to capture radiance on the surface
Alexander: but instead of using a regular grid, we use the holographic grid
Alexander: so it still captures overlaps, but instead of capturing rays that overlap, it captures ray projections that overlap
AdrianM: What do you mean by "ray projection" are the probes pretending like they are 2D or something?
Alexander: well, yes, there's a problem that we currently have no way of encoding a 4d radiance field (2d position + 2d direction) on a 2d holographic grid. we only have options to include 3d radiance field (2d position + 1d direction) or a 4d radiance field on a usual grid of 2d probes (vanilla RC in UV space)
Alexander: it's the same problem that prevents using holographic RC in 2.5d/screenpace/spwi
Alexander: the only way to apply holographic RC to anything 3d is to use intersecting holographic 3d planes:
Alexander: but imagine there's an array of parallel planes perpendicular to each axis, each planes is a 2d grid of 2d probes
AdrianM: Shouldn't there be a 3rd plane there?
Alexander: yes but that's a random image from the internet
Alexander: there's an array of those planes, not just 1
Alexander: along each axis
Alexander: but that doesn't scale efficiently enough, compared to screenspace/spwi
AdrianM: Yes, this aligns with my understanding so far
Alexander: that does scale better than a full 3d, but it's not good enough
Alexander: i mean it has the same memory requirement as storing a voxel world. i wouldn't say it's a dealbreaker for a demo, but i do think it's too much for an all-in-1 lighting solution
AdrianM: Where I lost track is how encoding the radiance onto the 2D surfaces of a 3D object fixes the problem - the problem being that when one a ray of light hits a surface it is by definition blocked so it can't be reused by any other surface point.
Alexander: the problem is that it's perfectly possible with a vanilla rc grid. again, you can think of the hologram from my video, but stored in uv space on every object's surface
Alexander: but how to transform that hologram to use a hologram/ring grid instead of a regular rc grid, is unclear (i don't know how)
AdrianM: It seems to me that switching to holographic rc for surfaces will always negate any potential for reusing rays, regardless of any clever scheme/topology we use. Any solution to that is going to as a consequence sacrifice hard shadows. That isn't a problem for vanilla rc but I can't see any way to avoid it for holographic rc.
Alexander: to me the "hard shadows" argument is just a byproduct of "we store information more densely and efficiently in higher cascades". in fact, we store information so much more densely, that in flatland it's sufficient to mitigate penumbras at infinite distance. but to me the goal is storing information with more efficiency and less redundancy, and "having zero angle penumbras at infinity" is just a measure of this efficiency. so there's infinitely many intermediate steps where we could increase efficiency by a lot, but not quite enough to represent zero penumbra shadows, but that's still potentially vastly useful.
AdrianM: My understanding is that anything we do to reduce information density is going to require a trade off somewhere else. It's just a question of which trade offs are acceptable. Does that align with how you see things? Or do you still think there is some inherant redundancy we could tease out?
Alexander: no, i see holographic encoding in flatland as we're removing redundancy (we're removing overlapping rays), so we can store more information in the same amount of memory
Alexander: i wouldn't say that holographic representation is somehow magically encoding more information that should be able to fit into the memory. no, it's just that vanilla RC encodes it in a redundant/inefficient way.
AdrianM: I agree with that
Alexander: but in 2.5d/spwi/uvspace i don't see the same kind of redundancy that we can exploit
Alexander: because rays never actually overlap
Alexander: because they're on a sphere and not on a circle
AdrianM: That's the conclusion I've come to as well, I think we are on the same page just looking at things from different perspectives
Alexander: i think this way of thinking about a grid of probes is very useful: we need to find what aspects of it are redundant and then how to represent the same thing without those redundancies
AdrianM: You're talking about applying these ideas back to vanilla rc?
Alexander: well.. keep in mind that all our current representations (including holographic) are still very redundant:
Alexander: this orange object will be encoded for every probe on this holographic plane
Alexander: so if that object is tiny, then the fact that it exists will be duplicated as many times as there are probes
Alexander: in vanilla RC it's even worse, because it'll be duplicated lots more time
AdrianM: I've had that thought but do you think there is any way around that?
AdrianM: Mainly because you don't know if orange will be occluded by anything from a given perspective without casting a ray
AdrianM: Also keep in mind for it to run in constant time it needs to be able to handle the worst case scenario, so there may be reduntancy in some cases, but not in all. Not that I'm against exploring adaptive approaches, but that's a different discussion.
Alexander: keep in mind that there exists a representation that does not have this redundancy:
Alexander: it's called a height field
Alexander: the downside to a height field is that even though it has no redundancy, it has no efficient way of querying rays at an angle
AdrianM: I've thought a bit about this, the other problem is that you have no way of knowing what is behind something
Alexander: what you depicted is a self-inconsistent state that's allowed by holographic representation but that can't exist in real life. the fact that we allow it is bad, because we allow encoding things that can't exist.
Alexander: that's where the holographic approach comes in: we just create more planes if we want to have more "slices"
Alexander: so the frequency of planes defines how frequently we want to capture intersections
AdrianM: It's possible to have a bunch of tiny floating points. But in general I agree with your point.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: This is the problem I see with depth based approaches
AdrianM: There's no way to see the orange light source without casting a ray at an angle
AdrianM: Or really seeing anything behind the blue wall at any angle
Alexander: it's more like this imo
Alexander: the frequency of those planes that capture height maps define how many "layers" of geometry it captures
Alexander: each heigh map containing only data between its plane and the next one
AdrianM: In that case wouldn't the distance between those planes have to be constant?
Alexander: it is constant
Alexander: that defines the spatial accuracy of representing layers
AdrianM: Well yeah, but in the current implementation we sample from planes with further spacings with each level
AdrianM: So further away things would be seen with less detail
Alexander: but that's fine because they also capture far-field radiance and far field radiance actually doesn't care about capturing more layers
Alexander: from far away you only see the top "layer" of any scene. if you see a house from afar, you actually don't know what's inside of it, and that's good
AdrianM: Hmmm, I'm trying to think if there's any sacrifice there. Seems like we'd still be getting some kind of artifacts on the edges of hard shadows with distant light sources/occlusions
Alexander: so i consider this to be the only non-redundant storage. but it's not an O(1) query for some arbitrary direction. so it's trading efficiency of storage for efficiency of query.
Alexander: imo the problem is not artifacts in this approach, it's that it's much slower to use
AdrianM: I wonder if increasing the number of directions from 4 to something higher would negate this issue...
AdrianM: Here you can see what 3 would look like, but what if we went the other way
Alexander: i think that'd still be redundant, because the same point would be then encoded in multiple different directions
AdrianM: I think you are right
Alexander: what if there's a way to construct a sheme that only uses one direction per probe?..
Alexander: like a height field

Alexander: but anyway
AdrianM: Haha, I just mean in that it encodes directions/points of importance that can be shared
Alexander: no, my point isn't storing things by importance. my point is only storing a horizontal ray like on that image

AdrianM: So if we go back to this case I don't see any way that we can capture both orange and magenta while only having a single ray per probe

Alexander: dashed lines here are cones

Alexander: we definitely can't reconstruct it from one plane, but that's where cascades with multiple planes come in
Alexander: it's about combining cascades that each captures a certain part of the radiance field

Alexander: wait, cones overlapping is not a redundancy of storage. it's a redundancy in how you're going to calculate them, but since each cone stores just one value, i don't consider it to be a redundant representation
AdrianM: Sure, if you want to look at it that way. I still think decreasing the number or rays by using cones is going to also sacrifice hard shadows.
AdrianM: We can get clever using depth information but I don't see any way we can capture everything with less information.
AdrianM: Though I do think there are likely trade offs we can make that are worthwhile
Alexander: well, storing a height field is definitely enough information to calculate full GI from it
Alexander: that's not even a question
Alexander: (yes, it does require storing depth for each direction)
Alexander: because a height field in itself is a sufficient enough representation of one layer of geometry
Alexander: and it's certainly possible to capture multiple layers by capturing height fields with a bunch of planes at a certain distance away from each other
Alexander: to me the problem is that there's no efficient way to calculate diffuse lighting using that representation. like, we can't do merging.
AdrianM: I still don't see how you get around this problem with a height map without making the slices so thin that there is no advantage to using them
Alexander: basically if you put slices every D meters apart that capture a height field from x to x+D, then you can clearly use a height field like that as a reliable way to capture radiance far enough away (for example, 10*D away)
Alexander: think of each field as having an infinite resolution (it's just a height field function)
Alexander: whatever the distance that you decide to put these planes apart from each other, at a certain distance away they serve as a good enough proxy for capturing distant radiance
Alexander: you might have to do raymarching inside of them though, hence capturing the distance
AdrianM: Proxy, exactly. Accuracy is still being sacrificed. Imagine in the case of individual pixels we have something like this. In order for the orange to be captured reliably the slices would have to be 3 or less pixels wide.
Alexander: but to me the whole idea of the penumbra condition is the principle that defines what can be sacrificed to be still usable in practice
Alexander: like, my point is: if we're not sacrificing that, we're doing something inefficiently
AdrianM: Perfect, we're on the same page then
Alexander: like, we don't actually want to be able to capture information about that orange square at a certain distance away, that's bad and means we have over-specified data
AdrianM: If you don't capture it you are either going to lose hard shadows or introduce artifacts. But that's probably fine for most use cases.
Alexander: but like.. we shouldn't have hard shadows at infinite distance imo. that's completely unnecessary in practice for GI
AdrianM: Honestly the vanilla RC actually did a pretty good job of approximating lighting with some loss of accuracy. It's all coming full circle haha.
Alexander: one really important thing is that there needs to be a way to gracefully "fade away" the orange square on that image with distance, which vanilla RC sometimes fails to do. imo the way we fade it out produces much more objectionable artifacts than the fact that we fade it out
Alexander: which means if we had a way to fade it out more smoothly with distance, it'd be perfectly acceptable in practice. but the way it actually happens, sometimes produces artifacts that are objectionable.
AdrianM: This isn't a complete solution to the height map problem but when I was thinking about it I realized that if we go back to the original circular cascades then we potentially can use fewer steps because each step we take makes us closer in line with the "optimal direction"
AdrianM: If we overlap different circles/cascades then we have a choice which one we use (or maybe we can even take advantage of both)
AdrianM: It's still bad, but it's potentially not as bad
Alexander: sorry you lost me with circles
Alexander: i'm not going there again
Alexander: that was too much for my tiny brain
AdrianM: What I mean is that to approximate the red ray we only need to step 4 times in this example even for an infinite distance
AdrianM: Because by the 4th step we are sampling a ray which is going in the same direction as the ray we want to approximate
AdrianM: So each step we take is always going to take us closer to being aligned with one of the sample directions
AdrianM: You can imagine even in the worst possible case (sampling a tangent) you would never need to sample more than 1/4th of the total number or rays, even for an inifinite distance.
Alexander: i guess i see what you're trying to do. it's like raymarching a height field on a ring
AdrianM: In the worst case for a normal flat height map the worst case for an infinitely long ray is infinity

Alexander: that will produce no artifacts for the limit case of having infinitely many directions

Alexander: btw a long time ago i wrote this octree of height maps:
Alexander: it's an octree where each face is a height field

Alexander: it was like 20 years ago g

AdrianM: Just not sure what the use case would be
AdrianM: I'm not aware of any nice way to build and maintain octree like structures on the GPU like we can on the CPU
Alexander: i really wanted to combine the efficiency of a height map (which is a true 2d representation of a 3d surface) and the capacity of representing multi-level surfaces like a usual SVO does
AdrianM: To be fair it's a pretty good approximation as long as you don't have any really complex shapes
AdrianM: Or want to look at it from any sharp angles
Alexander: i just subdivided the SVO until each leaf contains a single layer
Alexander: since the SVO can have uneven depth
Alexander: and as for sharp angles, they only make it less efficient, not less accurate
AdrianM: Hmmm, I guess that works. Interesting.
AdrianM: Oh also merging is still a problem. I know you mentioned that already, if you find some clever way to merge height values then let me know. Though honestly it's probably just going using min() and then averaging the radiance the normal way. Maybe we just cast two rays at slightly different angles and approximate the merge that way.
Alexander: i think the height field needs to be used to construct radiance intervals (by raymarching) and then radiance intervals need to be merged. you don't really merge height fields themselves
AdrianM: When you do the merge don't you want to know the distance for the ray they get merged into?
Alexander: you just need to know that one interval is farther away than the other one, and since the height maps only capture specific ranges, you always know in advance which one is in front of which
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: This is the case that I'm talking about
AdrianM: Whichever way you want to represent the height, you still run into a weird edge case if you have two different things you want to approximate. I don't think you can just take the closest one and overwrite the further one.
AdrianM: That's why I think realistically the solution might just be to cast two rays and treat the merge like vanilla RC for the radiance but take the minimum distance.
Deleted User: Very interesting way of going about more sparse rays. 
Can almost picture a  better 3D probe placement with that.

Is that what "holographic" RC is supposed to related to? 3D space?
AdrianM: This most recent conversation is based on taking the ideas of holographic rc and applying them back to the original rc algorithm. Holographic rc supports hard shadows, because rather than just taking the "average radiance" at each probe it tries to approximate the individual rays from the parent cascade. Unfortunately this requires many more rays than the original rc algorithm and doesn't scale well to 3D. That's the problem we were talking about above.
AdrianM: It's quite new and there aren't really any resources on it yet. But you can see the pinned messages for a rough idea of the structure.
AdrianM: There are two different versions of holographic rc, there is the original I created to prove the concept where the cascades are based on rings. Then there is the second version which Radiance Causality suggested based on lines.
Deleted User: Thanks for the explanation! Excited for RC exploration.
AdrianM: So I was thinking and I believe there is a faster way we can ray trace into a depth map.
AdrianM: Imagine we have a depth map like so
AdrianM: And we want to trace a bunch of (blue) rays into that depth map like so
AdrianM: Normally we'd have to step along each intersection, for steep angles that's a big problem as it requires a lot of sampling.
AdrianM: But what if we did it the other way - what if instead of tracing the blue rays into the red depth map we render the red rays onto the blue depth map.
RadianceCausality: So rasterization?
AdrianM: So every single red value becomes a single point which we can then render with point rendering
RadianceCausality: Wasn't there some game which did this back in like 2000s?
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: No idea, if there is I want to know more
RadianceCausality: Damnit I can't find anything
RadianceCausality: But it basically did this sorta stuff
RadianceCausality: With line drawing I think on the cpu
RadianceCausality: I think it might have been doing back to front instead tbh
AdrianM: Unfortunately this still doesn't solve the merging problem mentioned above. But it does give us a way to do quick reprojection of depth maps onto different cascades.
AdrianM: Also interestingly for a static scene you could in theory do this with all of the primary rays and use this as a general accelation scheme. Unrelated to rc but an interesting thought.

AdrianM: I was thinking a bit about how we could take the lessons learned here and apply it back to the original RC.
AdrianM: Right now we have something like this as our basic structure
AdrianM: If we just focus on a single probe it'd look like this
AdrianM: If we slightly shift our perspective we can make the model much more flexible though. Instead think of a probe like this:
AdrianM: If we show all the probes together we can see that these two views are pretty much equivalent and encapsulate the same rays.
AdrianM: What's interesting about it though is that this perspective allows us to decrease the probe density along the line, for example:
AdrianM: This means we can have the same scaling and interpolation as the original RC algorithm but without using rings at all.
AdrianM: This also means tricks like bilinear fix should also be applicable here.
AdrianM: Anyways, I don't have a use case for this so I haven't worked out all the details. But I'm leaving this here in case anyone else wants  to try a version of RC that doesn't require rings (looking at you Alex)
AdrianM: Oh also kind of interesting that the probe density doing it this way is completely arbitrary/continuous. Technically that's true of the original RC, but here it's even simpler.

Alexander: the way bilateral filtering explained in the context of image processing is next to useless in the context of realtime rendering, because in rendering we have much more information to use other than just pixel colors, and bilateral rendering can use much more efficient stopping criteria than stopping on color gradients. for example, you can stop on depth edges or on normal gradients
RadianceCausality: wdym normal gradients?
RadianceCausality: normal as in
RadianceCausality: that thing perpendincular to the tangent?

Alexander: you can also use hit distance information if you have in flatland/2d as a stopping criterion

Yaazarai: Okay so if I am understanding this correctly, each probe has 4 view frustums, up/down/left/right and we gather radiance only within and in the direction of each view.

The ray frequency doubles, which puts the end-point of each ray of cN half way between each ray endpoint of cN+1.
But wouldn't this mean each cascade is 2x as expensive if you aren't dropping probe density?

Alexander: some parts of HRC clearly show that some aspects of it scale better than RC vanilla and it doesn't have any of the ringing problems, but there's a lot of questions of how it should be formulated optimally and why
pulp_user: You mean the paper writing thread? I read through it but also can't say that helped much. ~~If a digestible explanation doesn't exist yet, could you maybe answer the following questions?
- Is holographic RC just about aligning probes to the end of the radiance intervals of other probes?~~
Alexander: recently i explained to Dolkar what we understand about HRC https://discord.com/channels/318590007881236480/1326079644969074718
Alexander: i mean this thread. this is where HRC started (almost) and this is where we pushed the idea from the initial AdrianM's idea of rings to holographic planes where it is now
Alexander: sorry i meant channel
pulp_user: That is EXACTLY what I was looking for
Alexander: >     Is holographic RC just about aligning probes to the end of the radiance intervals of other probes?
no, it's also about eliminating overlapping parts to make sure nothing is raymarched twice. however, this also naturally requires changing the structure of the grid, because a regular grid does not suit well for this
Alexander: it's called "holographic" because probes are placed on planes (instead of a regularly filled volume in 3d) and each plane captures the radiance function on it: for each point of the plane and each direction, it stores a radiance interval. similar way how real life holograms capture radiance field of a plane. but tbh it just sounded cool so we rolled with it, the analogy is pretty loose because there's no wave optics involved in HRC.
Alexander: HRC concerns itself mostly with the structure of one cascade. currently we use multiple HRC cascades same way how we use multiple RC cascades, but it's unclear how they should actually scale.
Alexander: actually this channel is not where HRC started. now that i'm looking at it, we started posting implementations here already
Alexander: The original HRC saga started here: https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1277053574425350224/1303508037138518037 . 
In that post AdrianM posted a same-memory comparison of encoding radiance of the same scene using RC and his earliest version of HRC. You can see that HRC is much more efficient and doesn't have ringing.

Then he was trying to explain me his idea of using a grid of rings of probes (rather than a grid of probes that RC uses), I was very skeptical for a long time, but then eventually started seeing why he thought it's a good idea. Then we went from talking about grids of rings to just parallel planes of probes that are now the standard way we think about HRC, but you can see in that discussion how we got there.

Yaazarai: So this is how intervals continue each other. Cut spatial resolution on the same axis as the current frustum direction. So if we're casting left/right, we reduce spatial resolution horizontally.

And then rays need to be balanced such that there's 1 ray for each probe along the plane at their endpoints. You could model it as `(2 ^ (cascade+1)) + 1` = `c0 = 3, c1 = 5, c2 = 9, c3 = 17...`.
Yaazarai: Then you have to cast your rays to the nearest cN+1 plane, which could be at the same position...

Yaazarai: (yellow c0, red/pink c1)
Yaazarai: Anytime a plane of cN lines up with a plane of cN+1 you either need to elongate the intervals to the next plane or teriminate.

Alexander: frustums are thick cones. you can also have thin cones and thick rays.
RadianceCausality: thick rays are when the size of both ends is the same I guess?
Alexander: it's basically rays that are integrated over probe position
Alexander: thin cones is rays integrated over probe angle
Alexander: and thick cones is integrated over both
Alexander: actually now that i'm thinking about it, we should do the same thing we did with parallax fix: we should assume some of the discrete parameters to be practically continuous. for example, figure out the maths for when probe density is so high that we can treat it as continuous
Alexander: or the other way round: what should the gathering process for cones look like if there are so many directions that they are almost continuous, but the number of probes is still discrete?
Alexander: one should result in gathering "thick rays", the other should result in gathering for "thin cones"
Alexander: and then potentially we should be able to combine both, but only once each proves individually that it can work
Alexander: so i'm planning to experiment with just one cascade, no extensions, just bruteforce marching. and set one of the parameters to practically infinity
Alexander: if i consider 1 cascade with infinitely many probes per line, infinitely many rays per cone, but 8 cones per probe and 10 lines, then this is the best gathering i could get so far:

Alexander: i'm not actually sure i know how to make it any better

Alexander: maybe HRC is meant to work only a limit case (for example, the number of direction needs to be high, so that rays can't diverge over the space between two subsequent lines)
Alexander: so maybe it's illegal to set up arbitrary limit cases like this and hope it still works. it clearly works in the limit, but maybe it can't work for these edge cases
fad: i realized this too. you end up with cones whether you like it or not
Alexander: you inevitably end up with cones when you merge down
Alexander: but you can represent a radiance field without merging
Alexander: you can also in theory calculate radiance from it using bruteforce interpolation for each c0 probe position for every cN direction. it's inefficient, but there's sufficient information in HRC to do that
Alexander: so i think the fundamental problem with HRC is in fact in merging cones down. that's where inaccuracies come from

Yaazarai: Store horizontal in texture memory first, then append vertical to the end.
Yaazarai: Ah so you're resolving all the way to fluence per direction?
fad: you never need both horizontal and vertical in memory at the same time
Yaazarai: I guess not...
fad: i calculate the first image of the light going up, then i add onto that the light going right to get the second image, then down then left
Yaazarai: Yeah I was hoping to do it in a single pass, but this works.
fad: you could do all directions at once as well. that's what alexander's shadertoy does
fad: these are all implementation details
fad: i'm rotating the texture each pass so to get coalesced memory accesses or whatever it's called
fad: cuz my drawing is stored in a buffer as well, row major when doing the up/down directions and then changes to column major when doing the left/right directions
Yaazarai: Right, okay well I'll figure something out. Either using packing or interleaving.
fad: are compute shaders not an option for you?
fad: imho it’s a lot more natural to use a storage buffer for this than a texture
Yaazarai: Oh true, yeah I can use compute.
fad: with storage buffers you can reason “ok for cascade 4 i need `32` directions per probe, `height` probes per column and `width/16` columns, so let me interpret the buffer as a `32`x`height`x`width/16` 3D array” instead of thinking about things like “to interleave or not to interleave this texture 🧐”
fad: and i reckon it’s pretty natural to map each workgroup to a column of probes in a direction, that way each thread in the workgroup is essentially doing the exact same task, just shifted one down the column
fad: harder to find niceties like that when trying to stuff it in a fragment shader
Skythedragon: What's the difference between normal and holographic rc, besides the different name?
fad: no overlap between intervals of the same cascade and direction, no (bilinear) interpolation of probes during merging which is the source of oh so many artefacts in regular RC, so perfect probe alignment
fad: if you already know how regular RC works then you can move around the blue point in this diagram to see how merging works and contrast it to your understanding of regular RC
fad: the magic that lets all this happen: HRC keeps spatial resolution of probes high perpendicular to interval direction
Skythedragon: why does the demo shader of it seem so slow compared to the normal one then?
Skythedragon: because it doesn't sound like there's that much of a performance difference?
Skythedragon: I should probably implement my own radiance cascades at some point
ion: It was my understanding that all a lens does is to shift phases. So maybe you just need to apply a radius-dependend phase shift to the hologram to make it focus the light? Alternatively multiplying the hologram with the pattern of a "zone plate" or "photon sieve" might help? Nikon makes lenses which combine these ideas in what they call a "phase fresnel" lens. Canon has a similar technology which their marketing calls "diffractive optics". These are used for photography and produce good image quality.

fad: something i realized:
if your interval marching routine is O(n) where n is the length of the interval, then the last cascade in HRC takes O(N^3) to compute where N is the width of your scene. this is the exact same time complexity as line sweeping N directions 🤔
fad: so, you have to make your interval marching routine better than O(n), e.g., via extensions, otherwise HRC isn’t that good
RadianceCausality: Yeah 
I pointed that out for RC as well
fad: in standard RC (where number of intervals halves each cascade), the total length of all your intervals per cascade is constant so it’s faster than line sweeping
RadianceCausality: Oh
Right
I meant for 4x
Yaazarai: Can't you also line-sweep HRC though?
fad: there’s no point. intervals don’t overlap in HRC
Yaazarai: Per probe they do.
Yaazarai: Oh wait only for c0.
fad: across all the intervals of a given length and direction, none of them overlap
Yaazarai: Except in c0...?
Yaazarai: There's no way they don't.
fad: they don’t overlap in c0
Yaazarai: Yeah I don't see that.
fad: in c0, all the intervals are 1 px long and spaced 1 px apart, so they can’t overlap
fad: if they were 2 px long and spaced 1 px apart then there’d be 50% overlap, like in regular RC
Yaazarai: A 1px interval is literally just the same pixel the probe is on?
Yaazarai: I feel like you'd need a bit more than that if you have multiple rays per probe.
RadianceCausality: 2 pixels
RadianceCausality: Or 3
fad: look at your own diagram, if you repeated the yellow c0 probes then there’d be no overlap
fad: you can also see this visualisation which shows exactly what i am doing in my HRC implementation, the intervals of a given length and direction don’t overlap
Alexander: yeah i was very loosy-goosy when saying that. the idea is that in order to define a 2d scene that consists of opaque objects, you need to define the color on their outlines. outline of 2d objects has 1d dimensionality. full radiance field can be calculated from the outline of 2d objects, so in that sense you can define the radiance field of a full-on 2d scene by encoding just a 1d (colored) outline of objects that it's made of.

those dimensionality discussions are really shortcuts that only make sense in a context when you're trying to compare two things to each other: for example, vanilla RC vs HRC or encoding object surface vs its radiance field. so by considering different parameters to be fixed, you can get different dimensionality scaling, but if you fix the same parameters between two different representations, you can compare their scaling. i'm just not being flippant with which parameters i'm considering fixed
Yaazarai: Yeah I guess I was thinking of it wrong. So this is kinda weird, c0 starts essentially at 1/2 resolution axis to begin with.
fad: yes my c0 intervals are 1 px long and touch a single pixel during marching
fad: i don’t get what you mean, i have 1 c0 probe per output screen pixel
Yaazarai: That only works if you have 1 ray per c0 probe to begin with.
Yaazarai: I guess it looks so good you don't need more anyways.
fad: ~~another benefit of merging with 2 instead of 3: you can do pre-averaging with 2 which you can’t do with 3~~ nevermind you can't do preaveraging easily with either
fad: i have 2 rays per c0 probe in a given direction. 8 rays total across all directions
Yaazarai: Okay but if you have 1 probe per pixel c0 and 2 rays per probe, then those 2 rays are basically doing the same thing.
fad: with treating all pixels as squares and doing DDA yes. but if you’re doing bilinear interpolation volumetrics then they will be slightly different
fad: but even then you probably wouldn’t notice if you cheated and made them 1 ray since they’re only 1 px long lol
Yaazarai: Right, that's what I figured.
fad: probe spacing and interval length are highly coupled in HRC. in regular RC they were two independent parameters you had to worry about and tweak, but in HRC you have much less parameters overall
Yaazarai: Yeah I've noticed, which is fine. Less parameters is nice.
pulp_user: From the way you said it originally, I expected one consequence to be that HRC scales better than RC, because if it encodes the information more efficiently, the calculations for GI will probably also become faster. Is this correct? Or do we not yet know what parameters to fix, so we can't make the comparison properly?
fad: one parameter you could increase is number of directions to probe spacing ratio. right now it’s 2:1 for me (cascade N has columns of probes spaced 2^N px apart and has 2*2^N directions (when tracing in one direction, e.g., right)) but you could maybe increase that. it’d make 1px light sources look better
RadianceCausality: HRC is approximately twice as slow compared to 4x branching for the same number of base probes and base directions

Its significantly higher quality though
RadianceCausality: You can't really make normal RC get the same quality
pulp_user: That's still faster than RC with bilinear fix, right? That's a 4x hit, I think?
RadianceCausality: But same thing, bilinear fix and half probe resolution is better than normal RC
Alexander: HRC definitely scales better than vanilla RC, because you can think of HRC grid as RC grid that stores only log N probes on one space dimension instead of N
Alexander: so you can remove most probes from RC grid to get an HRC grid
Alexander: the reason why HRC is slower in practice is because we re-distribute the same number of probes to be much more dense
Alexander: sorta
Alexander: but you can also imagine an hrc implementation that's literally just this:
Alexander: this will be much faster with about the same quality as vanilla RC
Randy: Alex drawings never fail to entertain
pulp_user: But in RC I also go log(n) in both dimensions in higher Cascades, no? Like, I start with 512x512 probes in c0, then go 256x256 in C1. That is log on both x and y. While from what I understand, in HRC I go 512x512, and then 256x512 and so on? Im missing something.
Alexander: actually imo it's a better 1:1 comparison: you achieve the same accuracy as you have in vanilla RC but you drop most of its probes
pulp_user: Oh wait but I don't increase directions in hrc
fad: will it really, i’d expect it to be worse quality, you have strictly less information, and you don’t get the regular HRC benefit of high spatial resolution perpendicular to interval direction
fad: HRC direction count doubles each cascade
Alexander: yes, because the way we implement HRC is we keep the total number of probes constant (same way how we do with RC)
RadianceCausality: I don't think so
Normal RC your rays have longer distance so there's less times where the interpolation makes a mess of things
Alexander: from my experiments yes, it's basically the same, jsut without ringing. we store "strictly less" information, but we just drop information that's duplicated anyway
Alexander: you can also store multiples of inter-probe intervals in HRC too: for example, each HRC probe can store the 3x distance between probes, doesn't have to be 1x
pulp_user: Oh right I confused this with Ray reconstruction stuff again
Alexander: so fundamentally there's no difference
fad: so what, if i did 4x RC with this, i would get similar quality to regular 4x but at the cost of 2x?
Alexander: it's not 2x
Alexander: it's 2x per cascade
fad: i meant if you did regular 4x RC but crossed out the probes like you did here, that would become the cost of 2x RC again
Alexander: yeah should be
Alexander: minus the longer marching cost
Alexander: it's just imo there's something that's optimized whenever cascades are the same size in memory
fad: oh true
Alexander: that's why hrc seems to perform the best (in terms of quality for a given memory budget) when we increase the number of probes on each line to compensate for saved space
Alexander: but i'm not sure about the way this optimization actually works or why

RadianceCausality: Hm
Yaazarai: Does vanilla or even bilinear-fix RC technically violate the penumbra condition due to the bilinear interpolation?
Yaazarai: Since the bilinear interpolation causes sampling to happen outside cones.
Alexander: penumbra condition defines the way things should scale asymptotically. it does not care about small effects like interpolation accuracy. it's also why something can satisfy the penumbra condition and still produce ringing
Alexander: it's basically saying "if you want to build an airplane, it should have wings that are of similar size to its body"
Alexander: it's very broad and is in some sense weak/insufficient/not specific enough

KornFlaks: Anyone got any idea how to calculate an off-center orthographic view-projection matrix that can produce the correct probe world space positions given reduction in probe count every cascade?
Yaazarai: Do you really need anything that complicated? Just treat it like regular RC. Multiply either the X or Y coordinate by the spacing for that cascade and that view frustum direction.
Calculating ray end-points isn't hard either, it's just rise-over-run in whatever direction. So if you double ray-counts, you calculate the end-point offset as rise-over-run, then iterate down adjacent to the frustum direction for each ray.
KornFlaks: Hrm, true. Never thought about separating the view from the vp matrix. Since I already have pixels in a grid, the projection matrix isn't needed

KornFlaks: With regular RC. But it should be possible with HRC as well
Yaazarai: What's the purpose of depth testing here....?
Yaazarai: IIRC depth testing is for rejecting overlapping fragments during rasterization?
KornFlaks: Rasterize solids onto each cascade since it's guaranteed that the pixels/probes in locations occupied by a solid will always resolve to that solid. Allowing pixels to be skipped during cascade rendering.
KornFlaks: It's a object mask, using the depth testing functionality to improve performance in dense scenes. Might even make the performance curve a bell shape, where sparse and dense scenes have very good performance, and partially occupied scenes have the longest render time
KornFlaks: I haven't actually seen how well it works since I'm setting up functionality and debugging infrastructure for HRC so maybe I'm just completely wrong. Wouldn't be the first time with my optimization ideas.

AdrianM: What do you mean by Direction/Position-Major?
Yaazarai: Direction-Major groups rays primarily by their ray direction. So all rays pointing in the same direction are grouped together.

Position-Major groups rays primarily by their probe position. So all rays that share a probe are grouped together.
AdrianM: Does this have to do with compute shaders?
Yaazarai: No just different texture layouts.
AdrianM: I don't think that makes any functional difference, does it?
Yaazarai: Hardly. You get hardware interpolation with Direction-Major. Which isn't that much. Imo its just better for teaching/debugging since it isolates individual ray directions as entire scenes.
Yaazarai: @AdrianM like this for example. You can see the entire scene but only in the desired direction as a sort of mip within the cascade.
AdrianM: Honestly I just treat the texture as a 1D array and then use functions to handle all the indexing logic for me so I don't have to think about it.
Alexander: i think it's healthy to think about memory that way first and then its packing second
Yaazarai: Its not about packing. Its about visual inspection.
Alexander: yeah in the general case you can't get much out of visual inspection. like, you can't really tell much from SH coefficient or from looking at SVO data
Yaazarai: I mean with shaders we don't really have much other than visual inspection for debugging. At least I don't in GameMaker.
Alexander: well.. shadertoy doesn't even have enough textures for RC, so we use the :Cubemap_A:
Alexander: which is translated into a 1d array not because it's convenient, but because it's the largest storage we have and 1d packing is the most general way to pack something into memory. like, it always works.
Yaazarai: True. I also switched to using more of a 1D array memory approach even with Direction-Major. Really helped with writing a modular RC implementation (swappable branch factors).
AdrianM: If you treat the data as a 1D array then you can render out that array in any way that you want for debugging purposes. I just have a separate shader called "renderer" that I tweak to see whatever I want to see
Yaazarai: That's so extra.
AdrianM: Not really, just a few extra lines
Yaazarai: Just skip the translation layer.
AdrianM: The power of programming is abstraction, I can isolate and solve parts of the problem so I don't need to think about them
Yaazarai: Same with HRC. Even with HRC I can just render each Frustum to its own individual texture and inspect them individually to check for errors.
Yaazarai: Abstraction has a balance to it though. For example the shadertoy, while very helpful, its a hellscape to read because its too abstract--rightly so for its purpose though.
Yaazarai: It also lacks naming conventions, comments....
AdrianM: If abstraction is handled properly then you don't incur any penalty from it (aside from perhaps a bit of performance loss from the overhead itself)
AdrianM: But a lot of abstractions are poorly thought out or overspecialized which causes problems
Yaazarai: IMO when you can just as easily write a Vanilla RC implementation cleanly in < 100 not so dense lines of GLSL, quadrupling that with abstractions is a bad idea. I'm talking from a teaching perspective more so than an individual implementation.
AdrianM: That's fair, it depends on what you want to accomplish
Yaazarai: Yeah true.
Yaazarai: Although in the case of HRC the more I mess with it the more I want to store and handle frustums individually--not for teaching, just for myself for sanity.
Yaazarai: The whole halving spatial resolution per axis thing makes for having per-frustum textures very easy to implement.
AdrianM: I'm trying to determine if HRC can be used for solving physics, and if so then where the limits are for that.
Yaazarai: Are you comparing to a reference path tracer or anything?
AdrianM: There's a connection between ray tracing and other kinds of physics calculations, the best exploration I've seen of this is through Walk on Spheres algorithm
AdrianM: Basically instead of tracing rays as straight lines you trace random walks (brownian motion)
AdrianM: Normally that's too expensive to do on a large scale without an acceleration structure (which won't work for dense/complex/volumetric data) but I think that the ray extensions we can do for HRC should be possible to do with random walks too
AdrianM: But I'm still working through the details in my head, there's still a lot of unanswered questions
AdrianM: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bZbuKOxH71o
AdrianM: This is a pretty good overview of what currently exists
AdrianM: I don't understand all the math, but the concepts are pretty simple
AdrianM: Unfortunately I don't think we can get a perfect solution, there will still have to be an iterative (or monte-carlo) solver. But I think it should be possible to use HRC to massively accelerate it.
AdrianM: Actually in general I'm wondering if HRC could act as an alternative to multigrid methods...

RadianceCausality: ...can you draw a diagram
Yaazarai: I did, with 2x though. With preaveraging regardless of probe position ray groups between probes never overlap, which is why preaveraging works, the perspective shift is constant with the scaling/branching factor.

RadianceCausality: I don't understand the diagram at all
Yaazarai: C0 is showing plane 0 sharing 4 probes.
C1 is showing planes 0 and 1 sharing 4 probes.
C2 is showing planes 0, 1, 2 and 3 sharing 4 probes.

The idea is you 1/2 probes along the y-axis (along the plane) and split the y-plane across the x-axis.
Then shift each probe down along the plane by cascade: `probe_yoffset = plane_index % cascade_index`.
The higher the cascade the more discretized the plane.
Yaazarai: It may require changing the raycasting strategy too, idk.
RadianceCausality: hm
RadianceCausality: I don't think that'd work with angles?
Yaazarai: Doesn't HRC still work when ray-angles get distorted from when planes between two cascades overlap?
E.g. if a plane of c0 overlaps a plane of c1, you cast rays in c0 twice as long to the next plane over (to reduce artifacts IIRC).

I was also considering connecting rays directly to their adjacent probes (like in the image), rather than connecting rays to probes along the plane.

Yaazarai: RC diagrams are the worst. Here's HRC with 1/2x probe scaling per plane per cascade. The general idea is that to reduce spatial scaling along the plane ray-endpoints to be spaced to end on each probe in CN+1. This is only possible for 1/2 of the probes of CN, since that's the number of probes in CN+1. For the other half of the probes of CN they need 2x intervals to march to the next plane to not over-extend the viewing frustum.

This is basically what normal HRC does, except without the drop in planar-spatial resolution every ray has a 1:1 probe end-point instead of 2:1.

```
* Probes scale 2x.
* Rays scale 2x.
* Intervals scale `(sqr(ray_count) / 2) - 1` or `sqr(ray_count) / 2`.
* Number of planes per cascade = interval length.
* Every 2nd probe has 2x length intervals and squished frustum.
* Distance between ray-endpoints scale as `ray_count - 1`.
* Extensions are basically impossible.
```

The number of planes and interval lengths scale at the same rate of `pow2-1`. To fix that to match 2x scaling you can just extend interval lengths `+1` on the *axis adjacent to the plane* (e.g. in the direction of the view frustum). I didn't do this in the images for diagramming purposes.

Despite the fact that ray-end-points grow further apart, the overall angle between is still reduced as you increase angular resolution with each cascade.
jason: Seems pin worthy
Yaazarai: Seems like pain.
Yaazarai: My brain hurts, I'm an idiot and diagramed like 50 different layouts that didn't work.
Yaazarai: You can probably also 1/2 probes starting at c1, but you'd need twice as many rays at c0 and they wouldn't be evenly distributed.
Yaazarai: Another option here to fix the squished view-frustums is to double the distance between end-points for every 2nd probe, which is probably much better and makes more sense--rise-over-run, double the run, double the rise.
Yaazarai: [image]
Yaazarai: Dark red being original, white being doubled distance between end-points.
fad: i think the diagrams would be simpler/easier to read if you only showed one C0 probe/pixel instead of multiple stacked on top of each other

fad: well i guess it depends what you're trying to visualize. maybe you're visualizing a different property than what i was focussing on

RadianceCausality: like what
I'm considering taking a shot at it a bit
Alexander: i think i know how to find the exact induced surface charges on an occluder
RadianceCausality: ooo
Alexander: imagine that AB is an occluder. the question is: what surface charges does it need to induce in order for them to precisely block the field of that source (to create a shadow)?
Alexander: so i think the answer can be aquired with FFT (naturally): first, use the same thing that I used for propagating wave fronts. so, break down the field crossing AB into its constituent planar waves. each planar wave can be easily "cancelled" by arranging point sources that create the same planar wave with a negative sign.
Alexander: then just simply add all those planar induced sources to negate the entire field
Alexander: basically, first solve the problem for a planar wave (that's relatively easy). then represent the target field as a superposition of planar waves.
Alexander: and since wave dynamics is always linear..
RadianceCausality: wait wdym by breaking it into planar waves
RadianceCausality: you mean like making a bunch of vertical and horizontal ones?
Alexander: i mean that's what fft does: represents an arbitrary field as a sum of planar waves exp(ikx)
Alexander: where k is a wave vector
Alexander: then, solve the problem for each k
RadianceCausality: also wave is a terrible choice since everyone uses it metaphorically
RadianceCausality: and diffraction didn't pick up anybody
Alexander: hmmm...
Alexander: worth adding keywords like "demo" to the search list
Alexander: i can't be sure, but it's easy to try at least a dozen rare words that seem related
RadianceCausality: what about electromagnetism

Alexander: yeah i think they also mentioned maxwell
