RadianceCausality: there's a lot of talk on holograms actually but its about black holes it seems
Yaazarai: @fad here ya go, as requested.

Consider that you have some finite viewing frustum (discrete interval). If you double the length of the view frustum and simultaneously double the distance between CN+1 planes/probes, the number of CN+1 probes within the viewing frustum of CN will also double (image 1 & 2).

In normal HRC, your planes have 1:1 planar spatial scaling between all cascades. Which causes all of your ray-endpoints to have the same spatial scaling as well (image 3).

If you 1/2 spatial scaling, then the number of probes within the view frustum no-longer double for every 2nd probe due to spatial mismatch (image 4). To fix that you just double length of the view frustum (like image 1 & 2) to the next-closest adjacent CN+1 plane (image 5). Other than that, you merge as normal like you would with standard HRC.
Yaazarai: Hopefully that clears things up.
fad: interesting. thanks for explanation
Yaazarai: There is a slight problem though... If a plane of CN lies between two planes of CN+1, every 2nd probe has 3x as long intervals.
RadianceCausality: there's another slight problem
I'm pretty sure you aren't going to get hard shadows with your approach
Yaazarai: ?
RadianceCausality: because the spatial resolution of your probes decreases in both directions?
Yaazarai: That shouldn't matter because the view frustum is respected?
RadianceCausality: ???
Yaazarai: What I mean is, with normal RC the lack of hard shadows happens because you sample directions outside the view of the current probe, e.g. each bilinear probe.
RadianceCausality: uhhh no?
Yaazarai: Isn't that the case?
RadianceCausality: no its entirely a spatial issue
RadianceCausality: the problem is that you can generate a penumbra with width 1 arbitrarily far from a light source
RadianceCausality: and then in order to resolve that you need the probes to have maximum spacing 1 there

RadianceCausality: I just multiply all of the rays by their angle when generating
RadianceCausality: and then you can add two "angular fluences" that represent non-intersecting angles to make an "angular fluence" with a larger angle
RadianceCausality: and then you don't have to divide at all
Alexander: multiply each color by its angle, add them all together. then add all angles. then divide the sum color by sum angle
Alexander: it's just a weighted average where weights are angles

JoshJers: A while back I had asked about why I was getting correct weighting results with cos^2 and people replied with "there's no cosine term" which is correct 😄
...but it turns out I stumbled backwards into a weirdly good approximation of the weighting
RadianceCausality: ?????????
JoshJers: instead of calculating the angle between the two rays, I was instead weighting using the inverse of the squared length of the ray whose "s" coordinate lies directly between the two
JoshJers: and the answers are *surprisingly* close and I have no freaking clue why
JoshJers: but doing `1/dot(midpoint, midpoint)` is a lot cheaper than `atan(cross / dot)` and it's accurate within 0.002 at the smallest cascade (and gets more accurate as there are more rays at the higher cascades)
RadianceCausality: Huh
JoshJers: (you don't have to use the C that's in the thing since it's a constant factor, I just put it there as a point of interest to get them to line up very well)
RadianceCausality: Yeah guess that makes sense
RadianceCausality: Since it's basically just tracking the derivative I think?
JoshJers: ohhhhh yeah okay
JoshJers: the derivative of atan is how you get there, yep
RadianceCausality: In my case I'm entirely memory bound or smth, because replacing atan with 1 doesn't improve my perf at all
RadianceCausality: Also note that you could just precompute it
JoshJers: I'd been using tables but also none of my merging works yet due to I'm apparently an idiot so none of that matters 😄
JoshJers: I did realize a thing I am doing that's not standard though, which is I was effectively just casting those midpoint rays rather than casting the ones around them like you're (I believe) doing
RadianceCausality: Midpoint rays are slightly easier
RadianceCausality: I don't think it really matters though
JoshJers: probably not, I just thought it was interesting
Yaazarai: Wtf are midpoint rays? I thought HRC was continuous between planes/cascades?
JoshJers: they are, I was doing it wrong and stumbled into this 😄
RadianceCausality: Yeah but you can do budget cuts
JoshJers: basically, as RadianceCascade said, my `1/dot(midpoint, midpoint)` approximation works because the derivative of `atan(x/y)` is `y / (x^2 + y^2)`
RadianceCausality: Basically difference between normal RC and forking
JoshJers: and you can think of the angle between the two vectors (used for weighting) as the delta of the angle of the full angle from "forward"
Yaazarai: Forking in vanilla is to fix discontinuities between rays which doesn't happen in HRC though.
Yaazarai: Unless you're interpolating between two probes?
RadianceCausality: I mean it's the same thing
You have an approximately 1 pixel jump between rays if you trace one ray to the center of the two probes you're merging from
Yaazarai: I'm guessing the benefit to doing that instead of just merging with one probe is less aliasing?
Alexander: yeah i expected it to be somethign like that
Alexander: having cos^2() sounds a bit unlikely, but since cos(x)~1-x^2/2, then it can easily match a short taylor expansion of the actual solution
fad: RadianceCascade himself
JoshJers: lol nailed it
JoshJers: cos^2 works because it boils down to (using d == "distance along the cascade's primary  axis" and s as the perpendicular ray direction)  `d^2 / (d^2 + s^2)` - and the d^2 can factor out since it's constant for the whole cascade

and because it's chosen at the midpoint (well, the x is the midpoint anyway, but it's close enough) of the two rays in the wedge, it ends up being close to the derivative of atan (which is `d / (d^2 + s^2)` but again the numerator can factor out)

Think of the wedge instead as basically rays from 0,0 to (sMidpoint - Δ, d) and (sMidpoint + Δ, d)  (it's not exact but it's close), well the angle between the two can also be represented as the difference in the angles from each to the "forward" direction - and the smaller that delta goes, the closer the derivative of the arctangent is to it.

And since the angle deltas tend to be rather small (especially at higher cascades) it ends up being quite a good approximation

Yaazarai: @RadianceCausality for planes of cN that align with cN+1 we can merge immediately... But if you do that don't you technically need an extra cascade to cover the missing distance?
RadianceCausality: Why extra cascade?
Yaazarai: For example log2 of 1024 is 10 cascades or intervals summed from 2^0 through 2^9 for a max merged interval length of 1024 across.

Which is fine if you extend intervals, but if you shorten intervals by immediately merging instead of cast->merge your intervals don't extend as far.
RadianceCausality: meh
RadianceCausality: well the thing i s that the only way you hit immediate merging on the 512 length cascade is if your probe is all the way at the leftmost pixel
RadianceCausality: so I just cut that off and run at 1023 pixels or something
Yaazarai: Well no, it happens at every cascade level.
RadianceCausality: yeah but those cascades already have to exist
Yaazarai: And what if it happens on the lower cascades? E.g. 64, 128, 256? Now your interval extends max 1024-N.
RadianceCausality: that isn't an issue
RadianceCausality: you'll end up extending to the rightmost part of the screen anyways in the last cascade
Yaazarai: Can you please elaborate?

If the sum of the intervals is 1024 or sum 2^0...9 merging early without casting means that you skip any or multiple of those interval ranges and can never extend to the rightmost of the screen.
Yaazarai: I guess the easy solution too is extending rays to 2x length instead of immediately merging...
RadianceCausality: if you're at pos 64, you only end up skipping the first 6 cascades or so
RadianceCausality: so the remaining cascades from 64..512 + the starting position 64 still add up to 1024
RadianceCausality: the only problem is if you're starting at 0, and then you skip all the cascades
but who the fuck cares
RadianceCausality: anyways it just isn't a problem

Yaazarai: Without merging inspecting individual cascades won't yield whatever Adrian did there.
Alexander: you do not. just cast infinite rays
Yaazarai: Ahhh.
Alexander: that's because you're not inspecting your cascades correctly
Alexander: you can reconstruct radiance field from any given cascade without merging
Yaazarai: How does that work?
Yaazarai: I don't think I've seen anyone mention that before.
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: this is how you can reconstruct the red ray from two green probes on a line
Alexander: because radiance is constant along straight paths, the radiance at the red ray origin point is equal to radiance at the point where that ray intersects the probe line
Alexander: in reality the distance between two green probes is much smaller than the distance between lines, so you're interpolating just a tiny amount
Alexander: so you can reconstruct any ray by doing just a short distance interpolation between green probes (as opposed to vanilla RC where for high cascades distance between probes increases and you need to do longer-range interpolation)

Yaazarai: @RadianceCausality  My understanding is that proposed HRC casts 1 ray per pixel across the frustum. For any given interval length you need 2x+1 rays to fill the width of the frustum--where x is the interval length. So e.g. if the interval length is 8 you need 17 rays to fill the frustum.

If you don't do that you get image 2, 1/2 as many rays, 1 ray every 2px and none of the rays point in the axial direction.
Yaazarai: Only using pow2 rays (right image) 1/2 of all the pixels across the frustum are hit, so rays need to be spaced 1px apart or you only cover 1/2 the frustum.
Yaazarai: Ah I guess you can cast 2*x rays, drop 1 ray since the frustums technically overlap.

Yaazarai: @JoshJers how are you summing your frustums?

JoshJers: ```cpp
    // Average the four quadrants for the output
    result = (tex.Load(int3(pos, 0))
        + tex.Load(int3(pos + uint2(quadrantDim.x, 0), 0))
        + tex.Load(int3(pos + uint2(0, quadrantDim.y), 0))
        + tex.Load(int3(pos + quadrantDim, 0)))
      * 0.25;```
Yaazarai: Interesting... I figured it was just a straight up sum since the frustums shouldn't overlap?
Yaazarai: If I do that I get this.
JoshJers: each quadrant, at any given texel, represents 1/4 of the total radiance reaching that point
JoshJers: since the weighting I'm doing is based on the number of rays in the quadrant (not the number of overall rays in a full 360) then it's an average to combine them all (making each worth 1/4 of the final result)

Yaazarai: Okay so I fixed this irritating offset.... Yo ucan see everything is shifted over. When I figure my merge probe position I was doing:
```
merge_probe += (diagonal * interval);
```
Where `diagonal` is the diagonal offset of the frustum. But technically all of my rays are 1px short (end 1px over from the closest cN+1 plane). So I have to extend my rays 1px to end at the next plane, which this is doing incorrectly.

Should have been: (where frustum is a vec2 pointing in the frustum direction)
```
merge_probe += (diagonal * (interval - 1.0));
merge_probe += frustum;
```
Yaazarai: [image]
JoshJers: oh yeah that first one is just ... slightly "rotated"
Yaazarai: Yup.
Yaazarai: Hurray off-by-one errors.
Yaazarai: Also when averaging my frustums, offsetting by 1px does not fix the overlap.
JoshJers: hm
Yaazarai: Wait I need to weight my rays first before doing that.
Yaazarai: @JoshJers how'd you do the weighting?
Yaazarai: Cuz right now all my rays have equal contribution regardless of angle.
JoshJers: ```cpp
float CalculateWedgeWeight(float target0, float target1, float distance)
{
    float3 vecA = float3(target0, distance, 0);
    float3 vecB = float3(target1, distance, 0);

    return atan(cross(vecB, vecA).z / dot(vecA, vecB));
}```
JoshJers: so the weighting is based on angular contribution
JoshJers: (target0 is the first cross-axis vector value, target1 is the second, distance is the cascade distance)
JoshJers: and then I just make the targets 0.5 offset in either direction at that distance
Yaazarai: wdymn cross-axis vector?
JoshJers: in left to right, it's y
JoshJers: in top -> bottom, it's x
JoshJers: the one perpendicular to the overall ray direction
JoshJers: so like for cascade 1 (in one of my implementations), I have four rays with targets -1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5
Yaazarai: Okay.
JoshJers: and then per ray I actually just do +/- 0.5 for each
JoshJers: so like `weight = CalculateWedgeWeight(rayTarget - 0.5, rayTarget + 0.5, cascadeDistance)`
Yaazarai: Do you just multiply the ray's radiance by that weight?
JoshJers: yeah and then divide by the sum of the weights when writing out
JoshJers: (you don't strictly have to do that if you weight everything all the way through correctly, I believe, but I didn't)
JoshJers: so the *first* cascade doesn't apply any weights (since I'm 1 ray per output at the moment) it writes the ray values directly
JoshJers: when *merging* it applies the weights for the loaded values that get merged
JoshJers: (The reason it's written with two targets is because in a more RadianceCausality-like implementation, there are N + 1 rays written as N wedges, and the two sides are what make the wedge angle)
JoshJers: For a while I had an approximation that worked really well, but it falls apart at higher resolutions (like 1080p lol)
Yaazarai: Yeah I would figure that you only have to weight the radiance sampled during raymarching, store that then everything should be weighted when merging.
JoshJers: yeah - the reason I didn't go that route is twofold: one, because my brain wasn't working when I was doing that part, but two, because each ray from every cascade but the last to run has its rays used in two different ways, with *technically* two different weights
JoshJers: so I weighted them post instead of pre to allow for a little bit of correction to the overall weighting
Yaazarai: @JoshJers could you explain how this works a bit? I don't actually have `atan(t)` available in GLSL1.0, I do have `atan(y, x)` though.
JoshJers: the overall gist is: you have two vectors and you're getting the angle between them
Yaazarai: Yup makes sense.
Yaazarai: The vecA/B thing makes sense.
JoshJers: `atan(x / y)` can be rewritten as `atan2(y, x)`
Yaazarai: Ah thats your `cross / dot`.
Yaazarai: Okay thats what I was missing.
JoshJers: yeah cross is sin and dot is cos
JoshJers: (but you knew that I'm sure 😄 )
Yaazarai: I need to stop writing GameMaker tutorials and get out of this GLSL1.0 hell.
Yaazarai: Looks at this crap.
Yaazarai: Very descriptive, ty.
JoshJers: I'm definitely team "about the most important part of a compiler is its ability to show good error messages" and most fail at that 😄
Yaazarai: Indeed they do.
Yaazarai: So modified:
```
float CalculateWedgeWeight(float target0, float target1, float dist)
{
    vec3 vecA = vec3(target0, dist, 0.0);
    vec3 vecB = vec3(target1, dist, 0.0);
    return atan(dot(vecA, vecB), cross(vecB, vecA).z);
}
```
Yaazarai: So output comparison, origin (left), weighted (right).
Yaazarai: Wait.
JoshJers: oh wait it's (y, x) I actually think those are backwards and I said it wrong
Yaazarai: Backjwards
JoshJers: I do that every time
JoshJers: that's a nice looking test scene 😮
Yaazarai: Okay so then weighting is literally just `radiance *= weight`?
Yaazarai: (before merging)
Yaazarai: Everything goes black lol.
JoshJers: what are you passing as the target values?
Yaazarai: ```
vec2 ortho_probe = infos.ortho.yx * infos.probe;
vec2 ortho_merge = infos.ortho.yx * infos.merge;
float target0 = max(ortho_probe.x, ortho_probe.y);
float target1 = max(ortho_merge.x, ortho_merge.y);
float weight = CalculateWedgeWeight(target0, target1, infos.intrv);
gl_FragColor *= weight;
```
`ortho` is horiz/vert `abs(frustum)` direction as a vec2, then flip that perpendicular direction `yx`, then take the max `x,y` of that for the target, because one will always be zero.
JoshJers: and this is just as the rays themselves are cast, yeah?
JoshJers: what's "probe" vs. "merge"?
Yaazarai: The probe you cast from, the probe you merge with and cast to.
Yaazarai: Their XY positions that is.
JoshJers: their positions, or directions?
JoshJers: I probably shouldn't have pasted my stupid function with its weird parameters lol, I think I made it more weird than it needed to be
Yaazarai: Oh positions, I can convert it to angles.
Yaazarai: I thought it took those perpendicular positions, found the direction, blah blah blah.
JoshJers: so your two rays are `vec2(target0, infos.intrv)` and `vec2(target1, infos.intrv)`
Yaazarai: OHH.
Yaazarai: Well yes.
JoshJers: and for a given single ray, I just offset a bit in either direction from the actual ray direction (where "a bit" is "to the edge of the 'wedge' that this ray is representing")
JoshJers: which in my world since the rays hit every pixel along the perpendicular axis, is +/- 0.5
Yaazarai: Are you centering the ray in the wedge before weighting or something?
JoshJers: for this setup the rays are the centers of the wedges, vs. the canonical one where there's a ray per edge
JoshJers: in that world, the two edge ray directions would be your two targets
Yaazarai: Hmmm no matter what I do I can't make it work...
JoshJers: yep, nailed it, A+ definitely right
Yaazarai: My lazy approximation isn't half-bad though.
```
float theta = 0.5 * abs((infos.index - (infos.intrv * 0.5)) / (infos.intrv * 0.5));
float weight = 1.0 - theta;
gl_FragColor *= (raylen >= 1.0)? weight : 1.0;
```

JoshJers: What I mean is, it's a sampling pattern issue, which I think is causing a lot of the streaking in my HRC implementation (and probably also others'). Basically for the connection case where you're, in one way or another, grabbing different rays/wedges from different (neighboring) probes, you can end up in a situation like in this zoomed-in diagram from a cascade, where you sample a too-dark texel from y + 0 (blue outline) then jump to, say, y - 1 and sample another too-dark texel from the next ray/wedge direction over (green outline), expertly dodging all of the actual radiance it's trying to sample.

Yaazarai: After looking at it a bit moving the light around a couple things happen:

1. The brightening effect happens on either a few rays within the frustum at any cascade 
2. Happens at all rays within a frustum at any cascade.

I believe whats happening is that the shifting viewpoint along the plane causes some groups of rays to hit exactly the same point and others to miss at exactly the same point. But since we average rays across the plane some merged rays have twice the contribution than others. Causing the brightening effect.
Yaazarai: My immediate idea then is to radially distribute start points by passing through the probe to avoid axis aligned contribution--rather than starting on the probe itself.

Sorry shit drawing, but typically we "weight," ray contributions by angle to solve the over contribution issue because all the rays are the same length.
Yaazarai: So here would be a probe casting its rays, but offset back to pass through the plane rather than starting on the plane.
Yaazarai: And the offset increasing with angle for radial distribution.
Yaazarai: So its like weighting the rays by angle, but offsetting start/end points as well.

JoshJers: oh I see, so rays that hit nothing would just be one ray along that direction, and rays that hit something would be broken into the intersection points, effectively
Alexander: and then to preserve memory scaling, each cascade is only allowed to store no more than N intersection points per line, maybe

Yaazarai: Don't you need the planes perpendicular to the ray direction...?
Alexander: they are
Alexander: the most important part about radiance is that it's constant along straight paths in the empty space
Alexander: so it only makes sense to store points where it changes from one value to another along a straight path
Alexander: which is interesection points
Yaazarai: Hence RLE....
Alexander: anything more than that is overspecification

Yaazarai: With line sweeping and general slope formula you can skew and modulo positions to fill the grid in its entirety. Which is why keeping the frustums I think will help.

Yaazarai: Courtesy of @fad

Yaazarai: But yeah instead of sweeping a long the lines. Probes are distributed a long the lines and you march/extend perpendicular to them.
Yaazarai: Then I can use my texture mapping by frustum direction and just apply line sweep skews to remap positions. :D
Yaazarai: Okay so pretty straight forward. Map texture memory to screen space via slope formula + modulo across x/y-axis. Then raymarch. Then for merging take the screen space position and map back to memory.

You can avoid interpolating with back planes by just raymarching 2x long rays--which you have to do anyways. Then just get the nearest cN+1 probe(s) by stepping back to the nearest plane the ray passes through.
JoshJers: the modulo doesn't work so hot for non-pow2 textures, that's where the majority of my time has gone 😄
Yaazarai: It should work just fine for any resolution within the limits of -90 and +90 degrees.
Yaazarai: See fad's desmos.
Yaazarai: It only works using the frustum layout to begin with because the number of probes per line changes between frustums.
JoshJers: okay trying to figure out how I'm wrong then
JoshJers: hm
Yaazarai: @JoshJers  here's a 15 x 7. For any arbitrary rectangle if you apply a single skew in one direction N probes go out of bounds and N probes shift within bounds. When you modulo those N probes wrap back into bounds. That makes the number of probes constant.
JoshJers: oh god I think I know what I've been doing wrong this whole time

Yaazarai: How feasible is this gonna be with extensions though?

JoshJers: remind me the difference between extensions and "vanilla" hrc?
Yaazarai: They're separate concepts.
Yaazarai: Extensions combine rays from lower cascades to build rays of higher cascades.

So 2 rays in cN connected end-to-end form one ray of cN+1.
Yaazarai: So starting from c0 you connect c0 rays to form c1 rays, c1 rays connect to form c2 rays and so in up to cN.

Its a substitute for raymarching.
JoshJers: oh you start at c0 and work up then back down?
Yaazarai: No just up.
JoshJers: https://tenor.com/view/patrick-star-spongebob-squarepants-wow-mindblown-gif-13169052
Yaazarai: You can eliminate raymarching by fully extending rays.
JoshJers: I see, but you have to have like a ... diagonal jog basically? to hit the angles that C2 has that C1 doesn't
Yaazarai: Yeah pretty much.
JoshJers: okay because I"m being dense, if you do it that way, do you even have an actual merge step?
Yaazarai: Yes. You still need to merge.

Extensions are a substitute for raymarching. They combine two rays to form a single path of radiance.

Whereas merging combines rays to form a cone of radiance.
Yaazarai: [image]
Yaazarai: Like that.
JoshJers: okay so that's what I meant by "back down" then, you go c0 -> cN to build the rays then back down for the actual RC/merge step
JoshJers: ?
Yaazarai: Oh yeah.
Yaazarai: Extend up. Merge down.
Yaazarai: The benefit of extensions is a cheap approximation of volumetrics + performance increase by eliminating raymarching.
JoshJers: got it
Yaazarai: I think I was the first one to ever try it with Vanilla RC. But I couldn't figure it out back in the day. :(
Yaazarai: Its too hard and not so deterministic with vanilla RC.

RadianceCausality: oh yeah I tried many rays per wedge
RadianceCausality: I had  issues with like

RadianceCausality: it did  look better when that wasn't the  case though
RadianceCausality: how are you doing it then?
JoshJers: right now it's just 
```cpp
  result = float4(0,0,0,0);
  const int rayCount = 16;
  for (int i = 0; i < rayCount; i++)
  {
    float u = (i + 0.5) / rayCount;
    RotSpaceRay rsrC = GetRay(probePos, CascadeIndex, lerp(hData.rayIndex - 1.0, hData.rayIndex + 1.0, u));

    float4 sampled = CastRay(
      TexSource,
      TexDist,
      ScreenPosFromRotPos(rsrC.start - float2(0, offset), pt),
      ScreenDirFromRotDir(rayDir, pt),
      abs(ScreenDirFromRotDir(float2(rsrC.end.x - rsrC.start.x, 1e24), pt)),
      hData.inDim,
      _);

    if (sampled.a != 0)
      { sampled.a = 1; }

    result += sampled;
  }

  result /= float(rayCount);
```
JoshJers: (the alpha hack is due to my source data not having all 0-or-1 alphas but I'm not supporting that yet)
JoshJers: basically just cast 16 rays at the moment, slightly wider than they should (which I think is the actual key here, basically a blur)
JoshJers: then if the alpha's not 1.0 after this, grab the extension and add it with `* (1 - alpha)`
JoshJers: so nothing fancy
JoshJers: also I'm currently not casting rays at all on the overlapping ones (so this is without your cast double and merge trick)
RadianceCausality: Huh, I suppose that might help
JoshJers: yeah here's +/- 0.5 "rayIndex" (i.e. interpolating within the wedge) on the left vs. no interpolation on the right, so it's *better* but it's still artifacty. It only really works when going the whole way to the next ray index, so it's likely it's not the rays themselves that really help but the blurring. Which, you know, has its own problems.

RadianceCausality: Yeah wow that really helps

Yaazarai: This is what I do. I interpolate 4 directions per wedge instead of 2. Although technically I am using HW interp as well. It REALLY helps. Without it my 1/2 HRC hass a bunch of holes.
Yaazarai: I'll send a picture in a bit... But one thing I noticed is that having all rays across the frustum be equal length contributes to the plane artifacts.

Even if you "weight," by angle the problem is that rays near the edge of the frustum hit in cascades that they shouldn't be hitting at.
JoshJers: I've also been think about whether there's a way to do some sort of depth-aware merging from cN+1
JoshJers: like if the rays had depth (Assuming no volumetrics, I guess)
JoshJers: part of the problem (it seems to me) is that the distance of all of the lighting at a given probe is treated as effectively infinity away
JoshJers: although I guess the shrinking distances of the probe rays should mitigate that to some degree?
Yaazarai: You cant shrink rays other wise cN and cN+1 dont connect. If you do shrink them you have to extend them backwards to connect and now you've recreated the problem.
Yaazarai: With normal HRC.
JoshJers: no not the rays from cN to cN+1
JoshJers: the rays stored in cN+1
JoshJers: so that when you try to merge between neighboring probe's data you'd have depth information to do a better angular recreation of what they saw
JoshJers: I don't have a good diagram of what I mean 😐
JoshJers: basically like parallax mapping
Yaazarai: Pleasem cuz my immediate thought is that breaks down with merging.
JoshJers: okay as an example from a realworld thing, early Oculus had a trick where it'd render a frame with whatever head position was available at the time, then use the render + depth information + updated head position/orientation to *warp* the already-rendered frame to make it look roughly like it was rendered at the new position (https://www.uploadvr.com/reprojection-explained/ )
JoshJers: basically when sampling from a disjoint probe, you're sampling from a direction that the radiance wasn't actually captured from, but you could hypothetically use depth information do do a similar trick - to warp the probe's view of what it saw so you sample something closer to what your actual new probe position would have seen
JoshJers: and if you do that for both( or more) cN+1 probes you're sampling, you would hypothetically end up with a more accurate merge
JoshJers: I guess timewarp wasn't the depth-aware thing, but still
JoshJers: I also know that they did a similar thing on SOCOM 4 on PS3 for its stereoscopic 3D (3D glasses/3D TV support), basically rendering the view down the middle then warping it gently in each direction to generate the separate left/right eye images
JoshJers: (or maybe they rendered one eye and warped for the other eye, I don't remember precisely)
Yaazarai: HRC doesn't have perspective issues though?
Yaazarai: So probes shouldn't be seeing anything outside their view.
Yaazarai: Thats why we cast planes of probes.
Yaazarai: I guess it does have depth issues if you consider ray interval length as depth.
JoshJers: I guess what I'm getting at is each probe (per frustum) has a 90 degree field of view of the whole thing which, in my world, it's 1 sample per pixel of "far plane" as it were (i.e. no missed pixels)
JoshJers: for the overlapped probe lines, going from that to the smaller set is easy - the view point (probe origin) is the same
JoshJers: but for the disjoint probe lines that have to extend, you need to grab influence from multiple probe positions
JoshJers: I think it's worth thinking of this as a reprojection problem
JoshJers: you're synthesizing a new viewpoint out of disjoint existing viewpoints
JoshJers: to go from c2 to c1 disjoint you need to use (in my world where it's 4 rays for c1 and 8 for c4) 3 different probes to cover the whole view
JoshJers: and while you have effectively a set of 1D views for each of them, the positioning of the things that are *hit* by those probes' rays can vary along depth
JoshJers: and if you were to gently nudge the sampling from each probe to adjust for the offset of the new synthesized view vs. the original probe's position, you might, say, be less likely to miss a 1px ray due to it otherwise falling just between the cracks of the probes you're blending
JoshJers: basically parallax mapping the cN+1 probes you're casting against, the 2D (or, I guess, 1D?) equvalent of
Alexander: it's curious how good edges are on that image above

Alexander: line sweeping doesn't need more than O(1) per marched line. maybe hrc can run using a similar amount of memory?
Yaazarai: Possibly? You still need to merge though. So if you sweep left -> right and merge as you sweep I guess you could store O(N)? Say you cast the right frustum, you grab the left-most 2 cN+1 planes, merge, sweep and overwrite and move onto the next planes.
Alexander: in theory to calculate [0..inf] radiance interval for any given point, one needs to know just K intervals where K is the number of cascades. and for 2 adjacent points those intervals are going to be the same most of the time, so it should be possible to march along lines and update a set of K intervals that contribute to [0..inf] interval for each point, merge them and keep marching. since those K intervals are only stored in thread local memory, it's essentially free in terms of global storage
Alexander: colors show intervals of different cascades contributing to each point
Alexander: i'm just speculating, not sure if it's actually possible or practical to implement this
Alexander: but 3d hrc seems to be limited by memory
Alexander: and i'm wondering if that's a real restriction or us just being lazy and implementing the most obvious solution
RadianceCausality: That's a real restriction I think
even if we ignored the memory issue its still limited because you have to do O(n^4) computation
RadianceCausality: also the big memory cost is in the highest cascade and that one we're only really storing one line
Alexander: computation is a real limotation, yes. but what about memory? how much memory do you need to calculate rc on a single threaded cpu-type implementation?
Alexander: without doing any redundant work, of course
RadianceCausality: hmm yeah
RadianceCausality: idk

Alexander: but you sometimes want to use the same probe to query radiance for a different "focal point", so there's kind of no uniquely correct way to arrange intervals for that
JoshJers: I'd been trying to build it up in my brain as a reprojection problem, like cN+1 is a series of evenly, spaced, same-direction frustums of radiance, and I have a position back from the line to make a synthetic view of
Alexander: so what each line of probes approximates if vanilla RC probes contain rays:
`l(p, dir)`
what people do when they want to approximate cones:
`integral(-eps..eps) l(p, dir + alpha)dalpha`
what people should be approximating when they want cones:
`integral(-eps..eps) l(lerp(p0, p1, x), lerp(d0, d1, x)) dx`
Alexander: imo you can't find a good way to think about frustums because currently our frustum-based formulations don't work
Alexander: as in, they're inaccurate and only work in the rays approximation

JoshJers: I think that's true regardless of technique?
JoshJers: (otherwise you'd be losing all of the angular resolution you're gaining by cascading in the first place)
Alexander: the nastiest thing is that you're only drawing the "convenient" location of cN line relative to a cN+1 line. see what happens if you consider the other cN line:
Alexander: for the right line of green probes you want frustums to converge on those right probes, not on the left ones
Alexander: so depending on which line of cN you're merging, you want different focal points
JoshJers: the problem is you need two different collapses, basically
JoshJers: I did an implementation where I flipped which one was the "ideal" one
Alexander: and it's only 2 for 2 cascades. it grows exponentially for more cascades

JoshJers: I tried building a "classic RC but with HRC probe positioning" thing, but with the strict doubling of distance/probe count instead of the 4x distance you get per cascade in base RC, the interpolation in of the "behind the target" cN+1 probes was a *lot* more noticeable

JoshJers: but that interpolation model is the only one that I'm aware of that is actually attempting to get an approximation of the radiance from the pov of the cN probe
Alexander: so what i'm thinking is that "integral radiance" between two lines can be represented as this:
`l(x0, x1, y0, y1) = integral(a=0..1) L(lerp(x0, x1, a), lerp(y0, y1, a))da`
this is exact, and we can recover actual radance from that "integral radiance":
`L(x, dir) = l(x, x, x+tan(dir), x+tan(dir)*l)`
and this integral radiance function has a bunch of exact properties:
`l(x0, x0, y0, y1) + l(x0, x0, y1, y2) =l(x0, x0, y0, y2)`
etc
Alexander: maybe what we want to represent is integral radiance (that contains integrals of actual radiance over some ranges of rays) instead of actual radiance that exclusively encodes rays?
Alexander: [image]
JoshJers: yeah, so for all the near-field radiance (i.e. the new radiance at a given cascade level), it's "rays" insofar as they're a reasonable approximation of a narrow area's integrated radiance
JoshJers: but the ultimate post-merge representation is some other ... something.
JoshJers: step 2: ???
JoshJers: step 3: perfect radiance

Youssef Af: Do you have a simple way of storing the data if i placed probes like this ?

Yaazarai: You can store them in blocks and correlate the angle around the circle to the position in the block/ring.
Yaazarai: Then for lookups you can just check which grid-cell (by ring size) that the ray lands in to figure out which ring to merge with. Since each ring is isolated to its own grid cell.
Yaazarai: Easiest method would just be to do a ray-circle intersect to get the point on the ring to merge with and compute the merge index from there.
Yaazarai: Any ray that lands in a grid cell is always going to merge with the ring in that grid-cell which is a nice bonus.

Alexander: i was thinking how to actually make HRC approximate frustums. i came to a conclusion that it's impossible to make it accurate if rays are sampled like this:
Alexander: so it can't work well if we integrate over a frustum of various directions. however, it can be made to work if we integrate over a frustum of possible _positions_ with the same direction:
Alexander: note that within each frustum all rays are parallel
Alexander: diagram shows 2 frustums
Alexander: this all converges to the same thing (rays) for very thin frustums, but for thicker ones i think it means that we have to "fill" a frustum with parallel rays, rather than rays that fan out
Alexander: because the HRC gathering process relies on radiance being constant along straight paths, and that property works for cones of parallel rays (doesn't work for cones of fanning rays)
Alexander: this whole thing is still about me trying to fulfill the dream of making large cones gather correctly

JoshJers: are you correctly weighting your rays based on their effective angular contribution?

Youssef Af: Nope and i dont have a good idea on how to do that and in which stage of merging should i exactly weight them
JoshJers: great 😄 well that's probably a good chunk of it
JoshJers: So if you look at this diagram (From the pins), you'll note that the radial width of the corners is narrower than those that are more axis-aligned
JoshJers: that narrower radial width means that it should have less contribution to the overall radiance
JoshJers: so without doing that, you get more contribution along the diagonals, which is where the X shape comes from
JoshJers: The way I've been doing this is basically, whenever taking multiple rays and combining them (i.e. when using cN+1 samples to generate cN), figure out the angular width of the "wedge" that the sample should represent (i.e. by doing an atan between its two edges) and weighting by that
JoshJers: my implementation is basically shooting rays down the center of each "wedge"
JoshJers: and so I use the angles that are halfway between that ray and its neighbors as the edges, if that makes sense
Youssef Af: I will try
JoshJers: in your case it looks like your'e doing something more like RadianceCausality's canonical solution
JoshJers: this one 🙂
JoshJers: in which case, you have two rays that are effectively approximating the *edges* of each wedge
JoshJers: and so the angular contribution of each to use is the angle from the ray to the center of the wedge it's a part of
JoshJers: so if you take those two ray directions, for instance
JoshJers: (call them ray0 and ray1)
JoshJers: you could merge them as follows:
```cpp
float CalculateWedgeWeight(float2 dirA, float2 dirB)
{
  return atan(cross(float3(dirB, 0), float3(dirA, 0)).z / dot(dirA, dirB));
}


float4 CombineSamples(float2 ray0Dir, float4 ray0Sample, float2 ray1Dir, float4 ray1Sample)
{
  float2 rayAvgDir = (ray0Dir + ray1Dir) * 0.5;
  float weight0 = CalculateWedgeWeight(ray0Dir, rayAvgDir);
  float weight1 = CalculateWedgeWeight(rayAvgDir, ray1Dir);

  return (ray0Sample * weight0 + ray1Sample * weight1) / (weight0 + weight1);
}
```
JoshJers: (no promises that that is exactly correct, but I think it is?
JoshJers: presumably you're only merging 2 rays at a time, in wedges as per the diagram
JoshJers: It's this, basically.
Youssef Af: ok so I should weight them based on the angle they make to the center ray
its computationaly expensive but lets see
JoshJers: yeah, basically
JoshJers: fwiw I do have an approximation of it that works fairly well:
```cpp
float CalculateWedgeWeight(float2 dirA, float2 dirB)
{
  #define USE_APPROXIMATE_WEIGHT 1
  #if USE_APPROXIMATE_WEIGHT
    // This is an approximation of the above. I stumbled into it by initially using cos^2(midpointRayAngleFromForward).
    // The derivative of atan(x/y) == y / (x^2 + y^2), and if we choose a vector that is *roughly* at the midpoint
    //  between the two rays, you can think of target0, target1 instead as:
    //    target0 ≈ targetM - d,  target1 ≈ targetM + d (where d is going to be 0.5 in practice)
    // As our targets get closer together, the angle between them approaches the derivative of midpoint angle from
    //  forward, which and since the derivative of atan is what's listed above, this makes a pretty good approximation
    //  (as our angle deltas are quite small, even at the lowest-level cascades, where the error is highest - at about
    //  ~0.02 or so).
    // But, since the "distance" value is constant across all of the weights for a given cascade, we can factor the
    //  numerator out of it, and end up instead with this very simple equation, which is less work for the GPU than
    //  doing almost the same thing, plus a (2D) cross product and an arctan.
    float2 midpoint = (dirA + dirB) * 0.5;
    return (1.0 / dot(midpoint, midpoint));
  #else
    return atan(cross(float3(dirB, 0), float3(dirA, 0)).z / dot(dirA, dirB));
  #endif
}```
JoshJers: (obviously you could use `rayAvgDir` from `CombineSamples` in my example above to compute this rather than re-computing the average)
JoshJers: it doesn't work in every scenario - it only works as long as the distance between each ray endpoint involved is constant (which it is in the standard HRC formulation), but it's remarkably accurate
JoshJers: so I'd definintely start your implementation with the atan-based version first since it's actually correct 😄

Youssef Af: If you were able to put probes in a texture it should be easy to merge ?
Yaazarai: Very easy. Its just a bunch of ray-circle intersects. The indexing schema easy. Probes around the circle index as a 1D array and the circles all index as a series of 1D arrays.
Yaazarai: Problem is like 40%+ memory waste or something (lots of probes outside of screen).
Youssef Af: You select positions using cpu then for every position you cast the rays
Then you make a compute shader to put these probes back to their place in the texture and the other pixels gets filled with bilnear interpolating
Yaazarai: So not good for 3D.
Yaazarai: Just do it all in a shader.
Yaazarai: You can directly calculate the total probes per cascade as Area/pow2(N) where Area is the area of the circle of the inscribed square of the viewport:
```
r = 1/2length * sqrt(2)
a = pi * r * r
cN_probes = a / pow(2,n)
```
For each probe you then do ray-circle intersects for each outter/inner ring in each ray direction and take the closest cN+1 probe. Then cast a ray to that probe and merge.

Yaazarai: But yeah you define the amount of porbes as the area of a circle that inscribes the viewport. And the number of rings = radius of the circle and you reduce the number of rings for each cascade.
JoshJers: and each probe on each ring is omnidirectional?

Yaazarai: Indexing is just a series of 1D arrays representing each circle.

JoshJers: I'm having a Slow Brain Day 😄
Yaazarai: We take the angle (blue) and reproject the "direction," as if it was coming from the center of the circle. This lets us lookup the cN+1 probe via its polar coordinate around the circle.
JoshJers: right but the reprojection depends on the length from green to the circled red area to be correct
JoshJers: i.e. you already have to know where it intersects the outer circle
JoshJers: i.e. you have to have already done the ray/circle test 😄
Yaazarai: Is that how that works?
JoshJers: your lookup intuition is good
JoshJers: you do the ray/circle test to find the point on the outside, then figure out its angle relative to center, that's your lookup coordinate
Yaazarai: Oh I wasn't thinking of it like that at all.
JoshJers: but you still have to know which point on the circle matters
JoshJers: because a direction, on its own, contains infinity points 😄
Yaazarai: Technically yes, but no.
Yaazarai: This is actually something the Parallax-Fix does in Vanilla RC. It reprojects angles from angle A to reprojected angle B.

TL;DR is you don't actually need to know what point you want to merge with. You take the angle (blue) and rotate the angle (pink) and the angle is equal to the polar coordinate of the point you want to merge with.

I'm pretty sure their should be some fancy vector math to make that work?

JoshJers: So there are two sources of artifacting I'm working around. 
- First image is "tiny light in my implementation with basic disjoint probe mergin and without the "cast 2x length ray and average" smoothing that you have for overlapped probes"
- Second image is currently "cast 5 parallel rays for every ray that are spaced out" which reduces error at ray cast time, because any issue where, say, a texel gets hit twice by some rays and once by others get magnified into giant streaks as the merging happens
- Third image is with just added angular resolution (in this case casting 4x the rays as the base implementation)
- Fourth image is with both
JoshJers: the "5 rays" thing is just a hack, I'm pretty sure a pre-blur or perhaps accumulating a small amount of blurring/averaging when constructing extensions (once I implement them) will accomplish the same thing

Yaazarai: Okay so turns out indexing rings is a mathematical hellscape.

I need to manually precompute all of my offsets or assume a waste factor of memory and fit my rings into a geometric series.
Yaazarai: Computing the total area (probes) of the rings is easy because it'll always less than or equal to `aA_c0 / pow(2,cN)`.

So we can know how many probes are roughly within the rings of each cascade, but not so easily where the rings are in memory.
Yaazarai: :|
RadianceCausality: you brought this upon yourself
Yaazarai: I know. The actual merging/lookups aren't hard, just the indexing part.
Yaazarai: The relationship of circumference between rings is entirely non-linear.
RadianceCausality: just have infinitely large  rings
RadianceCausality: problem solved
Yaazarai: The problem gets much worse if you try to use N-gons as an approximate.
Fixed memory indexing, ruins ray intersection tests.
JoshJers: n-gon indexing but circular positioning 😄
JoshJers: that is, an n-gon projected onto a circle
JoshJers: but imo if you want evenly spaced probes around a circle, going by theta around center seems like it should be straightforward
JoshJers: (emphasis on "seems" 😉 )
Yaazarai: The spacing isn't hard. Its the indexing in memory, finding where rings start/end in memory.

For example c0 rings for the first 8 rings: 6, 12, 18, 25, 31, 37, 43, 50 -> probes per ring.

If we pack these rings of probes into a buffer how do we index the rings to know where they start and end? The number of probes per ring don't increase linearly/geometrically because, 2pi BS. So the only way to figure it out is to pre-compute it recursively and store the results in a lookup table.
JoshJers: the circumference of a circle scales linearly with the radius of a circle, yeah?
JoshJers: like it's `2*pi*r`
Yaazarai: However, to know where ring `r` start you need to compute the circumferance of `0 to r-1` rings which is NOT the same as the area of the circle of `r-1`.
JoshJers: like the numbers you posted could just as easily be 6, 12, 18, 24, 32, 38, 44, 52
JoshJers: or 6 * n
JoshJers: which is much easier to compute 😄
Yaazarai: Probes wouldn't scale properly then, e.g. more interpolation.
JoshJers: the radial spacing between them would be even?
JoshJers: it's the same as a 2x/2x RC
JoshJers: well, if you pretend that you're just doubling radius every time there
JoshJers: double radius, double ray count
JoshJers: same spacing at the ends
Yaazarai: Yes, but for example at ring interval/radius of 256 you'd need 1608 probes, but instead get 1536.
Yaazarai: So you end up interpolating angles more.
Yaazarai: So maybe.
Yaazarai: I guess thats like 95% of probes.
Yaazarai: So it would probably work fine.

Yaazarai: So something like:
```
theta = (PI * 0.5) * (index / ray_count)) - (PI * 0.25);
width = (interval * 2.0) + 1.0;
yoffset = -tan(theta) * width;
```
Negative, because the y-axis is flipped in GameMaker (-y is up).

Youssef Af: How do you do off-screen lights ? rendering a wider frame ?
Yaazarai: With lots of tears.
JoshJers: @Yaazarai here's what mine is doing for evenly-spaced angles:
```cpp
Ray RayFromIndex(float angleIndex, HRCData hd)
{
    // hd.totalAngleCount == the number of total angles at this cascade (for all directions, not per frustum)
    //  so if you have 2 rays per frustum at C1, it'd be 8 (2 rays * 4 frustums)

    // hd.hSelector == (1, 0) when the rays are going mostly horizontally and (0, 1) when mostly vertical

    // Tau is just 2*pi :)

    // To keep the angle boundaries such that each quarter of them share a predominant direction, we start angle 0
    //  at -45 degrees + half the angle delta.
    float angle0Turns = -0.125 + 0.5 / float(hd.totalAngleCount);

    int cascadeDistance = 1 << CascadeIndex;
    float angleRadians = (float(angleIndex) / float(hd.totalAngleCount) + angle0Turns) * Tau;

    Ray ray;
    sincos(angleRadians, ray.dir.y, ray.dir.x);

    // The rays always travel to the next probe line so the more diagonal a ray is the longer it gets.
    ray.length = cascadeDistance / abs(dot(hd.hSelector, ray.dir));

    return ray;
}
```
KornFlaks: Hrm, theres actually quite a bit of artifacting on the build you sent (for optimization I assume).
Dropping my implementation down to approximately screen space only, I get 1.3 ms. Reducing my probe count to 3/4 screen resolution will get ~0.8ms. 1/2 screen probes makes a blurry mess on 1024 resolution.

Yaazarai: Oh so just ray delta from theta.
Yaazarai: Yeah I don't got that in glsl 1.0.
KornFlaks: combined sin-cos math. HLSL re-uses the calculation for sine to calculate cosine in the same operation.
JoshJers: can just do sin and cos separately
JoshJers: optimizer likely will optimize it properly
JoshJers: if it doesn't suck (which it might, who even knows)
KornFlaks: I think the benchmark was that if sine and cosine individually is 4 ops, then sincos is 3 ops.

pulp_user: I haven't followed holographic closely. Could someone give a quick summary to bring me up to speed? My current state of knowledge is from maybe 4 months ago, and boils down to this:
- holographic is new and exciting but it's not yet clear if it is practical
- It is not faster than vanilla (not sure compared to bilinear fix)
- It uses less memory than vanilla
- It can handle sharp shadow edges where vanilla cannot
- There were different approaches about how to merge the cascade data so that you would only do ray casts on the first cascades, but this was not solved yet and produced artifacts. This seemed to be one of the big questions at the time

What did I miss in the meantime? Is holographic the go to thing for certain use cases now?
Alexander: welp, looks like pretty much every point is wrong? :KEKW:
pulp_user: 😅
pulp_user: Did I misunderstand all that 4 months ago, or has lots changed?
Alexander: it's perfectly practical in 2d, it's faster than vanilla for a given accuracy and uses the same amount of memory
Alexander: it's unclear yet what its best formulation is, but what we have is already better
KornFlaks: * Holographic is fairly well mature at this point. It's essentially the cutting edge of 2D RC at the moment.
* For a probe-per-pixel implementation, it is about 50% slower than vanilla but it's superior in appearance (check my images above and notice the ringing, i'm still on vanilla while I work on a side project). 
  You can definitely drop the quality of holographic to achieve vanilla performance while looking better.
   By comparison, bilinear fix is 25% the performance of vanilla while having on par appearance as holographic. So Holographic is objectively superior.
* It uses the same amount of memory as vanilla.
* Seems to do better with sharp corners I agree. The lack of obvious ringing is the biggest benefit with penumbra rendering.
* No clue about the last one.

Yaazarai: I don't think we're going to get stable merge patterns using a single raycast pattern. If we're gonna keep HRC screenspace memory we may need two raycast patterns and just mimic the paper. Then we can mimic the paper's merge pattern.
RadianceCausality: So I was thinking a little bit about hrc in 3d, and the basic problem with it is that the higher cascades are O(N^4)
and I don't think this is solvable without approximation?
so the basic problem is "what's the minimum amount of space necessary to encode the light received from a large distance impacting on a 2d plane"
and I think its basically required to be O(N^4), since you can just go shine N^2 lasers at any point from a mile away.
RadianceCausality: ...so apparently a laser will disperse pretty far over a mile or [so](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beam_divergence), so it might be possible to bound the spacing of the samples within the higher cascades
> [For](https://www.reddit.com/r/askscience/comments/1ihca9/how_much_does_a_laser_beam_spread_over_distance/) a small, green laser, the wavelength is roughly 500 mm, and let's assume that the smallest width is at the pointer, and that it is 1 mm. This means that the spread is 0.00016 radians. Over 3000 feet, that becomes roughly half a foot, so, as this formula calculates the spread between the center and the outside of the beam, the beam is 1 foot wide. 

Apparently you can cheat it if you try hard enough
> [There](https://www.quora.com/What-are-the-most-precise-lasers-lowest-spread-over-distance-What-defines-this-property-for-a-laser) is one known way to beat it. Make the beam bright enough that it causes self-focusing in the atmosphere by turning the atmosphere into a non-linear lens.
RadianceCausality: now, this would mean that at some point you'll be able to be efficient by storing fourier transform / actual hologram of the light at a plane
RadianceCausality: but for that to work decently I believe you need a resolution around equal to the actual wavelength of the light
RadianceCausality: ...although strangely enough you can apparently just go lose all of the lower-wavelength fourier modes since they exponentially fade?
RadianceCausality: which might allow you to trivialize storage??

Alexander: just to highlight how much of an open question this entire merging process is:
Alexander: the two rays on cone boundaries don't really approximate that cone. they approximate a 2x wider cone
Alexander: same how an average of two values sampled at two points don't approximate an integral over a line connecting those two points. it's an approximation of an integral over a line that's 2x longer
Alexander: in order to approximate a purple cone with two rays, they have to be at halfpoints inside of it:
Alexander: not trying to say that RadianceCausality's scheme doesn't work, but i am trying to say that it works despite this. it might or might not hint at something that can be improved.
JoshJers: fwiw I had an implementation that was just shooting a single ray up the center of each cone and it has all the same artifacts as the canonical implementation
Alexander: in my experiments, the artifact pattern entirely depends on the merging pattern
JoshJers: because it's an approximation, I think the two rays at cone edges can be *as good of an approximation* as what you're  proposing, you just have to weight them as if they're that half of the cone
JoshJers: that said, absolutely there are other ways to change the casting/merging pattern that shift the artifacts around 😄

Alexander: but fluence gathering is fundamentally the same operation as merging, because during merging you gather fluence at the position of cN probe from cN+1 probes
Alexander: but you can just use those cN+1 to gather fluence directly
Alexander: and you can easily compare how much information is stored in one HRC high level cascade vs how much information is stored in on vanilla RC cascade and see how much more compact HRC information is for the same amount of memory used

Alexander: what it's lacking is spatial resolution, because rays inside of HRC cells aren't captured
JoshJers: agree there 🙂
Alexander: exactly, it lacks the spatial information _between_ lines
Alexander: which is a very different way from how vanilla RC degrades at higher cascades
Alexander: so maybe HRC needs another structure to fill those gaps
Alexander: so rather than fulfilling the high spatial resolution/low angular resolution role, HRC requires cascades to fulfill the role of capturing more ray origins at lower cascades :thonk:
Alexander: which is a knob that we don't have in HRC

Yaazarai: Why bother though? You cant produce cones without rays anyways.
RadianceCausality: you could use restir or something for sampling in the cones I guess
Yaazarai: Okay I think I am getting it now thanks to @JoshJers .
> "Weight each side of the cone as the angle from the edge to the middle."
Which is this: `tan^−1(y/x)` which is also just `atan2(y,x)`.

When producing a cone, we sample the two rays at the edge of the cone. And weight each ray by the difference of the angle of the ray to the middle of the cone.

And most importantly... you do this when producing the cone, you don't weight the rays directly when tracing, since each ray bounds two cones.
Yaazarai: My problem with weights was I was weighting the rays directly, but you can't do that, because each ray bounds two cones.
Yaazarai: Yeah massive distinction here with separating ray-casting from generating cones, that finally makes sense.
Yaazarai: @Alexander back to the cone thing. If you can generate fluence for cones then couldn't you hypothetically use one low cascade (e.g. c1) and continuously chain intervals (like extensions, but for cones)? And generate output fluence with one cascade?

Yaazarai: Is that what you're trying to do?

JoshJers: it makes sense why the blurring - for every other ray at every level you're basically averaging two sets to make the extended ray, and there are very few rays at the highest cascade where no averaging took place
JoshJers: a good chunk of the artifacts in an extensions-free HRC are due to ray to ray variation at the higher cascades getting effectively magnified to lower levels
JoshJers: not the planar artifacts which I still don't understand how you're NOT getting those 😄

Yaazarai: I'll diagram it later. Normal RC merging has messed us up.

With RC you average every group of 2 or 4 rays. With HRC the groups share their adjacent rays.

So if c1 has 5 rays it produces 4 cones:
cone0 = ray0+ray1
cone1 = ray1+ray2
cone2 = ray2+ray3
cone4 = ray3+ray4

That's it. What I endedup doing was casting pow2+1 rays: 2, 3, 5, 9, 17, etc.

Then stored cones separately as pow2 cones (since pow2+1 rays produces pow2 cones). I then store my rays/cones in separate texture memory.
Yaazarai: @JoshJers did this for me:

Youssef Af: Hold on, isnt this just pre averaging
Yaazarai: No. No its not. Pre-Averaging casts & merges multiple rays at once before storing.

This merging pattern changes which rays you merge with.
Youssef Af: You store cones instead of rays
When merging what do you do ?
Yaazarai: Okay so to preface, a cone is just some number of averaged rays that get merged together, this is the same for RC and HRC.

In RC we produce N/2 cones for N rays. So if your c3 has 8 rays, you average those into 4 separate merged cones. Which is what you're doing here.

In HRC we produce N-1 cones for N rays--which only works for ODD ray counts. So if c3 has 9 rays (pow2+1), that produces 8 cones, because now the cones overlap 1 ray at their edges.

So everything is basically the same for merging, you're just changing the rays you merge with and how many averaged cones you produce.
Yaazarai: Instead this is HRC cones where they overlap 1 ray at their edge.
Youssef Af: I still cant understand whats the difference between yours and regular HRC they sound the same
Are you using more rays per cone or merging with more than two cones ?
Yaazarai: I am doing what the HRC paper is doing.
Yaazarai: Look at the diagram again in my drawing. C2 has 5 rays, but merges more cones (4).

Then look at your c2. You cast 4 rays, but produce 2 cones.
Yaazarai: Its +1 extra ray for double the cones to be merged.
Youssef Af: I was kinda doing the same when I pre averaged every two rays to make merging easier
Yaazarai: If your rays produced the same diagram as your image above then you were not doing the same thing.
Youssef Af: no I'm not talking about the one here, I'm talking about HRC in general

Yaazarai: Difference between RC and HRC?
Youssef Af: no your cone merging and regular hrc
Yaazarai: No you don't understand. The cone merging is regular HRC from the paper. We've been doing it wrong.
Yaazarai: I'll diagram the difference later.
Youssef Af: oh I thought it's something else
Youssef Af: but its much cleaner than anything I got
Yaazarai: It is, because when you merge rays like this, your cones don't overlap and produce gaps between the cones. Gaps between cones in normal RC is fine because of bilinear-interpolation, but we don't have that in HRC.

Left is what you're producing. Right is what it should be, twice as many cones.
Yaazarai: [image]
Yaazarai: You have to change the raycounts too, but whatever thats the idea.
Yaazarai: So if you made those changes, and tried to output the same diagram, it would look completely different (like what I did) because the merging pattern completely changes.
Yaazarai: That's why the paper has this diagram: c0 has 2 rays, c1 has 3 rays, c2 has 5 rays per-probe. So look at c2 (R2), which shows 4 cones and each cone shares an adjacent ray, which is why c2 (R2) only has 5 rays per probe.
Yaazarai: Note rays, 1, 2 and 3 are shared between cones.
Youssef Af: Yey more rays per frustum
Youssef Af: but there is no real use to it
Youssef Af: left 2 rays per frustum, right 1 ray
Youssef Af: Maybe I need more frustums other than more rays
Yaazarai: Okay, so for anyone coming from RC to HRC, I figured I'd model the difference, because we think about how merging rays/cones works differently in RC vs HRC.

In RC each ray of cN effectively produces its own cone, by merging with 2x rays from cN+1 (or 4x, depends on your angular scaling). A cone is just directional radiance, e.g. a group of ray(s).

In HRC this is not the case, cones are merged independent of the rays themselves. In cN we want to produce a cone from two merged rays. So the cone has two rays (left/right), we merge each ray independently and then interpolate those two rays to form one cone.

In the left image is RC merging, one ray produces one cone and merges down.

In the right image is HRC merging, two rays produce one cone and merge down. Aqua is the left ray, green is the right ray, white is the ray (same ray index, different probe positions) shared between each two cones when merging. This produces TWICE as many cones.

I am only representing c2/c3 for ease of drawing...
Yaazarai: This is one of those things, that if you didn't start with RC, the whole merging process of HRC would 100% make sense. But if you start with RC it kinda throws off your whole intuition of how it works.
Yaazarai: The way I find it best to handle cones--albeit extra memory cost--is to define memory buffers/textures separately for cones and rays. That way you can reason about producing them independently of each other. Downside is for a 1024x1024 scene you end up with like 80 textures (or 42 if you just ping-pong the cone merging textures), lmao.
RadianceCausality: Pinned a message.
Yaazarai: So for the left-ray of the cone of cN multiply that by the size of the left-cone of cN+1 and vice-versa for the right. Then we merge the traced radiance by the cone of cN+1.
Yaazarai: Like this:
```
vec4 coneL = merge(weightL * rayL);
vec4 coneR = merge(weightR * rayR);
vec4 fluence = mix(coneL, coneR, 0.5);
```
Where the weight of each left/right cN+1 cone is:
```
float arcL = atan(rayL.y / rayL.x);
float arcR = atan(rayR.y / rayR.x);
float weight = arcL - arcR;
```
Yaazarai: Hashed area (red cones) is the weight (arc-size of cone) and that is multiplied by the traced radiance (yellow rays).
Yaazarai: The paper says to calculate the size of each thinner cone (left/right) as 1/2 of the larger cN cone.
That is conceptually the same as calculating the size of the cN+1 cone you merge with.
Yaazarai: Which is what the formula in #14 does, so that makes sense.
Yaazarai: Then the reason the weight is the arc-size of the cone is because each cone represents a fraction of the arc of the total frustum at their specified intervals.
Yaazarai: Makes sense, neato.
Yaazarai: I feel like I am missing something here @RadianceCausality .
RadianceCausality: ?

Yaazarai: So for the code, I have a very rudimentary DDA:
```
vec4 trace(vec2 rxy, vec2 dxy) {
    vec2 delta = vec2(1.0, dxy.y / dxy.x);
    for(float ii = 0.0; ii < dxy.x; ii += 2.0) {
        vec2 ray = (rxy + (delta * ii)) / worldResolution;
                if (floor(ray) != vec2(0.0)) break;
```
`dxy` is the ray delta vector `merge_probe - source_probe` and `rxy` is just the `source_probe` position.
I'm literally just skipping every-other sample and I don't handle connecting diagonals.

Yaazarai: Okay so super cool with the frustum world rotation stuff. If you do rotations via UV flipping--thanks for the suggestion @McPeverick --you can re-run the same UV flip to flip the image back without thinking about actual rotations.

Like so:
```
vec2 offsets[4];
offsets[0] = vec2(in_TexelCoord);
offsets[1] = vec2(1.0 - in_TexelCoord.yx);
offsets[2] = vec2(1.0 - in_TexelCoord);
offsets[3] = vec2(in_TexelCoord.yx);
gl_FragColor = texture2D(gm_BaseTexture, offsets[int(frustum)]);
```

offsets[2] = vec2(1.0 - in_TexelCoord);
offsets[3] = vec2(in_TexelCoord.yx);
    
vec4 radiance = vec4(0.0);
radiance += texture2D(frustumR, offsets[0] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumU, offsets[1] + (vec2(0.0, -1.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumL, offsets[2] + (vec2(-1.0, 0.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumD, offsets[3] + (vec2(0.0, +1.0) / worldExt));
gl_FragColor = vec4(LINEAR(vec4(radiance / 4.0)).rgb, 1.0);
```

JoshJers: ```cpp
radiance += texture2D(frustumR, offsets[0] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumU, offsets[1] + (vec2(0.0, -1.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumL, offsets[2] + (vec2(-1.0, 0.0) / worldExt));
radiance += texture2D(frustumD, offsets[3] + (vec2(0.0, +1.0) / worldExt));```
JoshJers: you have to also flip the vec2 you're adding.
JoshJers: otherwise you're adding it in the wrong direction
Yaazarai: OH.... Right so because they all face-right in memory I just offset all of them right.
```
offsets[0] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt);
offsets[1] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt);
offsets[2] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt);
offsets[3] + (vec2(+1.0, 0.0) / worldExt);

RadianceCausality: ```
impl ToFluence<fluence::SingleF32> for SingleF32 {
    #[tracked]
    fn to_fluence(
        color: Expr<Color<Self>>,
        segment_length: Expr<f32>,
    ) -> Expr<Fluence<fluence::SingleF32>> {
        let transmittance = (-color.opacity * segment_length).exp();
        Fluence::expr(color.emission * (1.0 - transmittance), transmittance)
    }
}
```

Yaazarai: Okay wait... Transmittance starts at 1 and then the ray travels, hits something and we multiply the absorptive property by that transmittance. And transmittance goes down...

```
void trace(vec2 rxy, vec2 dxy, out vec3 radiance, out vec3 transmit) {
    radiance = vec3(0.0);
    transmit = vec3(1.0);
    vec2 delta = vec2(1.0, dxy.y / dxy.x);
    for(float ii = 0.0; ii < dxy.x; ii += STEP_SIZE) {
        vec2 ray = (rxy + (delta * ii)) / worldExt;
        if (floor(ray) != vec2(0.0)) break;
        vec3 emiss = LINEAR(texture2D(emissivity, ray).rgb);
        vec3 absrp = LINEAR(texture2D(absorption, ray).rgb);
        radiance += transmit * emiss * STEP_SIZE;
        transmit *= exp(-absrp * STEP_SIZE);
    }
}
```

Yaazarai: EEEEEEEEEEHHHHHHHHHHHHHH
RadianceCausality: I said multiply by `transmission = exp(-absrp * SEGMENT_SIZE)`
RadianceCausality: not 1 - transmit
RadianceCausality: and remove the * STEP_SIZE there
RadianceCausality: ```c
void trace(vec2 rxy, vec2 dxy, out vec3 radiance, out vec3 transmit) {
    radiance = vec3(0.0);
    transmit = vec3(1.0);
    vec2 delta = vec2(1.0, dxy.y / dxy.x);
    float SEGMENT_SIZE = STEP_SIZE * sqrt(1 + (dxy.y / dxy.x) * (dxy.y / dxy.x));
    for(float ii = 0.0; ii < dxy.x; ii += STEP_SIZE) {
        vec2 ray = (rxy + (delta * ii)) / worldExt;
        if (floor(ray) != vec2(0.0)) break;
        vec3 emiss = LINEAR(texture2D(emissivity, ray).rgb);
        vec3 absrp = LINEAR(texture2D(absorption, ray).rgb);
        vec3 segment_transmittance = exp(-absrp * SEGMENT_SIZE);
        vec3 segment_radiance = (1.0 - segment_transmittance) * emiss;
        radiance += transmit * segment_radiance;
        transmit *= segment_transmittance;
    }
}
```
RadianceCausality: do this
Yaazarai: Basically the same result, but darker (left is the segment one).
Yaazarai: Same artifacts.
Yaazarai: Which obviously is my tracing function instead of DDA.
Yaazarai: Not sure how you derived this whole segment thing.
Yaazarai: @RadianceCausality why specifically this whole segment thing, this is very specific to HRC.
RadianceCausality: I mean this entire bit is just stuff you should have been doing all along
RadianceCausality: Otherwise you end up with unphysical results like having 0 absorption makes stuff explode
RadianceCausality: And also step_size instead of segment_size results in angular bias
Yaazarai: Okay this makes sense. What I was missing is that I should have been computing the transmittance at the sample-point and adjusting the radiance at the same point by its own transmittance. Then applying that to the total radiance and transmittance.
RadianceCausality: you're discretizing the ray into SEGMENT_SIZE segments which you can analytically evaluate and then merge use normal merging stuff
Yaazarai: Got it, that makes sense.
Yaazarai: Okay I'll go implement DDA and get back to you.

Yaazarai: Hmm, my ray extensions are definitely broken too.
Yaazarai: Ray extensions, left (as mentioned by the paper) only work for even cN+1 planes.
Yaazarai: The paper specifies to do the left pattern, but that only applies to even planes. Since even planes cast rays 2x as long. So an even plane of cN+1 is 2x as length, but odd planes are 1x length. Which means for odd cN+1 planes the aligned plane of cN is the same length.
Yaazarai: So to do the left scheme (as shown in the paper), you'd have to do ray-extensions twice. Once to extend cN -> cN+1 and then a second iteration to extend cN+1 into itself for 2x length rays.
Yaazarai: The paper does not mention anything about ray-extensions for odd/even planes only odd/even ray-indices.
Yaazarai: @RadianceCausality a few problems.

* Ray-extensions in the paper are not explained for even/odd planes. Is this the correct approach? https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1304556173671927878/1407636679971569755

RadianceCausality: RC doesn't really do nesting anyways
RadianceCausality: That's a bad interpretation
RadianceCausality: And yes independent grids
Yaazarai: @Kael HRC is laid out like this. You maintain full resolution on one axis and every probe casts (n^2)+1 rays. There's no nesting, you just connect to the next plane over in the next higher cascade--since all the cascades/planes line up at their start/end points. Merging is fundamentally different than RC--see pinned comment, I explained how it works.

The most straight forward implementation is that you write your shader to handle only the right frustum. You then rotate the scene 0, 90, 180 and 270 degrees to handle each frustum from that same shader input.

If you implement per-paper via DDA every voxel will be hit--since DDA produces 2px wide lines.
Yaazarai: 3D HRC for practical use is a pipe dream.
Kæl: Thanks for this explanation, I'll check it out. I really gotta change my username for searchability lol

- Storing only closest probe (essentially panoramic screen-space)

Kæl: @Alexander Yesterday you mentioned that "you can use vanilla 3d scaling with HRC probe structure" and I'm trying to visualize what you meant. Is it hierarchical like vanilla RC? I'm not sure what that looks like, which would help a lot
Alexander: people focus a lot on aligning cN+1 probe positions and directions very carefully with cN, and using the same positions, etc. but that's all from my perspective entirely arbitrary, you can even use irrational scaling for both the number of probes, the distance between HRC planes and the number of directions, and as long as your merging is correct, literally anything can work.

i don't put any value into the "we must cover every pixel" aspect of the typical HRC layout, and for me HRC is an RC layout that uses 1d interpolation instead of 2d (for flatland) by utilizing the fact that radiance is constant along straight paths and avoids radiance interval overlap, to remove some redundancy that vanilla RC has. everything else is kind of an implementation detail, imo. both of these are just straight up improvements over RC with no real downsides and they don't limit scaling options in pretty much any way.
Alexander: but people really like to attribute this specific scaling scheme to HRC where the distance between planes increases 2x, distance between probes is fixed and the number of directions increases 2x, resulting in cascades of constant size, which i think is just an arbitrarily specific setup
Kæl: With respect to rasterization based RC, to me, it seems like this specific setup is what makes HRC so much cleaner to implement that vanilla RC. Is that right? Or is there maybe more to it than that?
Alexander: imo for rasterization probes have to be aligned for a given direction
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: so this means that for every direction there's a different grid of positions, but they always align within one grid
Alexander: this is necessary if you want each rasterization direction to have the same resolution (which is the number of probes per plane). i'm not sure how you could keep that resolution constant for the typical layout where rays are skewed relative to probe positons
Kæl: So if green, yellow, and purple are rasterization frustums, the probes that are "hit" are perfectly aligned to some uniform grid
Alexander: yes you could align probes that way, but this way the rasterization viewport size is different depending on direction
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: here you can see that for a diagonal direction you need 6 rasterization pixels and for the vertical direction you need just 3 to cover all probes
Alexander: which is just an unnecessary annoyance originating from the typical HRC layout that you don't really have to follow
Kæl: Should I then space the probes out the same distance as their rays? Oh is that what you meant by vanilla scaling?
Alexander: that is vanilla scaling yes
Alexander: but it won't help with rasterization, because the number of probes seen from different directions is still different
Alexander: uhh.. let me try to draw the layout again, i guess it's still unclear
Alexander: imagine the 3 grids on the right are overlapping on top of each other
Alexander: it just becomes really messy if i actually draw them on top of each other
Alexander: but the idea is that probes actually don't matter. they are just starting points from which you cast intervals. so for each direction you can actually use an arbitrary set of starting locations to cast intervals. so it's very helpful to align those locations for each direction such that they project all on top of each other
Kæl: That explanation makes sense. And I think I finally got what you meant earlier. Even though when we slice it we're hitting only 7 probes *directly*, we still need to account for the probes between grid spaces which we can also hit. Is that right?
Kæl: So really here diagonal grids are 2x the resolution of horizontal
Alexander: yeah it's 2x for diagonals
Alexander: but that factor is different for different directions, making it less efficient to rasterize
Alexander: also imagine a high cascade, wtf is this even going to be with skewed intervals?
Alexander: so much wasted space where you actually just need 6 pixels
Kæl: Now it clicked. Okay thanks for helping me understand.
So the goal is then to structure your probes so that probe density along each rasterization "slice" is uniform for all angles of that cascade level.
Alexander: it's not really "the goal", but it really makes sense for a rasterization-based method, imo
Alexander: picking arbitrary probe locations like this might results in some more interpolation artifacts, but it's very hard to predict how much it's going to matter in the end
Alexander: and it's really much easier to test drive them in 2d, than trying to fight with 2 final bosses at the same time in 3d (wacky rasterization + wacky hrc)

Alexander: it only works if you directions can be expressed as integer offsets in probe indices

Yaazarai: Okay, so I'm not 100% sure how much this will actually help aliasing, but you can fix gaps in HRC merging using a 2x method `(2 x N^2) + 1` rays and `(2 x N^2) - 1` cones.

For every odd cone you merge only 1 single ray. For every even cone you now merge 3 total cones, left/right (even cones) and middle (odd cone).
Yaazarai: I'm working on an implementation, but I'm not 100% sure how weighting would work, since the `atan(right.y / right.x) - atan(left.y / left.x)` needs to be modified.

Kæl: My idea was to have (in 2D) a `width x 1` render target which represents all the rays facing the same direction in the fluence field. Vertex buffer is just pixels that are locked to the same grid as the rays themselves, and they are rasterized as points orthographically.
 Each pixel in the output target is a linked list with all the actual rays that need to be traced. The vertex rasterizer is basically a ray-hit query using the depth of the fragment to check intersection with the rays it hits at the position in the "slice" of the fluence grid.

Using linked list, with opaque geometry, I think volumetrics could work. But I gotta work out the above in a way that scales better than "just rasterize the scene a million times".
