Yaazarai: Huh, ray extensions don't need full `RGB16` float precision unlike merging. I wonder why...
Yaazarai: Okay everything is done for my [open-source](https://github.com/Yaazarai/Volumetric-HRC/tree/main) implementation. Here's a list of everything I've come across implementing this:

1. Ray-Extensions starting from c0 provide perfect stability for per-pixel lights. This is due to better diffusion up to cN.

2. You can just eliminate ray-tracing entirely by extending c0 directly and just sampling the origin pixel of each ray. My public implementation has no ray-tracing loop, it just seeds c0 into each frustum direction (since c0 rays are only 1px in length, excluding 2x length rays which I handle differently, see #4).

3. At the cost of slight aliasing performance can be cut by 50% by halving probes along the vertical axis of each plane. I ended up scraping this for this implementation because I didn't like the look or need the performance.

4. For simplicity sake its easier to just extend to 2x-length rays at the end-when merging rather than doing a whole separate pass or by passing in cN+1. This is mostly my opinion, but it very much reduces extra work into setting it up.

5. Ping-Ponging merge textures didn't save me any performance--so I just lazily stored two sets of cascades (one for merging and one for rays).

6. There's not much visual difference between using 8-bit and 16-bit floats for transmittance--otherwise 8-bit radiance will kill the output fluence.

7. Cutting out per-RGB channel transmittance and just writing it into your opacity channel of your radiance texture for volumetrics saves a good bit of performance by halving texture reads. So if you don't need per-RGB transmittance this can help to cut GPU costs, especially if you plan to use 2k resolution--cuts memory usage by 50% and performance by 25%~. Good recommendation from @jason .

8. Other ray-schemes are basically a waste of time unless they reduce the total-ray cost and somehow maintain the same number of ray-directions. For example rendering with 1/2x or 2x total rays is just the same as doubling the scene density.

9. I ended up memory-bound on my compute all 4 frustums in parallel on one-texture method because I absolutely ate up 8GB of my 10GB of GPU memory (some of that is reserved memory). This method works just fine if you ping-pong merge textures to reduce memory usage--but again provides basically no performance benefit.

10. When rotating the frustum texture, you don't need a rotation matrix, can just use UV flips, which is pretty cool. Thanks to @McPeverick .
Yaazarai: I quit, don't need anything else from RC but this.
Yaazarai: Per-Pixel volumetrics are the pinnacle.
RadianceCausality: Pinned a message.

Yaazarai: Instead of rotating the image using a matrix you can rotate the image by flipping your texture coordinates.
RadianceCausality: okaaaay
Yaazarai: Uhhh like this:
```
vec2 offsets[4];
offsets[0] = probe;
offsets[1] = 1.0 - probe.yx;
offsets[2] = 1.0 - probe;
offsets[3] = probe.yx;
```

Alexander: started a raster-based HRC implementation on triangle meshes. proof of concept HRC "layers" rendered in one draw call
RadianceCausality: wait  how  do  you get it in one draw  call?
Alexander: there's two parts to it: slicing planes in one draw call and rendering it from multiple directions. here colors show different directions, each direction is 9 slices.
Alexander: in order to render slices, i rasterize each triangle into the same viewport without the depth buffer and then per-pixel define which slice this fragment belongs to and write it using unordered image write.

as for directions, i currently do it with instancing: render each triangle N times using instancing into the same viewport, and then per-pixel figure out which view it belongs to
Alexander: so the rasterization viewport is always constant this way and it's equal to probes count per slice
Alexander: i guess the instancing part is technically not amazing, especially for higher cascades with many directions. but it 100% beats raytracing, that's for sure
Alexander: so again, it doesn't have to be perfect, it just has to be better than every alternative
RadianceCausality: huh
now that I think about it can't you do beam optimizations for raytracing
Alexander: why optimize something if rasterization does it better?
RadianceCausality: shouldn't this get slow if you have a high detail world?
Alexander: it shouldn't get slower than raytracing
Cody Bennett: I was going to port SPWI to my engine to integrate mega geometry (raytraced Nanite).
Cody Bennett: Curious if that helps at all for rasterized RC, but raytracing is so slow in software.
Alexander: with SPWI there's no choice. rasterization only works easily in full 3d.
Yaazarai: Didn't do much--I'm losing about 10ms to the even-plane exception stuff.
RadianceCausality: maybe try running the even planes  in a separate kernel
Kæl: So the  direction of a slice is perpendicular to the angles you want to trace, then the tracing becomes a single floating point comparison?
Does the number of angles you render it from need to be exactly the number of angles of the cascade you are tracing? Or can you somehow blend between them like with line-sweep AO?
Alexander: you can blend between them when interpolating (during merging or stuff like calculating reflections using HRC)
Alexander: but you have to actually render them from those directions first
AdrianM: I haven't implemented it because this kind of depth reprojection is really only an effective acceleration method for static scenes. In theory just take each pixel, render it as a point and then move it to wherever it would be with the new camera position and render it there. Then you can use that reprojected depth information as a starting point for rendering the next frame.
AdrianM: I was thinking of using this to accelerate ray marching fractals like mandle bulb. Realistically you'd also want some kind of directional depth-aware JFA to correct for points that could be occluded in the depth map.
AdrianM: And you wouldn't use it as is, you'd just be using it to seed the ray starting positions for ray marching. Which would be a significant speed up especially for distorted SDFs (which most fractals are)
AdrianM: It also has application when ray tracing through an octree. Same exact acceleration logic applies, but instead stepping the ray by an SDF, you instead step the ray through the different levels of the octree.
AdrianM: The fractal rendering case is cool but not that practical. The octree case I plan to implement some day because when a node of the octree changes you can mark it as dirty and invalidate the acceleration thus allowing rendering of dynamic scenes.
Yaazarai: Okay so there is a solution to 2x length even planes being costly.

You can store twice as many cones. So instead of merging cones left/right you store the left/right sides of the cone individually.

Then calculate all odd planes first and even planes second. This is because even planes sample the same set of cones at their endpoints as their nearest even plane. When calculating the left/right side of cones of even planes, now we can merge with the left/right sides of each cone of the nearest odd plane when calculating our interpolated fluence.
Yaazarai: But.... I don't think it'll help much, because you still have to sample the near/far planes, you just skip having to do the extension.
Yaazarai: I don't think the tradeoff of having 2x the memory is worth it though.
Kæl: I thought of a way to merge HRC with rasterization by using actual frustums. We can map c0 frustums (beige/green/blue) to c1 frustums (purple) by mapping the texels of the c0 far plane to the texels of the near plane of c1. Then rasterize the scene against c1 and compare hits using depth buffer. Any hit transmits light to the occluding geometry. Remaining texels transfer irradiance to the c1 frustum and we repeat the process.

The frustums would need to be large though I think. So macro-level HRC that transmits light on contact with the scene, which avoids merging.
Kæl: Ah but there's problems with too much ray bending... probably instead of bigger parent frustum, needs to be smaller and more frequent.

akadeschain: I gave a talk that ended up talking about this sort of thing, I thought it might be novel but you definitely beat me to it I found your vid like a year later haha. I called it the "global lattice".

You can then view it as just finding the optimal fitting strategy between them, balancing cost of over-draw, and meshing

Alexander: pixels don't matter. what matters is how many intervals each cascade has. and that's a wildly arbitrary metric, because you can scale the number of starting positions, the number of directions and the distance between planes within each cascade arbitrarily.
Peter226: yeah thats kinda my idea too, but to achieve that I had the idea to use similar tech to what VR does, it cuts up the edges of triangles and distorts them, as if you were applying a fisheye lens on the vertices
Peter226: like, if these lines were uniformly placed this would be the result
Alexander: i keep saying it, but i don't think people listen: there's nothing special about those arrangement that people usually depict for HRC. it's just one choice of how HRC can scale. you can (for example) increase the number of directions 4x per cascade and reduce the number of probes 2x per line. or make line spacing 2x larger. every option will work and the only way to constrain which one works better is to use something like the penumbra condition
Alexander: not sure what this depicts
Peter226: hmm how can I visualize this better
Alexander: anyway you can definitely use a non-uniform (although rectilinear) grid with HRC to cover a large (potentially infinite) volume with a cascade of a fixed size
Peter226: the fisheye application, it is applied to the vertices and the rasterizer can work on it as if it was a normal model while producing more pixels at the desired areas

Kæl: You mean that both of these are equally valid in HRC context? Each frustum is effectively a hierarchical extension of the one(s) below it.

Peter226: The problem would be that if the vertices overlap into different zones the actual voxels would be distorted. The vertices are moved correctly, but the pixel positions are just stretched towards each vertex. It would work on dense meshes well, but large objects would show problems
Alexander: they're both screwed in terms of weights that you'll need to assign to those directions to avoid anisotropic effects, but again i consider that to be an implementation detail
Alexander: i'd need to implement it to see why it doesn't work

Peter226: the left is the result if you just move the vertices, the right one is if you cut the mesh along the distortion borders. It will be smushed at different rates as expected. If you were to un-smush the grid the one on the left would appear incorrectly
Alexander: maybe you need to un-smush the grid continuously as well? then the edges would bend instead of becoming broken
Alexander: the grid undergoes a continuous transformation and you can account for it during interpolation/merging
Peter226: Yeah the grid does, and the vertices too, but the pixels and their output positions do not
Peter226: The rasterizer is not aware of our distortion and will rasterize it as if it were in linear space, which is not the case

Kæl: @Alexander Does some formulation of HRC exist where all frustums are the same size? I've been trying to come up with something like this but so far they all seem to bend the light around occluders.
Alexander: i'm not sure what you can possibly get from it. an important principle of HRC is that intervals don't overlap
Alexander: so that you're not wasting memory on storing over-determined information
Kæl: I have a mental model for HRC made out of uniform frustums, which should accelerate light propagation when used with rasterization.

Alexander: i think it sounds too abstract to say anything. imo if you want to experiment, implement it in 2d and see how it performs
Yaazarai: They scale the same but don't perform the same. Disconnected intervals provide a lot more opportunity for planar artifacts.
Yaazarai: I already tried something similar. You cant have angular discritization that wide. It just doesn't make sense.

If all your frustums are the same size from near/far there's no discretinization of angle over distance. So left/right blur together.
Yaazarai: In 2D at least you need to be able to distinguish between left/right to have discrete radiance that's even remotely accurate.
Kæl: Looks like you were able to do it, or something at least? Or maybe this wasn't the implementation that used uniform cones.
Yaazarai: No, that was just my first semi-working implementation of HRC.
Kæl: Ah so no uniform cones. Do you think it's not possible, even if we encode RGB in separate channels dedicated to left/right directions? Doubling the memory cost wouldn't be an issue, I'm just trying to prove the concept.
Yaazarai: Just use half the cone width?
Yaazarai: But anyways you can force it to work, but anytime you have overlapping cones within your radiance field you'll end up with artifacts.

I think for this to work you'd have to also increase the base width of the cone as well. Then interpolate between an adjacent cone. So in your far field you would have less overall cones--and less occlusion.

If you aren't interpolating over angle then you have to trade that for interpolating over position.
Yaazarai: @Kæl This would be similar to mip-mapping but in HRC across the plane.
Kæl: funny, I just wrote down a note for mip-mapped frustum planes

Kæl: Okay so basically just segment frustums into hierarchical segments, with one whole connected frustum propagating the lighting for however small the sample area is at c0?
Yaazarai: Like this.
Yaazarai: You get angular discretinization by interpolating over space using the wider frustum bases.

This only works because you widen the frustum base. If all frustums of all cascades have the same base width it won't work.
Yaazarai: But yes. Pretty much this.
Yaazarai: What we're doing is trying to reconstruct the full frustum over N cascades. So we have to segment the frustum either through rays or mipped wedges.
Yaazarai: This would also work with ray extensions. In 2D at least

Yaazarai: I have ideas for two separate implementations:
* One cone per-probe per cascade (generated from extensions).
We merge the full frustum. Since we maintain the full resolution along the y-axis you can always merge 1:1 the frustum cones.
* `sqrt(2)` cones per probe, more angular discretion, small cone-width scaling, less potential for light-leak perpendicular to the frustum.

Yaazarai: You can't merge with more than one cone at any cascade either, because the cone's base/frustum width is equal to what it should be at that interval. Where as that is not the case with rays, so we have to branch and cast more rays to achieve the same frustum width.
Yaazarai: The obvious loss is there's no discrete angles. Which is why I recommended the second idea.

Alexander: i think there's two distinct ways of looking at extensions: one way is thinking of each interval as a vector sum of its parts, so in order to construct a cN+1 inteval we try to construct it in all possible combinations of cN intervals. in 2d with alighned probes the number of combinations is always 2, but i don't know what it should be in the general case or in 3d.

a completely different way of looking at it is extracting cN+1 interval directly from cN by interpolation
Alexander: that's why i never use 45-degree intervals
Alexander: all my angles are half-integer
Alexander: so they never overlap like that
Yaazarai: Complicates extensions though doesn't it?
Alexander: my extensions are currently implemented with interpolation, so the code is exactly the same for any probe layout, any scaling and any directional layout
Alexander: but it produces a different result from the case like the image above where you could represent a cN+1 interval as a sum of 2 cN intervals
Alexander: and i don't know which one is more correct
Alexander: also either of these implementations would be better than anything that we currently have in world space 3d
Alexander: so i'm leaning towards whatever is easier to implement or whatever works faster

JoshJers: I think
Alexander: you can build this about 2x cheaper than normal if you build every odd interval, and then every even interval is a half-sum of its adjacent odd intervals
Alexander: it has dawned on me that actually the act of building extensions by "observing" cN from the perspective of cN+1 intervals (as my 3d demo does) and building those intervals by connecting them (as the paper does) is actually an approximation to the same operation: calculating a cN+1 interval.

in other words, the goal of extensions isn't to interpolate and it's not to connect rays, it's to correctly reconstruct what cN looks like if you observe it from the perspective of each cN+1 interval, but there's no single obvious way of doing it.
Alexander: this also means that whatever "looks" better, has to also be more correct
Alexander: at least i don't see how something can result in a better extensions algorithm while producing a worse looking image when observed directly
Alexander: it might seem like a semantic difference from an outside perspective, but i really need to think of what approximates an interval better: connecting two intervals like vectors, or interpolating it between two intervals
Alexander: i think this has a good chance of looking worse if you evaluate its shadows. shadows will likely become blurrier.
Alexander: you should always include an occluder in your tests
Alexander: because GI is actually a trivial problem (in 2d at least) if you don't account for occlusion. it's just a convolution.

Alexander: i realized that when using extensions, lower cascade always contain more information than higher cascades. and it makes sense, because higher cascades are derived from lower ones. so technically, we could calculate full GI/fluence exclusively from c0, it'd just be very slow, so we use higher cascades to integrate data faster over multiple directions, so that we don't have to reconstruct those paths form c0. however, those paths can be reconstruted from a lower cascade, and that might be more accurate under some circumstances.

for example, sometimes it might be beneficial to trade speed for accuracy and merge a bunch of longer intervals at c0 before switing to c1, then merge a bunch more intervals at c1, etc
Alexander: currently we always merge either 1 interval from each cascade or 2 intervals in HRC, depending how they align (even-odd). but technically, nothing prevents us from merging 10 or 20
Alexander: left: what we're currently doing in HRC merging, right: what we could be doing in HRC merging to get a higher spatial resolution
Alexander: each color is one cascade
Alexander: this is also useful for specular reflections, since you can achieve arbitrarily sharp reflections with this, although at some higher merging cost
Alexander: this is also hugely useful to mitigate deminising probe count in 3d scaling, since you can't afford to same count on every cascade per plane
Alexander: you can raymarch(via merging) part of normal cN+1 path using cN probe resolution
Alexander: got my first 3d hrc irradiance merged
Alexander: currently ultra glitchy, but most of the things are there already, now need to tweak and make it faster
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: i think i have so much better understanding of 2d hrc now.. but now i'm on hard mode dealing with 3d scaling
Alexander: shadows. still can't understand why there are seams
Alexander: hmmm.... found a way to do HRC merging that results in much smoother connected rays. left: usual merging, right: with connected rays, elongated rays for illustration
Alexander: this looks like forking fix, but it's actually not: rays are cast exactly the same way, but during merging I interpolate cN so that it matches with cN+1 probe positions
Goobley: So that artificially increases the angular resolution of cN, but post tracing?
Alexander: there is no tracing here, everything uses extensions
Goobley: Ah, okay, so it’s basically making sure that everything smoothly ~~interpolates~~ connects
Goobley: That makes sense
Alexander: so rays aren't really cast, it's only additional interpolation during merging
Alexander: unfortunately it makes the most expensive operation 16x more expensive. sadge.
Goobley: You just construct approximate paths during the extension that add up to what the interval should be? Like in the hrc paper?
Alexander: i don't construct paths from non-parallel segment like in the paper. instead, i interpolate probes of cN and then merge interpolation results to obtain cN+1 intervals. it's not equivalent
Alexander: i have not implemented the thing that the paper implements, because i don't know how to express that in a general way
Goobley: Ah right, I guess that’s fine for isotropic emission terms. Being able to get this close without any tracing is super cool
Alexander: i'm now convinced that HRC does not need any tracing

RadianceCausality: like instead of using cN you merge cN with itself and use it as cN+1
Alexander: yes, sort of

RadianceCausality: and you should be able to do this arbitrarily right
RadianceCausality: like instead of having 2 repeats you have 3
Alexander: left is how HRC usually merges this case. but you can actually skip one or more planes of cN+1 as shown on the right
RadianceCausality: yup alright
Alexander: and the forking thing happens because instead of connecting to one probe in the direction of cN, i merge to 4 probes (my branching factor is 4 on each axis) in the direction of cN+1
Alexander: this works like elongating rays in vanilla, so you can create arbitrarily long rays for visualization purposes and stuff. but in practice you're gaining more spacial resolution this way, but losing angular resolution
Alexander: but you can compensate for lost angular resolution by merging cN in multiple directions with itself as you said
Alexander: also this scaling scheme seems to work amazingly well:
```cpp
    glm::uvec2 probes_count = {256, 256};
    glm::uvec2 dirs_count = { 1, 1 };
    glm::uvec2 planes_count = { 16, 16 };
    for (size_t cascade_idx = 0; cascade_idx < 5; cascade_idx++)
    {
      cascade_storage.emplace_back(CascadeStorage(core, probes_count, dirs_count, planes_count));
      probes_count /= 2u;
      dirs_count *= 4u;
      planes_count /= 2u;
    }
```
Alexander: note that it does reduce the number of probes each cascade. it oversamples spatial scaling 2x per cascade relative to the penumbra condition
RadianceCausality: Actually don't you need to merge cN with itself up to 3 times anyways because of the 4x plane spacing?

Alexander: for funsies, another constant-cascade-size scaling, which violates the penumbra condition (directions don't increase fast enough with higher cascades):
```cpp
    for (size_t cascade_idx = 0; cascade_idx < 5; cascade_idx++)
    {
      cascade_storage.emplace_back(CascadeStorage(core, probes_count, dirs_count, planes_count));
      /*probes_count /= 2u;
      dirs_count *= 4u;
      planes_count /= 2u;*/
      probes_count /= 1u;
      dirs_count *= 2u;
      planes_count /= 2u;
    }
```

Youssef Af: Hold on, you can use extensions from one cascade ?

Yaazarai: So for diffused GI its 10x better than raytracing.

Alexander: in 3d the scaling in directions count is 1:4 on each axis, but that's too much to draw, so i'm showing just 2x scaling. i'm also using 1:4 scaling in planes count, so that's accurate
Alexander: each cN+1 interval is constructed from 4 cN interpolated intervals
Alexander: interpolation is done exactly the same way as here when observing cascades directly
Alexander: so ultimately that's the only function that ends up reoccuring everywhere: given one cascade, to interpolate an arbitrary interval from it
Alexander: you can only construct intervals that start on one plane and end on another plane
Yaazarai: I figured out arbitrary ray-extensions using interpolation, so that's neat.

1. Calculate ray-endpoint for current ray.
2. Get the normalized distance (0...1) across the frustum.
3. Multiply normalized value by cN-1 ray count -1 (to get index).
4. Offset -1 and +1 for left/right ray directions (fractional values).
5. Then use those fractional indices to connect cN-1 rays.
(No offset at end-point rays for obvious reasons).

Alexander: i think it behaves weirdly is because fundamentally, extensions is interpolation and merging is integration
Alexander: but if your probes are nicely aligned, then integration becomes interpolation, but it's not true in the general case
RadianceCausality: Oh right I'm also doing evenly spaced angles here instead of evenly spaced intersections with the next line
Alexander: because in merging process you collect multiple directions into one (integration) and in extension you have a lot of positions and you extract one direction from them (but ultimately only one of them will have a contribution)
Alexander: extensions builds intervals, but merging builds cones
RadianceCausality: Yeah
There's that shift from fluence to angular fluence

Alexander: since all operations in HRC during merging and extensions can be expressed as multi-dimensional integration, i was considering to use a sum area table for that. note that integration always takes place over a 5d "rectangle" (for example, from this probe index, direction index, plane index to this probe index, direction index, plane index), this means that any integral can be expressed as a difference of two reads in that sum area table
Alexander: but then i think that calculating such a table might be exactly equivalent to extensions/merging respectively...
Alexander: still, i wonder if there's anything in this direction... especially for non-integer scaling schemes and large scale multipliers (like when the number of probe directions increases by 16 each cascade)
Goobley: Slightly off-topic for the actual development of HRC, but I can't decide if HRC actually gives a meaningfully more efficient representation of a "hologram" on a surface like what Alexander did with vanilla RC here. Any thoughts? https://youtu.be/xkJ6i2N32Pc?si=0eSVzTqpKEpl6PAl
Alexander: i think HRC only has a meaning when it's encoding radiance field in a volume, not on a surface

Alexander: for a dense representation HRC would suit very well
Alexander: but for sparse.. yeah a bit annoying
Alexander: i guess you could use dense planes, but each plane is a sparse quad tree

Alexander: actually extensions work very well with sparse planes. if you extend an interval and at least one of its constituent parts is present, the interval is also present
Alexander: so that way you'd not even need to raymarch anything

Alexander: i think screenspace RC is in some sense equivalent to capturing the radiance field over a surface (of the depth buffer). and HRC is specifically exploiting symmetries of a dense representation of a radiance field in ether 2d or 3d
Alexander: it'd be great to somehow combine them, but i just don't see how
Alexander: HRC solves a problem than SPWI doesn't have
Alexander: of overlapping intervals
Cody Bennett: I think it would be a modification to the vanilla merging process and not an adaptation of HRC.
Cody Bennett: And you pointed out why. Can't use the inventions of HRC when operating over a surface or height map.
Alexander: btw it's unfair to say that SPWI doesn't have the kind of problem that HRC solves. it does have the same overlapping rays in the image plane, but it also captures intervals in other directions that don't overlap the same way
Alexander: so it's not like SPWI is perfect in this sense, it's more like the solution HRC provides is not general enough to apply to it, maybe
Yaazarai: I don't see how you'd apply it with irregular probe placements on the depth buffer anyways.
Alexander: because if you select a flat depth buffer and only rays that are in the image plane in SPWI, then specifically under those scenarios HRC would absolutely help. it's just SPWI has a more complex geometry than that and it's unclear how to generalize HRC to cover that
Yaazarai: Okay so I found a decent optimization. Instead of a constant probe reduction per-plane for all cascades... You can define a min/max probe spacing and set that per-cascade. That way I can say have 8-probes per pixel in the higher cascades, but 2-4 probes per pixel i nthe middle cascades.
Yaazarai: So just reducing probes along the plane without the pow2 scaling aspect.
Yaazarai: Which is really nice, cuz if you do a spread of 1-8px (70% reduction), 1-4px (52%), 1-2px (27%).
Yaazarai: Will update this weekend with the different scaling factors.

Cody Bennett: Maybe see the latest in #projects.
Alexander: where's your implementation?
Yaazarai: 4x plane spacing isn't worth it. Its the same cost as 2x, but more complicated.
Yaazarai: So far the only worth-while optimization is just reducing probes on the y-axis and interpolating.
Yaazarai: Everything else just increases complexity and doesn't add any meaningful optimization.
Yaazarai: Changing the ray/merge pattern ruins the output.
Yaazarai: Increasing planar scaling increases complexity and produces the same throughput.
Yaazarai: Like I said, I'll have visual results this weekend--but just doing a linear probe reduction on the y-axis drastically improves performance.

If you do a linear scale of 16px spacing (c0 = 1px ... cN = 16px) You can get 4k performance (at least in my particular implementation) down from 45ms -> 12ms.
Yaazarai: Also you can use mip-mapping with it and just mip the y-axis (without ray extensions).
Alexander: fixed some srgb garbage, normalization, added multi-bounce, etc
Alexander: i think optimal scaling has to be of constant cascade size. in 2d this means 2x plane distance scaling, in 3d it's 4x.
Yaazarai: At least for 2D, depends on what you mean by constant. For HRC cone merging is constant size (pow2 cones), but rays/extensions at c0 -> width*2 to cN -> width+1 (due to casting pow2+1 rays).

For the 3D case that's mildly confusing, at least when I calc'd it for 2D, the case if you double distance between cascades you ha to also double the number of rays. So the end-result is the same amount of total samples/throughput, just with less total cascades.
Yaazarai: How're you doign multi-bounce with extensions? Is it just diffuse sampling?
Alexander: i don't have any +1's in my implementation, so all cascades are of the same exact size
Yaazarai: Oh right. I just got interpolated ray-extensions working so I can do the same in 2D.
Alexander: > the case if you double distance between cascades you ha to also double the number of rays. So the end-result is the same amount of total samples/throughput, just with less total cascades. 
yes, except in 3d dirs count is a ivec2 instead of int and probe count is ivec2, but planes count is an int. so dirs count increases cascade memory (4, 4)=16x, probes count decreases (2, 2)=4x, and planes count decreases 4x, so 16x/(4x * 4x) = constant size
Alexander: at least that's the scaling that seems to work best for me so far in 3d, i don't have any other justification for why it should be optimal except that it satisfies the penumbra condition and it's constant size
Skybar: @Yaazarai hi! im looking at your implementation (its very cool!), is it expected that the volumetrics absorb differently depending on resolution? Here is 512 and 4096
Yaazarai: The volumetrics are functioning as expected. The difference is due to scaling. I'm auto-scaling the same scene, which means the density on the 512 scene is much lower, so it occludes less. I couldn't be bothered normalizing the results.
Yaazarai: If you took the same downscaled scene and rendered it directly into 4096x4096 it would produce the same results as on the left. Its just a difference in density due to scaling.
Skybar: ah right i see, that makes sense, thank you 🙂

Yaazarai: I think if you're using 1/2 as many rays, you'd need to compute 2x extensions (which is fine because 2x reads is better than 2x writes for throughput) and interpolate in order to keep the same stability. Since the end-goal is to maintain stability at low resolutions.

Yaazarai: No. HRC's merge pattern only works because it maintains full probe resolution on one axis.

Alexander: alright, so let's assume we have a perfect sparse storage, implemented in whatever way. let's assume it requires some amount of memory to store c0 in a sparse way. I think that if we want an optimal implementation, then at least for simple geometry (like an infinite plane), each sparse cascade needs to use on average that same amount of memory that c0 did.
Alexander: a loose justification as to why: if it takes more than that, we're in trouble and we'll run out of memory. if it's less than that, then it means we could get away with having a more aggressive scaling.
Alexander: but this rises an entirely new way of thinking about RC/HRC layuots: how to pack probes in a way such that their _sparse_ representation remains equally sparse for all cascades?
Alexander: curiously, for vanilla 3d RC it's acutally not that hard: just a flatland-like scaling (2x2x2 spatial, 2x2 angular) should suffice, because if we want to sparsely encode an infinite plane, then one of the 2's in 2x2x2 cancels, and then we're left with 2x2/2x2 = const.

however, it's absolutely unclear how to encode a sparse HRC and/or how to encode sparse extensions without increasing the memory cost
RadianceCausality: practically speaking for vanilla RC most scenes actually have a higher fractal dimension so you can probably get by with even less higher-cascade stuff
but ofc having this scaling is necessary for a flat plane
Yaazarai: Okay I think my last attempt will just be the simplest method... Just allocating Pow2 rays, offset so that each frustum doesn't share a ray at their edge.
Then for ray-extensions I'm going to have to extend between frustums--by allocating every direction in memory--for rays that share an edge.
Then since the sampling point doesn't match up with the planes the solution is to essentially do the interpolated fluence for the ray extensions like we do with 2x planes.

AdrianM: And yes, I am 100% sure that edgeCount is an int, this isn't some weirdness with floating point imprecision where 6 != 6.0
Alexander: never rely on mod returning a 0
Alexander: mod(val, val) might return 0 or val, for all intents and purposes randomly
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: This fixed it for me
Alexander: for ints it's fine
Alexander: but not for floats
Alexander: this should work with % just fine
AdrianM: https://registry.khronos.org/OpenGL-Refpages/gl4/html/mod.xhtml
"This is computed as x - y * floor(x/y). "
Alexander: again, for floats it's not the same, because floor(val / val) might return 0.0f or 1.0f depending on the phase of the moon
Alexander: you can't rely on it
AdrianM: That part I understand
AdrianM: The part I don't understand is why the result changes depending on where I get the float from
Alexander: because the compiler is allowed to rearrange the code
AdrianM: int(6) and int(somevarthatis6) should be the same...
AdrianM: Oh well
Alexander: if you know your x and y are floats containing ints, then do this instead:
`x - y * floor((x + 0.5f) / y)`
Alexander: this is stable
Alexander: ints are the same, but as soon as you put them into floats, they're not
AdrianM: I would assume a-b*(a/b) is also stable. The a/b will always round down since they are both integers
Alexander: if they're integers, it's stable and a/a is always equal to 1
Alexander: if they're floats, floor(a/a) should be expected to be either 0.0f, or 1.0f
Alexander: btw that behaviour can even change while running the same shader on the same machine over time or even be different for different pixels
AdrianM: Oh goodness
Alexander: it can change over time because the driver is allowed to recompile the pipeline while you're using it, it happens all the time on nvidia
Alexander: i also recall it happening across different pixels, but that i'm less sure of
AdrianM: I knew that floats are a little chaotic but I was under the impression that they would at least produce the same chaotic result each time
Alexander: they produce the same result while they run the same machine code
Alexander: but you have no guarantee that your shader will correspond to the same machine code every frame
Alexander: in fact, we know it's not how modern drivers work that optimize your pipelines as you're running them
Alexander: so an easy rule of thumb is to just assume that every float randomly jitters by eps after every operation. it's not what's actually happening, but it's not even that far from the truth and prevents mistakes like that
AdrianM: I've used epsilon like that before, is there a standard value you can set as the epsilon? Most of the time it feels arbitrary
Alexander: 0.5f
Alexander: for integers
AdrianM: Yeah, obviously
AdrianM: but for floats
Alexander: ideally write code that doesn't require it
Alexander: but it's hard
AdrianM: Yeah, I've been in that habbit for a long time. Every once in a while it is unavoidable though
Alexander: the best programmers i know write code without any epsilons, and if you look at it it doesn't even look strange
Alexander: but it's actually very cleverly crafted

Yaazarai: @Alexander found a method to avoid sweeping layout. Right-casted rays, rotate the scene around it center as you sample/build extensions.

The potential issue is that the corners going outside the texture, requires upsizing the texture so that the total area is 2x as much... Which can be resolved using a mip1 of the scene.

I'm wondering if it can be done without upsizing/mipping?
Alexander: that's what i did here and that's why it looks so trippy towards the edges of the scene https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xT_7JtYXdNc
Alexander: a major downside is that node locations don't match for different directions
Alexander: so doing stuff like hrc extensions is really tricky
Yaazarai: Well extensions with rotated planes in general becomes very tricky.
Yaazarai: I know how to make them work though.
Alexander: certainly a good exercise/alternative to have in your toolbox
Alexander: worth implementing
Yaazarai: Agreed. The line-sweep method while cool brings up too many unknowns, see #offtopic . Where planes get closer together in space as they're rotated.
Alexander: but i'm thinking long-term it's really importnat to develop a robust and convenient to use skewed sweeping tools : a bunch of well tested, fast and predictable utility functions
Alexander: sure lines at 45 degrees are going to be 1/sqrt(2) times closer together, that's the worst case
Alexander: so?
Yaazarai: Its not an issue, but the interpolation gets more complicated. I think I'm on the right track.
Yaazarai: The planes being 1/sqrt(2) closer means rays are shorter and the Vanilla HRC extensions don't work.
Yaazarai: And I'm wanting to do Vanilla HRC extensions.
Alexander: 🤨
Yaazarai: So vanilla HRC extensions roughly. But if the rays get shorter near 45 degrees, you can't do extensions without potential occlusion issues because the rays are too short.

So for example at c7 rays should be 128px, but at 90 degrees they're 90 pixels in length, so to fix that you'd need to interpolate the total length across two planes.
Alexander: for hrc i use an arrangement like this. red are probes, green/orange/blue are some intervals that i store. note how horizontal intervals are on average more spaced apart from each other than intervals that are more horizontal. and it works perfectly fine with extensions. obviously, extensions require interpolation between nearby probes, but that's not an issue at all
Yaazarai: You can't use that for camera-stable 2D HRC.
Alexander: why would you think so?
Yaazarai: I'm not 100% why it happens, but anything apart from the c0-cN vanilla HRC extensions causes massive flickering and artifacts at the per-pixel level.
Yaazarai: So yes you can do it, but it looks awful in motion.
Yaazarai: I've tried using cones for extensions instead of Vanilla HRC extensions, same problem.
Yaazarai: (I'm very specifically trying to solve the problem at the per-pixel level, not in general)
Alexander: yeah i'm not convinced. i think it entirely depends on how you're doing your extensions, and how exactly you're interpolating extended intervals specifically
Alexander: pixels have no meaning, it's just a grid
Alexander: btw you can also have sweeping arrangement where probes aren't even aligned with pixels, like this:
Alexander: it's literally arbitrary floating point positions where lines happen to intersect
Yaazarai: Yes, but diffusion of samples over that grid matters. Since at the per-pixel level, e.g. low resolutions radiance values can shift drastically depending on where lights are in the scene.
Alexander: you're introducing error by snapping intervals to an arbitrary grid of pixels that's entirely artificial
Alexander: nothing should prevent you from having a light source that has the radius of 3.14159265358 pixels
Alexander: real vector objects are not aligned to pixels at all
Yaazarai: True, but I'm rendering from a rasterized scene--not from geometry.
Alexander: you can treat rasterized scene as a set of square pixels
Alexander: and you can calculate accurate vector intersections of vector lines with those square pixels
Alexander: like this:
Alexander: note how the red ray intersects very specifically the orange pixel
Alexander: but if you move it down by 0.3 pixels, it'll intersect the blue one
Alexander: even though if you rasterize that ray it might look the same
Alexander: but its subpixel position matters, ray is just a floating point origin and a floating point direction
Alexander: also note that sometimes it does make sense to treat pixels as raster, and sometimes it does make sense to snap rays to integer pixel positions, but only for the sake of optimization, like for line sweeping and such. but it does introduce error and it should not be though of as a reference to strive for. it's an artifact, not a feature.
Yaazarai: Right, this I understand... I've done interpolated extensions using less rays in HRC. Its like 90% working.... Frustum edges are always a problem.
Yaazarai: I factor in the exact position of expected rays in merging interpolated exactly from actual stored rays.
Alexander: even in my 3d impl i don't have that issue at all, i have no artifacts on cardinal directions that i could observe
Yaazarai: Are you storing any actual rays in cardinal directions?
Yaazarai: What the hell.
Alexander: i store all directions as half-integer
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: i encode ray directions by their tangents
Alexander: for example if there are two rays, tangent coordinates are 0.25 and 0.75. in the general case of N rays, tangent coordinate of i'th ray is `(i + 0.5) / N`
Alexander: note that it never reaches 0 and it never reaches 1
Alexander: so the diagonals are never touched
Alexander: which means there are no rays at +45 degrees or -45 degrees, they just get closer to that at higher cascades
Yaazarai: I think the HRC paper also specifies doing that.... But I never got it to work.
Yaazarai: It always broke my frustum edges.
Yaazarai: Hmmmmm, I'm definitely breaking something then.
Alexander: the second order effect is that you need to weight frustums during merging proportionally to their atan instead of their tan, but that's a second order effect
Alexander: you don't need to do that to get rid of the glaring cross
Yaazarai: How am I supposed to get rid of it then?
Alexander: the cross is not supposed to be there at all if you use half integer tangent rays
Yaazarai: The HRC paper uses half-integer rays and its artifacts are worse than mine.
Yaazarai: At least IIRC, obviously I'm not the best source of that.
Alexander: RadianceCausality is also in the pixel bog
Yaazarai: Indeed. He didn't test in-motion artifacts for the paper though.
Alexander: note how here i have the square distribution instead of a circle because i weight my cones equally instead of using atan's, but there are no crosses on diagonals
Alexander: i'll weight them properly, proportional to their solid angle next time if you care about that, to see what it looks like
Alexander: but yes, in 2d if you draw isolines of the brightness profile of a point light source using equal weights with a tangent distribution, you should get squares instead of circles:
Alexander: but note that you should not be getting bright 1-pixel diagonal crosses
Alexander: it's like a 1/r^2 point light, but the r is manhattan distance instead of euclidean distance
Yaazarai: Its an artifact of HRC extension formulation--extending from c3. Left is HRC per paper, right is my formulation--extending from c0 (still has it, but more uniform).
Alexander: left one seems broken to me

AdrianM: I unfortunately don't have the math background to give you all the technical jargon. Give me a second while I attempt to explain what I'm solving here.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Okay so imagine I have some grid of connected pixels
AdrianM: Each node is also connected to 3 imaginary nodes that represent RGB
AdrianM: The weights of each node (excluding the RGB weights) must add up to 1 or less
AdrianM: Awesome, so in this set up we have a 4x4 grid, which is 16 nodes + the 3 imaginary RGB nodes.
AdrianM: So now if we wanted to brute force this we could create a matrix with every node connected
RadianceCausality: can't you do everything without the rgb?
AdrianM: The RGB nodes are crucial. They represent the "endpoints", the connections to them only go in one direction. In other words once we move to an RGB node we can't leave.
RadianceCausality: why the G and B though
but anyways
AdrianM: I can't explain why without going into too much detail.
AdrianM: For now just imagine the above setup I've described
RadianceCausality: okay
AdrianM: Now imagine that we start on one of these pixel nodes, we can imagine doing an infinite number of random walks around the grid to find the probability that we end at each of the RGB nodes
RadianceCausality: okaaaaaaayyyyyyy
already seems weird lmao
AdrianM: The brute force solution would be to either monte-carlo or create a 16x19 matrix and raise it to the power of infinity
Goobley: This sounds vaguely adjacent to walk on spheres but in an hrc framework?
RadianceCausality: can't you just write it as a poisson problem and invert it?
I guess its either fixed point or matrix exponential, hm
yeah those are basically the same (inversion is more general though)
RadianceCausality: anyways yes
AdrianM: Okay, so you will have to correct me if I'm wrong (again I have 0 math background) but my understanding is that there is no way to get a perfect solution other than either a large number of monte-carlo samples or a large number of iterations to approximate it.
RadianceCausality: matrix inversion

AdrianM: Alright, so that's the problem I'm solving. We've defined the kind of diffusion model here we're working with
AdrianM: Okay, I'm going to try to explain this in a simple way...
AdrianM: Oh goodness, this feels like such a stupidly simple idea it barely has any right to work. But it seems to work...
AdrianM: Okay, so we have a grid like so
AdrianM: [image]
Alexander: how does it compare to a simple diffusion solver?
AdrianM: I need to compare it to jacobi still
Alexander: because converging to a diffusion solution isn't exactly hard, it's hard to do it fast
Alexander: unless i'm missing something
AdrianM: I will upload the code later
AdrianM: For the examples I've shown it's easily 30fps+
AdrianM: The scaling seems to be about n^3 for an n*n grid
AdrianM: If we ignore the parallel cost then its bascially n interations per pixel
AdrianM: So for a 512x512 grid we can get a perfect solution in 512 iterations
AdrianM: I say iterations, as you'll see these aren't really iterations. It's a different process.
AdrianM: Okay, back to explanation
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: So imagine like the image above we have everything locally connected
AdrianM: So each box represents an area where all the nodes are connected. Basically a n*n matrix
AdrianM: The basic idea is, what if we took some of the nodes and just deleted them and redistrubuted their weights
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Then we can just repeat that process, over and over as a "merge up" pass.

AdrianM: I've taken a quick look at this paper. Are they just diffusing the pressure and then using the pressure gradient to update the velocities?
AdrianM: Cause I had a similar thought but it didn't quite work and I'm going to attempt to explain why:
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Imagine we have a closed pipe with a few grid cells. One of the cells is high pressure and another one is equally low pressure
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: If we diffuse the pressure gradient we eventually get this
AdrianM: Which we can now use this pressure gradient to update or fluid velocities
AdrianM: Simple enough.
AdrianM: But it seems to me that this doesn't quite work, because what if we have multiple cells with different pressures, then wouldn't they prevent the diffusion through them?
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: So for example, this case I've set the far left cell to be an even higher pressure
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: And if I diffuse the pressure across it then it can't diffuse through the second high pressure value
AdrianM: We could instead have each pressurized pixel add to the already accumulated value. But then if the net pressure is greater than 0 it will increase without limit.
AdrianM: The other alternative I see is to have the pressure values as initial conditions instead of constraints. But then everything will just diffuse to a perfectly smooth pressure gradient with the average pressure of the system.
AdrianM: This paper talks about using poisson equation for fluid simulation but I don't quite see how that's possible.
Goobley: By a process called projection (technically  Hodge decomposition/Chorin projection), they are making the velocity field divergence-free, which is a requirement of incompressible fluid dynamics. The divergence of the velocity can be removed by subtracting the gradient of a scalar field (the pressure) that satisfies the Poisson equation with source the divergence of velocity.

The divergence being zero means that the same amount of fluid enters and exists each volume element: I.e. incompressibility. Projection is a way to get to the minimum difference between your predicted field, and a divergence free one.

In this, the diffusion equation isn’t used for the actual simulation of fluids (which is a hyperbolic conservation law, rather than a parabolic diffusion), but a sub-step of process.
AdrianM: Hmmmm, thank you
AdrianM: Wait, no that still doesn't answer my question. I know you use the velocity field to generate the pressure values, and then you can diffuse the pressure values to get a corrective gradient.
Goobley: The poisson solution (diffusion step), is a global process. That's why it's so expensive in these algorithms
Goobley: So it can effectively diffuse through regions of high pressure
AdrianM: That's what I'm not quite sure about
AdrianM: If we set the pressure value for a cell then that also blocks other pressure values from diffusing through that cell
AdrianM: If we make the cell add to the pressure value then if the negative and positive pressures aren't perfectly balanced then the pressure will increase.
RadianceCausality: or you can have nonreflecting boundary conditions
Goobley: The Poisson solution is global, so it will modify the pressure everywhere in the grid to solve for it
AdrianM: If we set the pressure value once as an initial condition then it is going to diffuse and average out so there is no gradient
AdrianM: Well yeah. But I'm specifically talking about the case where all the boundaries enclosing the fluid are reflective
RadianceCausality: just don't do that then
Goobley: The source on the diffusion is the divergence of the velocity
AdrianM: Correct
AdrianM: Hmmm I guess if within an enclosed space the net pressure for an incompressible fluid doesn't balance out to 1 maybe it makes sense that the forces would be infinite.
AdrianM: Okay, I might just have to plug things in and see what it does
AdrianM: Actually should be relatively simple based on what I already have
Goobley: Yeah, for an incompressible fluid, the pressure in the domain needs to balance
Goobley: Which is weird
Goobley: Well, it feels weird to me
Goobley: But all my fluids are compressible gas/plasma

kuba: What's the difference between holographic rc and other rc?
Yaazarai: (Left) RC casts uniformly distributed rays/probes and utilizes spatial interpolation to connect cascades.

(Right) HRC casts rays/probes in planes and rays perfectly connect between planes/cascades.
Yaazarai: This some caveats in there, but close enough.
JoshJers: @kuba Adding to this: the HRC diagram (the one on the right) represents one 90 degree chunk of the rendering (probes pointing upwards), there's 3 other passes for left/right/down. 
But the effect of how this works is that, where classic RC loses probe density more-or-less uniformly along both axes for each cascade (Trading it for higher angular resolution), HRC only loses probe density along the predominant ray direction axis (i.e. for rays going mostly up/down it has half the probe density, but twice the angular resolution). On the axis perpendicular to the predominant ray travel (x, in the diagram) it keeps full probe density (per-pixel, likely) which is what enables it to have sharper shadows
JoshJers: Basically HRC's upsides are: much sharper shadows where they make sense, no lightsource ringing that base RC has that needs things like bilinear fix to adjust for
Downsides: for small light sources there are some unfortunate artifacts (although @Yaazarai's implementation has way less than what mine did and I haven't been able to get back to working on it to figure out what I did wrong) and it takes more memory (especially if you're doing "extensions" which is a clever alternative to ray casting but requires you store effectively all cascades' ray information at once vs, RC where you can ping-pong)
JoshJers: also to be clear: regular RC doesn't really do any better for tiny light sources, it's just that the artifacts tend to be blurrier than they are with HRC
Alexander: the difference is that for flatland, vanilla RC stores a 3d radiance field, HRC stores 2d radiance field
Alexander: since it's 1 dimension less, it allows storing it with much higher resolution
Alexander: or at the very least that's what HRC is trying to exploit, the fact that in the empty space the radiance field has one less dimension, because it's constant along straight paths
Yaazarai: Don't worry about it just use mine.
JoshJers: Haha I don't work that way 😁
JoshJers: To my own detriment
Yaazarai: Also you only need to store one frustum (1/4th total rays), not all rays.... for Vanilla HRC.
JoshJers: Right but you need all the cascades for extensions
Yaazarai: Ah, right right.

Yaazarai: In basically every case since nothing lines up.
kuba: I've been reading this chat a bit and I saw some references to reducing the problem to sorting in 1D. Is the general idea:

If I'm computing at RC0, I'm going to have a bunch of short rays going to the top-left which connect to one another. Instead of computing them all independently, I could compute a single ray for the full length of them which passes through opaque objects and marks that it's hit them.

By doing so, instead of having to compute a number of rays equal to the number of probes on the line, I can limit the number of rays to the number of triangles on that line. The memory used can also be limited to the number of triangles on the line, and I can derive the radiance at that level (in that direction) by finding the probe's point on the line and checking the distance to the next triangle. 

This technique works for all levels, but has diminishing returns as you have fewer probes per level, and so at some point, the number of triangles on the line will exceed the number of probes on the line. But this seems pretty cost-effective for lower levels, where you have sparse ray angles and dense probes.
Yaazarai: Regarding this... I think I can just assume some imaginary plane behind me (because we can't see what's actually there) and just interpolate with a ray of 0.0 radiance and 1.0 transmittance.
Yaazarai: Which I think makes sense..m since as the sampling point gets further away its should have less contribution to the nearest plane in front of it.

Where as to respect occlusion we can't interpolate with the plane behind the sample point because it can't be seen.
Yaazarai: In which case if that works I think I can wrap this up.
Yaazarai: If it works first for merging, then it'll 100% work for ray extensions. Since they're the same process (merging rays vs cones).
AdrianM: Ah, so the cool thing about HRC is we can actually use the rays from the previous cascade to construct the rays of the next cascade. This "merge up" pass actually allows us to construct all of the cascades without doing any ray tracing at all and is way faster, though it requires more memory.

AdrianM: On a tangent, it may be possible to reduce artifacts if we could find some clever way to "subtract off" the error while building the cascades. I was taking inspiration from Strassen's Algorithm for matrix multiplication that basically speeds up matrix multiplication by chunking calculations together into shared blocks which then are arranged to subtract off the error later.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strassen_algorithm

AdrianM: I can do this, the other option is to allow drawing while the simulation is paused
AdrianM: If I get some time this weekend I'll do a few enhancements
Yaazarai: Anyways, amazing demo. You're a genius.
AdrianM: The idea is stupid simple
AdrianM: Just create a bunch of matrixes of connected nodes. Remove each node that isn't on the edge, multiplying and redistributing the weights to all the other nodes. Then merge the matrix block with the one next to it, rinse and repeat
AdrianM: The merge down pass is even simpler, just take all the weights of the nodes you removed and multiply them against the edge values to get their values. Now you can do that with the next cascade.
AdrianM: The mind blowing realisation for me was that I could correct for the self-weights for the node I'm removing by multiplying all the outgoing connection weights by the infinite geometric sum of the self-weight.
AdrianM: Which is basically why this thing can solve for how the system will look after infinite time steps without having to iterate anything. Because that sum of a geometric series lets us calculate infinity.
Youssef Af: I just found out that my gpu isnt supported in chrome, i switched to brave
tmpvar: write it once they said, it'll run everywhere they said
Yaazarai: Get a real GPU.
tmpvar: works fine on my machine btw
Alexander: i wonder if this can also be applied to the fluid equations..
Youssef Af: ooooo
Youssef Af: it's a real one, i guess
AdrianM: Oh I think that if we used this to diffuse pressure values then we could create a perfect incompressible fluid (at least if we pretend each time step is infinitely small)
Alexander: wait hmm this could be used for pressure solve
AdrianM: I don't think it'd even be much work
AdrianM: As I understand it if we have the pressure add to the sum without absorbing anything then it should work. That's already supported by the simulation.
Alexander: in 3d the cost just to store the last matrix would be O(w^4)
AdrianM: In 3D the cost to run this terrifies me
Alexander: 2d being convenient strikes again
AdrianM: That basically sums up everything related to HRC
AdrianM: I personally don't have much interest or use for fluid simulation, but it should in theory be possible to use this for fluid sim
Alexander: for fluid sim O(N^3) is a bit much, but are you sure it's actually O(N^3)?
Alexander: isn't this O(N^2)?
AdrianM: We have an N*N grid
AdrianM: And we need to run 2*N shader passes to remove the nodes on the merge up pass
AdrianM: So that is a scaling of N^3, no way around it.
Alexander: why 2*N? why is it not log?
Alexander: every merge up pass should merge 2x2 squares into 1 square
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: We have something like so
AdrianM: We want to remove all the middle nodes
AdrianM: That means for each middle node we remove we need to redistribute the weights to all the edge nodes which it could be connected to
AdrianM: [image]
Alexander: there's total of O(N) boundary nodes, each boundary node can affect each other O(N) boundary nodes and it can affect it through any of the O(N) internal nodes, is this where N^3 is coming from?
AdrianM: No way around it
AdrianM: So basically when we remove a single node the cost of redistributing the weight is effectively N^2
AdrianM: [image]
Alexander: that's a bit much for 2d fluid sim and far too much for 3d
AdrianM: I'm confused how fluid sim can be N^2 normally, at least for incompressible fluids
Alexander: how? approximately
AdrianM: In theory if I have an incompressible fluid and I pump it through a maze it can solve that maze instantly
Alexander: nobody solves the poisson equation exactly, people just iterate a couple iterations every time step
AdrianM: Ah yeah, that isn't perfectly incompressible then. An approximation
Alexander: so over time it kind of mostly converges. most of the time.
Alexander: yeah the only realistic truly incompressible solution is without boundaries with fft. but then the error is mostly dominated by the advection step anyway
AdrianM: Well, we could use this to make a truely incompressible fluid sim
AdrianM: But yeah, n^3
AdrianM: I still count this as a win though
Alexander: what if instead of removing nodes we maybe joined them together 2->1? then after log N joins it'd be 1
Alexander: the part where you just iterate over all nodes and remove each one independently sounds kind of wasteful
AdrianM: I'm trying to picture how that would work. I can see how that would be log N
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: So here is the worst case for a single cascade
AdrianM: You have something like a spiral
AdrianM: In this case if we try to iterate even on the level of the cascade we just get stuck waiting forever
AdrianM: Which is why I decided to just sequentially remove one node at a time until it is resolved.
AdrianM: I thought about doing pairs of nodes together, but you don't know the order that is might matter or what might be dependant on what
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: This is another really bad case
AdrianM: In this case two pixels/nodes can be almost right next to eachother and yet the paths connected to them can be terrifyingly different
Alexander: the way AMG works is if you have a bunch of points p[i], you can create twice fewer new points `q[j] = (p[2 * j] + p[2 * j + 1]) / 2`. then, knowing your matrix Ap that governs p, you can calculate what the matrix for Aq has to be, and that matrix will be 2x2 smaller. then, if you have the exact solution for q, you can more efficiently calculate the exact solution for p.
AdrianM: Can AMG solve for if semi transparency?
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: For example, if I made the maze walls here a little bit transclucent
Alexander: amg is usually formulated for approximate solutions and iterative methods. but actually nothing should prevent one from using it to solve exactly, if you can find a way to use the exact solution of q to get the exact solution for p
Alexander: what you're doing is very close to AMG
Alexander: in some sense it is AMG, but not the way people usually use it
Alexander: because you can formulate AMG in infinitely many ways, since the entire projection step is completely arbitrary
Alexander: usually people group 2x2 adjacent nodes into 1 in 2d AMG. but what you're doing is you're grouping squares of boundary nodes into larger squares of boundary nodes. this can be though of as AMG, but i don't think anybody did that specific way of grouping nodes between cascades
Alexander: anyway i know it's unpleasant to think that somebody did something similar to what you're doing, and it kind of can steer you away in a known direction rather than feeling you're discovering something unknown. but also they have a bunch of pretty clever stuff there that can be possibly useful, like guaranteed log time.
AdrianM: If we get log time at the cost of an inexact solution then there's not really any advantage over existing methods
Alexander: well that's my point, maybe if you can find a way to use the exact solution on a higher cascade to get the exact solution on the lower cascade, then AMG will be exact
AdrianM: That would be insane if that's possible
Alexander: it's just there's this whole thing about AMG about smoothers and projectors (whatever they're called), TL;DR is that AMG is provably particularly efficient with iterative relaxation solvers. because iterative solvers usually suck at propagating influence over large distances, but in AMG higher cascades iterative solvers are particularly good at compensating for exactly that : covering large propagation distances in a couple iterations. that's why people love to couple AMG with inexact iterative relaxation solvers, because they by design couple particularly well
AdrianM: I can't see it being possible the merge the middle nodes without removing each one independantly. Consider the case where we have a bunch of horizontal tubes. In this case each node on the right connects to one middle node and will connect ultimately to one node on the left.
AdrianM: If we merge any two of the middle nodes together in any way then we lose that 1-to-1 mapping in this case
Alexander: yes, but you don't lose them, you find what their average is. by doing `q[j] = (p[2 * j] + p[2 * j + 1]) / 2`, you construct a system that you can solve to find out what left plus right node is equal to. if you can use that knowledge to then find what the exact left and the right nodes are, provided you now know their exact average, you win
Alexander: but if you say "knowing their exact average can't help me", then you lose
AdrianM: Like with HRC, the merge up pass we aren't solving for the values, we are calculating the connection weights for the next cascade
Alexander: yeah, that's different because HRC never introduces new values if you look at it this way
Alexander: but it doesn't mean introducing new values can't be useful
Alexander: HRC keeps removing values from the system until you're only left with a few values that you know.  AMG continually joins values that you don't know together until you're left with just a couple values that you can solve exactly.
AdrianM: Interesting, I might have to look into this further

Alexander: that's not how AMG works. AMG does solve a simpler problem on every subsequent level, but then gives you a way to use that solution to construct a solution for a harder problem on the previous level
RadianceCausality: Yeah...
I guess that can't combine then
Alexander: you can also keep the dimensionality of your problem constant if you want on every level, but i think then it will result in literally the same thing adrian did
Alexander: with the same speed and the same result
RadianceCausality: Yea
Alexander: i think his algorithm is AMG, where the projection operator drops half of the sparse variables on every iteration and connects the remaining ones
Alexander: the choice of the projection operator is completely arbitrary in AMG
Alexander: variables on level N+1 is some linear combination of variables on level N
Alexander: and dropping half of them is one way to make a linear combination

Yaazarai: I use a strict per-pixel path tracer--not DDA. Iterate N pixels over the primary axis, then fractionally over the secondary axis and round to the nearest pixel. Which means regardless of angle every line has the same path length and sample count.
```
vec2 delta = dxy / max(abs(dxy.x), abs(dxy.y));
for(float ii = 0.0; ii < max(worldExt.x, worldExt.y); ii += stepSize) {
    vec2 ray = floor(rxy + (delta * ii)) / worldExt;
    if (floor(ray) != vec2(0.0)) break;
    ...
}
```

Alexander: there must be a compact form of what this process converges to if there are infinitely many rays
Alexander: because it literally operates on something like 4 values total. there's only so many ways you could combine them
Alexander: basically this problem: rays 1, 2, 3 and 4 are known, the goal is to interpolate ray marked with the question mark.
Alexander: there has to be a fast way to do this, because only 4 values can contribute to it
Yaazarai: Ah yes, this problem is also in the Secret Life of Photons: https://benedikt-bitterli.me/tantalum/
Yaazarai: TL;DR is to multiply the radiance of each ray-segment before merging by the ratio of the length of each rasterized ray by the actual length of the ray.
Yaazarai: So something like: `radiance *= length(probe, merge) / interval`.
Yaazarai: Where the `probe` is the current XY ray/probe position and the `merge` is the probe you merge with in the next plane over and `interval` is the fixed rasterized length of each ray at that cascade.

AdrianM: This is a bit of a trap. I don't believe it is possible to merge down without creating error. At least not interpolating from two probes
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Here we can see the default merge pattern
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: And here we can see if we offset by one tile the new merge pattern
AdrianM: We can't create this pattern by interpolating between probes using the default merge pattern
AdrianM: I have a solution to this problem but I'm still working through the details.
AdrianM: In a nut shell, we can create different types of merges which have different associated patterns. Then we can set up an L-System to build the unique merge patterns we need for each pixel.
AdrianM: I've got that part solved. I have an L-System  rule set that can do this with 3 states, I'm working on finding a way to generate the exact indexes now.
AdrianM: It might require setting up 2 additional states which would bring the total to 5. This means that the active cascade will cost 3-5 times as much to calculate and require an equal increase in memory. But it should completely solve down merging.
AdrianM: The up merging also introduces its own error. I have a simple solution to that too.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: The final major source of error is that equally spaced probes on a line should not have equal contributions. In the ground truth the rays are spaced equally around a circle instead of along a line
AdrianM: I don't have a solution to this. Perhaps I'll give it more thought if the first two sources of error can be eliminated
AdrianM: We're still going to have a little bit of error due to discretizing the rays. And the L-System may introduce minor biases where it has to chose where to add/remove a ray all things being equal. But overall I think we can get the error really close to 0

AdrianM: Alright, fixed it by adding an additional 2 L-system states for the +/- offset states
AdrianM: That brings the total to 9 states. We can probably optimize a little, the even/odd lines use different rules so we may not need to store all 9 states for each probe.
AdrianM: But hey, this should almost completely remove the error from down merging at a cost of x9 memory and compute.
AdrianM: We can use a pretty similar trick to remove the error from the up merge too, but that will only be x2 the cost since we only need two states to correct the error there.
AdrianM: I want to throw in one additional note. We only need 3 states to generate the correct number of rays. The additional states are required to get the correct y coordinate and angle indexes for those rays. But fundamentally the substitution rules are still the same. Think of these additional states as variants on the original 3 that don't change the behavior but help to pass through information required for correct indexing.

AdrianM: If you give me a minute I can draft up a diagram and explain a very simple solution to the merge up pass
Alexander: idk what you call "merge up"
AdrianM: I think "ray extensions" is the term you use to describe combining shorter rays to construct the longer ones
AdrianM: .
AdrianM: ^ This is what we currently have ^
---
⌄ This is what I'm proposing ⌄
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Alright, so we have some arbitary ray. We want to construct it using smaller rays so we don't have to do any ray tracing
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: The way we do this currently is something like this. We take the rays from the previous cascade and combine them 50/50 to get approximate the new ray.
AdrianM: But what if instead of combining them we split it into two different options. A left and right bias. (in this diagram red and blue)
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Let's look at the right biased ray
AdrianM: Now we want to construct these two smaller rays from even smaller rays.
AdrianM: The top blue ray already aligns perfectly with the smaller rays we'd need to construct it, so we can just split it in half.
AdrianM: The bottom blue ray we now construct while inverting the bias
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Now we can do the same for these new rays: If it lines up then keep the bias as is and use the two rays that exactly line up. If it doesn't line up then invert the bias and construct using the two closest rays.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: And there you have it, with each merge the error remains less than 1 pixel.
AdrianM: It comes at the cost of doubling the cost of the merge up pass, but I suspect the increase in precision is worth it.
Alexander: so that is running with extensions instead of raymarching then?
AdrianM: The demo I'm showing is using ray marching.
AdrianM: I haven't implemented the solution for the merge up pass that I've just described yet.
Alexander: so it sounds like brezenham/dda liner rendering
Alexander: where you store a floating point "error" state, and whenever that error exceeds 1, you move to the next pixel
Alexander: but instead you move to the next angle
Alexander: so your goal is to keep a long interval as straight as possible by adding up all errors that contribute to it
AdrianM: That's one way to look at it. The nice thing is we can do it recursively
Alexander: well i'm less interested in extensions and more interested in gathering
Alexander: i guess what you call merge down
Alexander: how does that work?
Alexander: Pinned a message.
AdrianM: Yeah, that's the hard problem. Which is why the current demo I'm playing with is using ray marching instead of ray extensions
Alexander: honestly, yeah this is kind of genius
AdrianM: Thank you
Alexander: well even with ray marching how do you merge things when you gather?
AdrianM: The short answer is I construct a set of merge rules that always results in the correct number or rays at each distance without any overlap or gaps
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: I posted this earlier, this is what that looks like visually
Alexander: yeah idk how to interpret it

Alexander: btw, how exactly do you do merging in this case?
AdrianM: In the default case literally each white ray just merges with the next two white rays.
Alexander: here do you merge B with (A+C)/2 or do you merge B with just C?
Alexander: because B is aligned with C, so adding A into the mix looks like it will skew the result
AdrianM: Okay so that's what I was talking about how there are 3 states in my L-System but I have to create variants of those states to get the correct indexes
AdrianM: So here I call the default white merge type "a"

Alexander: hm i see

Alexander: so your overall error will look like this:
1/2-1/4+1/8-1/16+1/32-...=0

Alexander: Pinned a message.
AdrianM: Yes, the default case is trivial
AdrianM: It's the even merges that complicate things
Alexander: what do you call even vs odd merge?
AdrianM: If the merge is "odd" then it needs to ray trace to the next cascade. If it is "even" then it is already at the boundary so it doesn't move, it just merges rays at the probe it is already on.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: [image]
Alexander: so you think even case is more complicated?

Alexander: isn't even just an average of 2 cN+1 rays?

AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Let's only consider up to the blue line
AdrianM: Each probe should only be hit a single time, and the total number of probes hit should be equal to the horizontal distance to those final probes
AdrianM: That's pretty obvious since we are drawing a cone with a 45 degree angle
AdrianM: But this isn't possible with the default merge type we discussed
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: As you can see the cones that result from the default merge pattern will have overlap no matter how we interpolate them
Alexander: right i guess you're right hmm..
AdrianM: If we think of the a-type as resulting in 2^n nodes, where n is the number of cascades it has been doubled by. Then we need a merge type that results in (2^n-1)
Alexander: so you seem to think the other way round.. when you gather influence, you want to gather each point exactly once?..
AdrianM: Enter the "b-type" merge
AdrianM: This pattern will result in 2^n-1
AdrianM: So each time we turn an a-type into a b-type it is the equivalent of subtracting off one

Alexander: one thing i'm curious, is should the source appear exactly once in the merge tree, or should its total weight add up to 1? i actually bet the important part is that its weight is supposed to add up to 1

Alexander: that's literally the thing that determines what a single point light will produce
