AdrianM: This means we just have two merge types now
AdrianM: A-type which is the typical branching pattern that doubles each time
AdrianM: And now B-type which is just a single ray that doesn't double.
AdrianM: @Alexander
AdrianM: Basically we just do the merging the same for even and odd cascades, both will branch, but even cascades don't change the position. Then on even cascades we also subtract off a B-type ray when we merge the two A-types.
AdrianM: Surprisingly simple
Alexander: how do you know which ray to subtract?
Alexander: i wonder how this generalizes to cases where we have arbitrary direction count scaling and arbitrary probe spacing scaling
Alexander: i feel like by trying to generalize it to those cases, we might come up with a more compact explanation of how it needs to work
AdrianM: If I do the merge normally, branching it in two, then on an even merge we get an overlap
AdrianM: So I subtract off the ray that will overlap
AdrianM: So if I am about to merge a+ and a-
AdrianM: Then I can subtract off either b+ or b-
AdrianM: Doesn't matter which
AdrianM: Of course since b+ and b- will resolve to b (no bias) in the next cascade we can just grab the ray directly skipping one cascade if we want. Then we only need 1 variant of b
Alexander: i feel like there's so many ways to implement it that can work.. i want to come up with a way to formulate general energy-conserving HRC that all produce an exact 1/r^2 falloff for a point light. i feel like only now i realize what the conditions for that are
AdrianM: So basically we can do the merge the normal way we already are, but subtract off a ray each time we merge two rays on an even merge
Alexander: currently you use integer rays. i want to have a way to do this with half-integer rays too
AdrianM: Is that so you can vary the scaling and possibly make it scale for 3D?
Alexander: yup
Alexander: there has to be a way to do this
Alexander: i think it has to be arithmetic
Alexander: something along the lines of "the sum of all directional contributions for every probe has to be a constant"
Alexander: or something along those lines
Alexander: and geometrical considerations is a secondary factor that we'll need to satisfy if we want to create shadows that are straight
Alexander: but it's not a given and it should be energy conserving even if shadows are all wonky and not straight
Alexander: i want to focus on its arithmetical/algebraic part, which should be just a condition that tells you whether a given merge pattern is energy conserving or not
Alexander: i also realized that energy conserving gathering is not trivial at all _between lines_. but _on the lines_ it's trivial
Alexander: but lower cascades lines are spaced more and more frequently, so gathering from c0 should be trivial
Alexander: i think it should be possible to create penumbras even for a 1-px light and 1-px occluder and still keep them energy conserving
Alexander: but to achieve that, we should be enforcing the condition "contribution of all paths should add up to 1..." instead of "there should be a single path..."
Alexander: in some sense your single path condition is like nearest interpolation, that's why it produces a hard shadow, but there can be also a formulation with linear interpolation

Alexander: are people now going to pretend to not see the difference between iterative and analytic solvers?
Alexander: analytic solvers give you the exact answer after some finite calculation time, iterative solvers will only get you closer to the answer the more iterations you do, no matter how fast they converge. the fact that computers operate on floats is completely irrelevant from analytical point of view
Alexander: so yeah even if it's not as fast as FFT, i see adrian's approach having some pretty remarkable properties
Misha: yes, because from a practical point of view - it doesn't matter
Misha: especially since we are talking about discretizations of space (and time), so its not even representing the "true" underlying solution

Misha: yes, but even with the same spatial discretization there are different definitions for a solution, if you use different finite difference stencils, the exact solution is different for those, and for FFT its different still, since its "stencil" goes over the entire field (i'd argue FFT would be more accurate here)
Alexander: yes, but for a given stencil there is a unique solution that satisfies it

Alexander: whenever he removes one node, he redistributes all of its influences to every node remaining in the system, losslessly
Misha: what does redistribution imply here?
Alexander: gaussian elimination
Alexander: if A connects to C via B, you can remove B, but strengthen the link between A and C.

Alexander: Pinned a message.

Alexander: pinned some of our previous 3d work that's too hard to find otherwise
Misha: apparently there is something similar done way back in 1973
> Abstract. Let M be a mesh consisting of n squares called elements, formed by subdividing the
> unit square (0, 1) x (0, l) into n small squares of side l/h, and having a node at each of the (n + 1)
> grid points. With M we associate the N x N symmetric positive definite system Ax b, where
> N (n + 1)2, each x is associated with a node of M, and Ai 0 if and only if x and x are associated
> with nodes of the same element. If we solve the equations via the standard symmetric factorization of
> A, then O(n4) arithmetic operations are required if the usual row by row (banded) numbering scheme
> is used, and the storage required is O(n3). In this paper we present an unusual numbering of the mesh
> (unknowns) and show that if we avoid operating on zeros, the LDLT factorization of A can becomputed
> using the same standard algorithm in O(n3) arithmetic operations. Furthermore, the storage required
> is only O(n log n). Finally, we prove that all orderings of the mesh must yield an operation count of
> at least O(na), provided we use the standard factorization algorithm.

Alexander: i feel like the source of error happens because we rely on geometry instead of combinatorics when we put an obstacle between cascade planes:
AdrianM: "combinatorics" that's a fancy word
Alexander: here a green obstacle is intersecting how many intervals? 1? shouldn't it be 2? shouldn't it be always divisible by 3? but if we were to place that occluder exactly on the line, there's no such ambiguity
Alexander: well, my point is it seems to work well when we abstract the geometrical part of it and treat it as merge diagrams
Alexander: or L-systems
Alexander: merge diagrams or L-system don't deal with partial occlusions
Alexander: so the way to rely on merge diagrams and L-systems entirely is to remove any geometry and do everything with merge diagrams and L-systems with extensions/merge up pass
AdrianM: I think we are on the same page just phrasing it differently
AdrianM: The issue is the discretization of the rays/merging can't capture partial occlusions / gradients
Alexander: yes. so we should never have partial occlusion at all
Alexander: instead, we have only full occlusion on c0 and propagate that full occlusion up the cascade chain
Alexander: that will result in effectively partial occlusion, but it's easier to control and make it energy conserving
Alexander: so the same analysis how you did that there should be a single path from the source to every point of every line during merge down, there should be a similar analysis how there should be a single path during merge up
AdrianM: Oh, I see what you are saying
Alexander: and that way we don't have any geometry anywhere at all, it's all just arithmetic/combinatorics

Alexander: so one thing i realized is that if a source is exactly on the line of cascade N, then if you calculate the fluence from that source it creates on the nearest line of lower cascades, it increases exactly as 2^N:
Alexander: you can represent 1/2^N falloff exactly with arithmetic, because that's also how the number of directions that we store per probe changes, cince cN-1 probe has 2x fewer directions than a cN probe

Yaazarai: Okay so I have an idea? When extending 2x on even planes we create a gap, even with interpolation (image #1, green is cN we want to interpolate for, aqua/blue is extending to cN+1). Which causes the aliasing difference. So just drop the interpolation in favor of extending directly?

What you can do is assume that any even plane of cN is just a plane of cN+1, where we instead extend using c1 and merge with c1 for 1/2 as many cones and the other half are single ray merges (yellow rays, image #2).

Except that when you merge your cN cones you have to average them with the merged ray that runs through it (dark blue rays, image #2). Then when you merge odd planes with even planes they extend perfectly, but 2x further (image #3).

This does mean that to produce N^2 cones you need 2(N^2) memory.

I think Josh proposed a similar idea once, where we cast an extra ray up the middle of every cone, which didn't work... But  I think that's because it should only happen on even planes. This also lines up with Adrian's diagram.
Yaazarai: Problem is that this would fix the merge diagram, but you still end up with different levels of precision at c0 based on where the c0 probe lands.
Yaazarai: Unless you could weight it out, idk.
Yaazarai: @JoshJers does that look right to you?
JoshJers: I think that falls apart once you go one more level
JoshJers: If I'm understanding right, it's good when you have cN and cN+1 but the moment there's a cN+2 it still doesn't work
JoshJers: But also I might be reading it wrong I am very tired 😅
Yaazarai: Normally yes, but remember that when merging the gap is solved by merging a single full depth ray. So you have two cases:

Odd) merges normal cones left/right. Carries down single rays.
Even) merges 2x distance away and average with a single ray. Carries down single rays.

Here we're merging 2x far away to an odd plane, so merging with cN+1 has the same length. Then after that would be another even plane which would be 2x the length.

Green (odd), Blue (Even), Pink (Odd).
Yaazarai: Yeah I think this will work. It essentially creates a 1 ray gap when merging 2x the distance at even planes and then we manage where that gap gets filled in with the extra rays.
AdrianM: What happens when you hit another even merge?
Yaazarai: That even merge will also be twice as long, so it balances out.
AdrianM: What happens to that darker single ray in the middle
Yaazarai: Never branches.
AdrianM: What happens to it in an even merge?
Yaazarai: Even merges don't merge with those rays.
Yaazarai: They don't need to.
AdrianM: The numbers aren't going to add up
Yaazarai: Wdym?
AdrianM: I mean if you do an even merge with a cascade that is 8 pixels wide, then you need to remove 4 rays from the final total.
AdrianM: In an odd merge you'd go across half the length of that cascade, which would be 4, which means branching to add 4 more rays
AdrianM: But in an even merge you are essentially skipping those 4 units
AdrianM: Here we have the default case again
AdrianM: Now we do an even merge, we have to remove 4 rays
AdrianM: But now imagine we've already done an even merge (say at the start), then it already looks like so:
AdrianM: But now we still need to subtract off 4 rays if we want to skip the same cascade as before
AdrianM: (so a final total of 5 rays removed in this case)
Yaazarai: Not really, because take a look at this: Green (c1), Aqua (c2), Pink (c3) and Yellow (c4).

What happens is that the interval scaling changes drastically:
c1 -> 2^1
c2 -> 2^2 * 2
c3 -> 2^3
c4 -> 2^4 * 2

So when the next cascade odd plane (pink, c3) merges with an even c4 plane the interval length is 4x as long.
Yaazarai: Order here is: odd, even, odd, even.
Yaazarai: Because the intervals of c4 are 2x the intervals of c3, so an even plane of c4 which traces twice that distance is now 4x longer than c3.
Yaazarai: Same thing even if the pink plane was an even plane, it would be 2x as logn as the current even plane.
AdrianM: Could you do the same diagram but with all odd merges?

---
A becomes AA
B becomes B
AdrianM: ---
Even Merge:
---
A becomes ABA
B becomes B
AdrianM: This is the exact same merge pattern as we had before, just instead of subtracing off B on an even merge you are instead sampling from the next line (still in the same cascade) and adding B
AdrianM: The memory and compute cost is the same though
AdrianM: Though this does now mean you have rays which are longer than normal for the cascade. That could screw up ray extensions

Yaazarai: Hmm, I do need to figure out how to modify the weights. I think its safe to just consider the 2x length cones as having the same weight as cN+1 since they'll have the same arc-width when split in half by the midpoint ray.
Yaazarai: But you'd have the split the contribution of the midpoint ray in half between the cones to avoid double counting.
Yaazarai: So something like:
```
left = coneLeft + (midray * 0.5 * cone_weight);
right = coneRight + (midray * 0.5 * cone_weight);
total_radiance = left + right;
```

AdrianM: There shouldnt be any weight issue with @Yaazarai's approach
AdrianM: In fact it shouldn't even require any changes in how the weight it calculated from what I already have
AdrianM: There's probably other ways to do this. But I just start each ray off with a weight of 1 at the highest cascade. Then every time I merge down I add up the weights of all those rays using the alpha channel
AdrianM: That will give you the correct weight for any possible merge pattern
Yaazarai: Wdym? Cant you also just multiply the ray itself by cos(theta) and just add that to the total cone radiance?
AdrianM: Oh yeah, if you want to correct for the falloff
AdrianM: The discussion above you guys were talking about how to handle the fact that some objects might be closer or further away, so blocking a single ray has a larger effect closer
AdrianM: Adding the weights down using the alpha channel is the way to fix that without thinking about it or the merge pattern
Yaazarai: Wdym adding the weights down the alpha channel?

Are you averaging the weights at the end?
AdrianM: Yeah exactly, just divide everything by the final total weight
Yaazarai: Okay thats easy enough.
AdrianM: You could probably calculate it without the alpha channel by considering the merge pattern and how many rays you are expecting for a given coordinate. But doing it this way means it will work for any merge pattern without adjustment, which is better for experimenting
Yaazarai: I'll try the alpha channel for now.. I am currently not using it. Since I have two RGBA textures for per R, G, B radiance and transmittance.
JoshJers: ah, I balanced everything to 1 at each level on the way down
JoshJers: which is simpler to my mind (and doesn't require an extra channel), but maybe there's a downside I didn't factor?
Yaazarai: Hmm idk how that would work per level since in my method each cone only has 1 midpoint ray per cone. So theres nothing to average with?
Yaazarai: Doing it at the bottom makes sense since you can accumulate total ray contribution then average.
JoshJers: oh, yeah, got it. Sorry still waking up, that makes sense 😄

Alexander: i tried some stuff using HRC ray reconstruction and vanilla probe placement. i feel like there's something going on I don't understand
Alexander: this is vanilla probe layout, HRC interval scaling and vanilla radiance reconstruction (just bilinear interpolation of nearest probes) for a small circular light, one cascade is shown
Alexander: this is the same thing but using the HRC way of radiance reconstruction (finding intersection with the nearest line of probes, then doing linear interpolation instead bilinear)
Alexander: i don't think my implementation is bugged, i think it functions how i intend it to. but it also shows that in this case the HRC linear interpolation way of reconstructing radiance produces results that are strictly worse than vanilla bilinear interpolation
Alexander: i think HRC (at least the version with arbitrary probe placement, the one using interpolation) always has these discontinuities, they just happen kind of rarely enough so that they don't matter much
Alexander: but they're there and they are a bug, not a feature
Alexander: i'm thinking now that maybe HRC should also actually have bilinear interpolation, but it should be done in a more careful way to avoid long distance interpolation of disjoined intervals
Alexander: and i think the way to achieve this is actually interpolating _paths_ connected to the two nearest lines. this is actually closer to what bilinear fix does in vanilla, where instead of interpolating radiance of disconnected segments we interpolate connected paths
Alexander: it's kind of handwavy, but i have a feeling there has to be a way to formulate a version of HRC that doesn't have discontinuities and that uses interpolation on both axes to produce a smoother result, while keeping benefits of HRC
Alexander: maybe another reason why people don't see these discontinuities in HRC implementations is because people usually keep probe linear spacing constant in flatland, so those discontinuities always have error smaller than that distance. i definitely saw the same kinds of artifacts in 3d, because it's not an option there to keep probe spacing constant.
Alexander: but yeah TL;DR my new working theory is that long distance interpolation of intervals is bad, but long distance interpolation of connected paths should actually be fine.
RadianceCausality: hm
RadianceCausality: long distance interpolation of connected paths still may result in artifacts though if it isn't balanced
RadianceCausality: but its definitely *better*
Yaazarai: Huh... How exactly are you constructing intervals? If you're trying to reconstruct Vanilla RC rays directly you should be able to get a mostly exact solution?
Yaazarai: My idea was to use HRC ray exstensions to perform a secondary Vanilla RC extensions pass and merge up from c0 from the exact Vanilla RC interval start positions.
Yaazarai: And then use linear interpolation on to get the exact ray length.
Yaazarai: @Alexander doing a second extend up pass would still be faster than ray tracing. Anyways you should be able to do this. Extend the ray up from c0 to cN. Then at cN where the ray ends you interpolate its endpoint from the two nearest planes.

E.g. compute the shorter ray, then compute the longer ray and interpolate.
Yaazarai: And that should provide an exact extension for the rays.
Yaazarai: Rather than trying to reconstruct merged radiance directly.

Alexander: note that the shadertoy code places probes every 4 pixels, but you can paint the SDF arbitrarily, including subpixel precision. so you can place a light source between probes, or a light source with a fractional radius. and it will actually produce a partial occlusion for some of the rays but not others. you can put a light source or an occluder exactly between rays, because the scene is treated as in vector graphics and probes are placed on a grid with discrete positions

tomatopaste: moire pattern?
AdrianM: I'm still waiting to find a use case for moire patterns. They have such interesting properties but I still haven't come across any setting where those properties are useful.
Yaazarai: Wdym intertesting properties?
Alexander: one might say that fft is a moire pattern
Alexander: because the first harmonic behaves exactly like the last harmonic, it's just the last harmonic oscillates so much that at discretely spaced points it looks like it's almost constant
Alexander: like sin(n * 100 * 2pi) behaves exactly like sin(n*0) for all integer n. i kind of see it like a moire pattern
Yaazarai: Almost done with this implementation... Had to add a intermediary pass to merge-down 1x rays as my midpoint rays for my cones. So it ends up being two merge passes (could have done one, but that would be messy....).
Yaazarai: It ups requiring 2x memory for merging only which isn't bad.
Yaazarai: So it ends up being: extend up -> extend down -> merge down
tomatopaste: you’re saying a n*100 hz sin wave looks like a dc value?
Yaazarai: Also all my performance metrics I think are like 50% slower than they should be. GameMaker has to draw/overwrite an image to set its render-area before actually running the shader. So al my shader calls are 2x memory bandwidth lmao.
JoshJers: for any integer `n`, sin(n * 2pi) == 0 because every angle in there is a perfect multiple of 2pi
JoshJers: so yes it looks perfectly like a dc value if you ignore all the non-0 angles 😄
alexander: according to nyquist-shannon theorem, yes.

Yaazarai: @AdrianM adding up rays is more complicated than I thought because it has to factor in occlusion.....
Yaazarai: If my understanding is right:
* For every plane carry down the total single ray contribution count.
* For even planes tick up this count by one for each cone. Also add the single ray radiance to the total cone radiance.
* At c0 divide the total radiance by the total single ray contribution.

Except that obviously you have to factor in occlusion because you can't see every ray that contributes....

So you have to treat the total rays like radiance and merge using transmittance?
Yaazarai: Oh god.
Yaazarai: If thats the case you essentially need to treat it as a second set of RGB values--assuming per-RGB transmittance.
Yaazarai: @JoshJers does that sound right to you?
AdrianM: No. Occlusion doesn't affect the weighting at all.
AdrianM: Whether a ray is occluded early or not doesn't change the arc that it covers/represents in the merge, thus the weight is the same either way
AdrianM: You may have to multiply the occluder's value by the current weight before adding to the total radiance.
AdrianM: But again, this is just one way of doing it which I implemented to make the solution independent of the merge structure (so I can experiment with different merge structures)
AdrianM: Since you know the merge structure in advance you probably dont even need the alpha channel
Yaazarai: The problem is knowing what fraction of the overall cone/arc that ray represents.
Yaazarai: Because it extends from cN to the last cascade without diffusion.
Yaazarai: Okay eyah not sure what the hell I am doing wrong here. Left is Vanilla HRC, middle is Vanilla minus Near Plane Interpolation, Right is Add mid-ray at odd planes.

On the 2nd image (no interpolation) you can actually see the breaks in the fluence where those mid-point rays are missing.

The current method I am using is 3 passes:
1. Extend Up.
2. Extend Down for every ray (minus the last one).
3. Merge Down: even/odd merge strategies.

Number of midpoint rays = number of total cones because you never need the rays at the frustum edge, so I just skip them.

AdrianM: @JoshJers Sounds like you hit a similar issue with ray vs cone weighting. Since you've been thinking about this longer than me I'm curious if you have any ideas how to cut the Gordian knot.
JoshJers: Well, I gave up on the idea like 6 months ago, so I never found a good solution 😐
AdrianM: Any impression on where we might be able to look for a solution?
JoshJers: The only thing I had that really worked(?) was when I figured out the weighting curve sort of empirically and weighted based on distance, but I don't love that solution and it completely falls apart once there's anything with less than 100% opacity
JoshJers: The primary issue (to me, at least) of HRC is with the merging pattern there are either gaps or overlaps in the cones, and I could never find a way to adjust the merging to eliminate that
JoshJers: I had one idea that was effectively doing a parallax mapping of the disjoint cN+1  (to effectively synthesize the view as if it had been taking from a different position) but that again relies on depth information and depth is increasingly a challenge through the merges and with transparencies
AdrianM: Depth information is fine for a single ray but for a cone I don't see a clear way to merge depth values.
JoshJers: if there were a way to somehow get overlapping cones and have a merged *cone* to subtract to even it out, I could see that working
JoshJers: but I couldn't find a pattern for that
JoshJers: there maybe is one and I just didn't find it 😄
AdrianM: This is exactly what I'm thinking but I don't think it is possible for that to exist
JoshJers: oh the other problem is that when doing a disjoint merge the ray origins change
AdrianM: A cone is going to double each merge, but if the final position we are merging into is offset by one then the gap we are trying to fill will always be 1 pixel wide.
AdrianM: disjoint merge?
JoshJers: I forget which is even or odd merges so I've been calling them overlapped/disjoint
JoshJers: the ones where cN and cN+1 don't line up 🙂
AdrianM: Got it
JoshJers: I don't have any of my diagrams handy, but when you merge multiple cN+1s together in HRC the rays from those all originated *at* cN+1, if that makes sense
JoshJers: which is why in the canonical model there are the parallel rays (beside a gap)
JoshJers: those are a source of over/undercounting radiance contribution for things that are close to those origins (the parallax problem)
JoshJers: and that error compounds, which is where most of the streaks in my implementation were coming from
JoshJers: a mismatch in a disjoint merge from cN+4's error propagating and expanding as it merges on down
JoshJers: You can merge from multiple cN+1 probe distances and interpolate to alleviate that somewhat (you have to do it for both overlapped and disjoint merges to get the best effect - the paper mentions doing it specifically for the overlapped ones, I believe) but it doesn't solve it
JoshJers: imo I think this is all a result of taking something that is truly an O(n) problem and trying to make it o(log n) - some corner has to be cut, it's just a matter of finding which cut is the least visibly displeasing 🫠
AdrianM: Thanks for the insight
JoshJers: I'm also willing to bet I'm fundamentally wrong on at least one of those points, so, ya know 😄
Yaazarai: You can only use the interpolation for even planes (overlapped).
Yaazarai: Also even planes are just planes that line up with cN+1.
JoshJers: People keep telling me which are even and odd and I promise I will never remember lol
JoshJers: I don't know why, my brain just refuses
RadianceCausality: the odd ones are even
RadianceCausality: and the even ones are normal
RadianceCausality: I don't see what's confusing
JoshJers: honestly that right there is more likely to make me remember than anything else hah, just make it funny and it'll stick 😄
JoshJers: although I would argue that the numerical parity of the probe row indexing is less important than the way it lines up with N+1, so odd/even aren't the right signifiers <_<
AdrianM: @JoshJers You know there's nothing that says we have to use cascades which double the distance between each line each time
AdrianM: We get to choose how we set up the recursive structure
AdrianM: Perhaps we could choose a different cascade structure that allows for cones to merge nicely for "disjoint" merges
JoshJers: I actually flipped it around once, I made it so that the disjoint ones were the clean merges and the overlapped ones were the ones needing fixup
JoshJers: which resulted in overlaps instead of gaps (so brighter streaks instead of dark ones)
JoshJers: One thought I had was doubling everything, doing both of those, and then averaging
AdrianM: That sounds equivalent to the difference between adding and subtracting the middle ray (the merge structure I am using vs the one @Yaazarai uses)
JoshJers: Yep, it was 😄
JoshJers: except I bet your merge structure is way less of a hack than the one I did
AdrianM: By default we are merging 1,2,4,8,16 etc
AdrianM: I wonder if for disjoint merges we could merge 1,3,6,12,24 etc instead
AdrianM: We can't do it on the existing cascades but nothing is stopping us from adding more lines to our cascades
AdrianM: The important thing is finding a merge pattern that still allows for reductions
AdrianM: Personally I'm not concerned if we need to double or triple the cost of the algorithm as long as the scaling is unaffected
Yaazarai: I don't believe so because the planes scale at the same rate as the rays. So using a higher scaling on rays means they need to be closer together and you immediately end up with even more overlap.

cxii: Okay yeah

Yaazarai: Yeah sorry math doesn't translate well in Discord.

Yaazarai: [image]
cxii: That is very interesting
Yaazarai: The number of rays is 1/2 the width of the frustum the rays span for that cascade.
cxii: So we have

Width / 2^N * Height * (2^N)+1 rays in cascade N
Yaazarai: Also you only calculate radiance in one direction. You then run the shader 4 times passing in the scene rotated to point right for that direction.
Yaazarai: Makes it very very easy.

Yaazarai: No you don't understand.
cxii: It’s because of the way you merge I guess?
Yaazarai: Teh shader only computes 1/4th of the total screen radiance.
Yaazarai: So you have to run the shader 4 times, once for each direction up/down/left/right.
Yaazarai: You can't cast more rays, because again we're halving resolution on one axis in one direction.
cxii: I see yeah that would double the buffer size if you cast on both sides at once
Yaazarai: Its not worth it.
Yaazarai: The point of doing 1/4th is that it simplifies the math for the exact same performance.
Yaazarai: So no point in making it more complicated for no reason.
cxii: Ok I see
Yaazarai: Trust me, I did it.

Yaazarai: I wrote my first variant of HRC frustum-independant.
cxii: But yeah it’s just my intuition that invoking the shader x4 more times would be slower but ig not
Yaazarai: Nope! Because the only difference is a single ping back to the CPU to run the next shader, which doesn't cost anything.
Yaazarai: A lot of different shaders benefit from using multi-pass methods. For example using a 2x pass vertical/horizontal blur is actually much much faster than a single pass blur (uses far less samples).

Yaazarai: Also fyi HRC is memory bound for performance in my implementation using ray-extensions.

Yaazarai: Just a GameMaker thing, not an HRC thing.
cxii: what about gm makes you have to render twice, i've never used it before, downloaded just to run this actually
Yaazarai: The way it works is I render HRC to "surfaces," e.g. a texture/image. In order to do that I have to set the render area within that surface... The only way to do that is to draw directly to the surface.

So it goes: set target -> draw (set render area) -> render shader.
Yaazarai: And the cost of drawing here is basically the same as my HRC implementation to an extent.

ion: Did you only remove the b-rays light contribution, or also its contribution to the weighing scheme?
ion: If I blur the image a bit the banding +- disappears, as if the banding existed to correct for the missing rays and exactly compensates for the gaps in the image.
ion: I came up with some thoughts about b-type merge weighing.
This is endlessly fascinating to me, but i dont have an RC implementation to test my ideas.
I thought I'd post them here in case someone else finds them inspiring or wants to test them.
Using Adrians images from above as a basis.
ion: The weighing of rays in a merge should be proportional to the amount of space represented by this ray.
For example if a ray splits up into two rays in the branching diagram, the space represented by this ray halves, and then as the two sub-rays drift further apart the space they represent increases until the next split.
For example if rays are at most 1 pixel apart then we can say each ray represents at most 1 pixel. Thus right after the split each ray represents 0.5 pixels.
Here I've delineated the regions of space represented by each ray in an a-type merge with yellow lines (for simplicity I'm ignoring the fact that frustum-edge rays might have to be handled differently).
Since in an a-type merge the tree is always perfectly balanced, the weighing isn't actually needed (again ignoring some stuff for simplicity).
ion: Actually in a given merge what we care about ultimately is what angle each of the merge components represents.
Shown here (blue lines) for the two lowest cascade rays.
In the following I'm going to ignore the difference between angle and slope for simplicity.
ion: Here i highlighted one ray and its sub-rays in red.
The angle represented by the ray (blue lines) is the same as that represented by all its subrays together.
But this is only true in an a-type merge.
ion: Here it is shown for a b-type merge.
The angle represented by the b-ray (and its subrays) (blue lines) depends on distance (and thus the same is true for b-merged a-rays too).
If distance has an effect on the angle represented by a ray, then it should have an effect on the weights used in the merge.
ion: How the contributions work in the top left might be open to implementation. I show 3 variants here.
The first variant has the delineations in the middle between the rays, so each location is attributed to the ray closest to it.
The second variant has all three rays carry equal weight (until the branching starts).
The third variant has the nice property that the lines are all straight, which makes it simpler.
I'm going to use the third variant for the following images and formulas.
ion: Note that if all rays hit at that same distance we can compute the combined weight of the a-rays just from the weight of the b-ray for any given distance.
This is the resulting formula (assuming the weights are subsequently normalized):
    a-type merge:
        no weighing (or both rays have same weight)
    b-type merge:
        b-ray: weight 1
        a rays: weight d * x
            where:
                d = hit-distance of b-ray (possibly propagated through multiple cascades)
                x = a constant, same for all b-type merges in this cascade.
                    formula for x might be something like: x = y * 2^(-n)
                        where:
                            y = a global constant (depends on implementation, e.g. on probe density and unit used for distance measurements and cascade numbering scheme)
                            n = number of the cascade in which the merge happens
                                assuming that each cascade changes probeline distance by factor of 2 and its in 2d.
ion: But what if the rays dont hit at the same distance?
When I first had this idea I had a strong feeling that the formula above still holds true, and only the b-rays hit distance is needed for the weight computations. But i couldn't quite put my finger on why.
The following is the best explanation for it that i could come up with:
In an a-type merge the hit distance doenst matter, because the angles represented by each ray (together with its subrays) are independend of the hit distance. As long as all of a rays subrays do hit, their combined weight should be the same no matter where and in which cascade that happens.
This is different in a b-merge: The closer the b-ray hits an object the larger the angle this hit represents. It basically "steals" (covers) angle from the a-rays, but the merging pattern doenst account for this.
The result is that a b-ray hit can cause light from behind the hit object to be merged even thought it shouldn't.
Rest of explanation see images.
ion: I suspect that this "covering effect" of the b-ray, as well as its inverse where a part of the angles remain uncovered because the b-ray hits further then the a-ray, is a case of an artefact that is hardly noticeablle in practice.
Yaazarai: The second image isn't entirely accurate because the angle the b-type ray covers isn't just based on hit distance... Its also based on opacity, even worse is if you have per-RGB transmittance you have 3-sets of opacity.
Yaazarai: Even worse is ray extensions, you can't know hit distance.
cxii: Full disclaimer this is completely yaazarais code un modified, and textures added on in an editor. But yes I will be implementing it myself but just wanted to do a mock up to see what it’d look like
cxii: Very cool thankyou
Yaazarai: We may be able to just treat the ray like a normal cone, you just need to segment the weighting by cascade level. Where the weighting isn't a fraction of the cone at that level, but a fraction of the frustum at that level.
Yaazarai: So single rays should work just like cones--different weights per cascade.
Yaazarai: Just that now the weighting is represented by a fraction of the arc-length of the total frustum.
ion: yeah I wasn't thinking about opacity at all.
Yaazarai: Volumetrics make everything harder.
ion: Wouldn't that introduce up to something like a factor of 2 range of deviation in the weight, depending on how far it hits? And discrete weight change steps when crossing a cascade boundary?
Yaazarai: Yeah.... I think thats why we haven't done it.
Yaazarai: The problem is that cone weights for merges at cascade N add up to the cone width at N-1.
Yaazarai: Which isn't possible on a single ray.
Yaazarai: Hmm you also can't do a separate C-type merge either (all B-type rays only). Due to disjoint contribution with even/odd planes.
ion: Not sure if that came through clearly, but what I had in mind was not add weights up to keep track of them, but rather calculate them from scratch for each merge according to the formula I posted.
ion: I havent heard of c-type merges before. Is there one in the image? Looks like b-a-b-a to me at first sight.
Yaazarai: There is no c-type, just a thought. The A-B is just the 2 even/odd merges, could technically have as many as you want--not that that would necessarily be a good idea.
Yaazarai: Right, but calculating weights based on hit distance isn't a good idea due to opacity issues with volumetrics.
JoshJers: Because HRC calculates the lighting in quadrants (light from up/down/left/right) you can also sample those separately and blend based on the normal to get lighting from specific directions
JoshJers: (I haven't read down to see if anyone mentioned this yet or not)
cxii: Oh that’s actually quite smart
cxii: I wonder if doing that would look better than taking the gradient of the radiance per channel and using that as the direction
cxii: Yaazarai , do you know if your implementation with ray extensions, can work to be influenced by lighting that’s off-screen?
cxii: It has to be real raycasting for this I guess, or at least in the last cascade?
Yaazarai: You have to do it entirely separate from HRC.
Yaazarai: And then merge that into HRC.
cxii: why is that?
Yaazarai: Because ray-extensions work in screen-space based on screen size. So anything that's not on the screen cannot be rendered.

Also my particular implementation is specifically set up to maximize on-screen stability. So trying to swap out the final cascade with ray-tracing will make everything unstable.
Yaazarai: I don't bother with offscreen lights--but I am also working with low-res.
cxii: I see thanks
cxii: So you tried making the last cascade use raycasting and it started messing stuff up?
Yaazarai: Didn't even bother. Its just how the method works. Ray-Extensions diffuse samples between rays and across cascades. So if you swap out those ray-extensions for tracing you lose that effect.
Yaazarai: We had a discussion on how to handle it up here: https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1304556173671927878/1447363898184568883
cxii: Got it,so the only solution in this impl is to make the buffers larger so you can capture stuff off screen?
cxii: Oh nice I’ll read that
Yaazarai: There's plenty of methods... You can do a second low-res render or you can do specific off-screen tracing and merge them with onscreen.
Yaazarai: I specifically am working in low-res so I just do a level 1 mip and render my whole game-world in one pass.

Yaazarai: Ray extensions only work because you can extend across a finite space in texture memory.

Yaazarai: Yes but why.... Just start the rays off-screen.
Yaazarai: It'll cost more in performance too for no reason.
Yaazarai: You'll already have cN rays might as well use them.
cxii: Ok thanks. I will let u know when I get to trying this
AdrianM: Dang I don't believe it is possible to fix the weighting issue for merging cones and rays. Even if we try to be clever with the size of the cones it is always going to be off
JoshJers: that is also the conclusion I came to, but I still hope that it's wrong somehow 😄
JoshJers: more generally, I always think there will be a necessary loss whenever merging incoming radiance from many directions at once, sparsely
AdrianM: If the end point is offset from the default case by n pixels then in the highest cascade the number of rays/weight will be 2^cmax + n
JoshJers: because those many directions always have to have *some* origin, and the "sparsely" nature of it means that you end up interpolating between sets that were incoming from different points, and that will always be inexact
AdrianM: But since the cones double each time we cant express n as a cone
JoshJers: yeah, even trying to pretend it's a narrow cone falls apart because the narrowness relative to the cacade changes every cascade, effectively
AdrianM: I wonder if we can change where the gaps or overlaps appear in the cascades
AdrianM: We could at least make the artifacts random
JoshJers: A thing that I found illustrative is to basically render c1 "perfectly" using ray tracing and then just do the c1 -> c0 merge and see where the gaps are
JoshJers: I could make modifications to the sampling to move the gaps around, but I wasn't ever able to find anything useful to do with that

Yaazarai: @AdrianM can we make this work? This requires 2x the number of rays in each cascade... When you extend your cones you need to half their width, which means double the cones/rays. So instead of `2^N+1` rays you end up with `(2 * (2^N)) + 1` rays.

This squishes B-type rays towards the center so that they never have an infinite length.

AdrianM: Alright, so we should be able to move around the artifacts by random offsets.

AdrianM: We are capable of correcting the gaps perfectly, but not adjusting the weight correctly in a way that is correct for all possible light positions
AdrianM: The thing is, once we have it this state, for each ray we corrected we can fire an additional ray to reintroduce the artifacts
AdrianM: But because the cascade structure has already been corrected by this point to have no overlap or gaps this extra extra ray can be put wherever we want
AdrianM: That should let us choose where the artifacts appear
AdrianM: So in theory we could take something like this and make it so that the artifacts look like completely random noise (or some other pattern that makes it less obvious)
AdrianM: In any case that's where I'm going to leave my exploration for now. The next step is going to be taking the lessons we've learned here and applying it to 3D / screenspace so that this can be more than just a cute toy.
AdrianM: To summarize. We can correct the artifacts in 2D HRC by filling in the gaps in the merge structure (or removing the overlap). But fixing the merge structure doesn't give us a way to calculate the correct weightings for a smooth light falloff.

In fact the weights are not correctly calculated for the existing line-based HRC either but the artifacts if averaged out are what results in the correct falloff. In theory we can intentionally reintroduce these artifacts with random offsets to make the artifacts more like random noise.
AdrianM: We also of course have the option of bluring the result either during the merges, ray marching, or final rendering to smooth out the results. But that doesn't increase accuracy.

AdrianM: ... I have been toying around with the idea of circle based HRC for a screenspace implementation...
Alexander: one thing that we're often losing with line-based HRC is that circles can actually be of arbitrary radius. they can overlap. in line-based representation this corresponds to gathering radiance from lines far away, not from the closest ones
Alexander: maybe there's something there? not sure
AdrianM: " from lines far away, not from the closest ones" Could you elaborate? You lost me at the last sentence
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: you see on the bottom right squares overlap, which is equivalent to using more distance lines for querying radiance, instead of using the nearest ones
AdrianM: This feels like alchemy
Alexander: [image]
Alexander: you know how you can overlap the rings more?
Alexander: the radius of rings is a completely arbitrary parameter, you can have them arbitrarily large or small relative to the distance between the rings
Alexander: and you can do the same with line-based hrc and square-based hrc by querying distant lines to reconstruct radiance
Alexander: usually we use radiance queried at point A to reconstruct radiance for ray P. however, nothing prevents us from querying it from point B, C or D. also, each plane might have an arbitrary "focus point" to where its rays converge, and it can also store intervals at an arbitrary offset
AdrianM: In theory we can place a probe/ray anywhere. We aren't limited to placing them on boundaries of shapes. Though enclosing an area with a holographic boundary is the easiest way to think about this.
Alexander: the HRC paper makes some arbitrary assumptions here that we always use point A, that focal point of every plane matches the plane itself (intervals start at the current plane and end at the next plane), etc. but all those assumptions are quite arbitrary imo.
Alexander: absolutely, that's why i'm visualizing reconstructed radiance for some arbitrary point P
AdrianM: It's a little intimidating to remove some of these assumptions because it opens up the possibility space an insane amount.
Alexander: https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1277053574425350224/1303960956614873098 like here, you stored radiance on boundaries and then reconstructed it everywhere inbetween
Alexander: yes, but i don't know why people always just assume that what we're currently doing is optimal
AdrianM: I am not assuming that at all
AdrianM: Thus why I say this feels like alchemy
Alexander: technically you can use one ring to reconstruct radiance everywhere outside of it
Alexander: at an arbitrary distance
AdrianM: You can do that with any enclosing shape
Alexander: but only enclosing light sources inside of it
Alexander: and those shapes can easily overlap
Alexander: it might be worth doing just to remove seams if anything
AdrianM: With line based HRC the boundary probes use different rays depending on the orientation of the line. So already line based HRC isn't fully enclosing areas with boundaries of probes.
AdrianM: That opens up a lot of questions though. If we can place probes and rays in an infinite number of configurations then how do we narrow it down? (heck, even probes are an abstraction that might not be required)
Alexander: i'm not sure if that's an important property or just an edge case
AdrianM: Yeah, that's kind of what I'm getting at. I don't know which properties are esential for this to work either
Alexander: imo we need to actually compare a couple arrangements and at least heuristically decide which ones work better
Alexander: i think the most important property that we never understood is the interval start offset. in vanilla each probe starts at an offset, that offset scales exponentially with cascade index. in hrc, probes store intervals without an offset, each interval starts at probe position.

i did try in vanilla storing probes without offsets -- it does, and produces objectively worse results (more aliasing). but this is exactly what we've always done in HRC. why?
AdrianM: If you do vanilla *RC* without offsets then doesn't that mean an occlusion placed on the probe will block all of the rays for that probe?
Alexander: yes but you account for that offset during interpolation
Alexander: you just interpolate at a point `p + dir * start_offset` instead of `p`
AdrianM: Oh, like setting an arbitrary offset?
Alexander: HRC stores probes that look like A. vanilla RC stores probes that look like B. storing A probes in vanilla produces worse results than B
Alexander: it is completely arbitrary
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Ignore that the diagram is quickly pulled from google
AdrianM: But pretend the rays don't have any offset and originate from the center of the probes
AdrianM: In this case an object (like the red box) placed at the probe will completely block that entire probe and all of its rays
AdrianM: Without offsets the effect of an object is disproportionately large as it gets closer to the probe, right?
AdrianM: The offsets limit how disproportionate that error can be by not considering anything closer than some arbitrary distance
Alexander: it will block all the rays originating from inside of it. but in order to reconstruct radiance for some arbitrary ray in a given direction, you can query radiance for that direction, but at different points:
Alexander: here `radiance(P, P + dir * inf) = Merge(Merge(Merge(radiance(P, A), radiance(A, B)), radiance(B, C)), radiance(C, C + dir * inf))`
Alexander: you just assemble long intervals from interpolated shorter intervals. where those intervals are stored is completely arbitrary
Alexander: note there are no probes on this diagram at all, it's completely arbitrary. we're just connecting intervals that are stored somewhere
AdrianM: But you have a finite number of those intervals. So if a bunch of them get blocked at their origin then that limits how they can be used later. Although saying it out loud this sounds more like an issue with the intervals not being evenly distributed over the space.
Alexander: imagine there's a blocker at point C. so ray (C, C + dir * inf) gets blocked immediately. this means that ray (P, P + dir * inf) should also be blocked at point C
Alexander: > Although saying it out loud this sounds more like an issue with the intervals not being evenly distributed over the space.
the way you distribute intervals directly affects the accuracy of trying to interpolate them
Alexander: it's unclear how intervals should be stored in order to minimize the error of their reconstruction via interpolation

Youssef Af: I really hope to achieve this
Yaazarai: I think I have a solution... Back to the whole treat it like path tracing thing.

For each cone store its total cumulative rays. When merging multiply each left/right radiance by total cumulative rays. Add B-type rays as needed. For each ray change weighting to cos(theta) rather than cone width. The idea here is instead that each ray represents a segement of all N rays above it.

When collapsing to screen space divide total cumulative radiance by cumulative rays.

That should be a rough approximate of path tracing.
Yaazarai: So instead we would be storing cummulative rather than diffuse radiance.
AdrianM: I don't think that will work
AdrianM: Imagine you do the merge and then there is a solid wall right after that all the rays slam into. In that case it is basically like the merging ends at the next cascade and the weighting should reflect that.
AdrianM: Alternative those could continue to the highest cascade in which case the weighting would be different.
AdrianM: You can use a depth value to solve this for a single light source. But I dont know of a way to solve for multiple light sources simultaneously
Yaazarai: Okay so then multiply the total cumulative rays by transmittance. So that the amount of multiplicative radiance adjusts based on depth.
Yaazarai: Ideally the total number of rays accumulated should be equivalent to transmitted radiance.
Yaazarai: That said it should work even without it, because if you treat each ray as a segement of N rays above it, as long as you accumulate total rays without occlusion properly it should work fine.
AdrianM: Whether a ray hits something or not shouldn't affect the relative weighting of the other rays
Yaazarai: Like I said that when merging you can still accumulate total rays without factoring in occlusion.
Yaazarai: So multiplying radiance based on N rays above should be perfectly applicable.
AdrianM: Alright, well you might just have to demo it and see if it works. I'd love to see it work but I'm cautiously pessimistic.
Yaazarai: I'll see what I can do, also pessimistic....
Yaazarai: I'm doing it without ray extensions to make sure.
AdrianM: That's the right move
AdrianM: I'm going to throw a thought out there: Can we come up with a perfect solution to 2D lighting (without artifacts) if we remove the requirement for shadows/occlusion?
AdrianM: I'm curious if it is something about calculating the falloff of multiple light sources at different distances which is turning out to be the snag. Or if it is something specific to the way HRC is currently structured which we're using to solve occlusion.
AdrianM: Honestly doesn't even have to be a multiscale solution with cascades. I'd be curious to see any perfect solution to this problem.
AdrianM: I think solving it without occlusion could give us valuable insight and more alternative models to play with.
AdrianM: An interesting property of thinking about it this way is that if I cast a ray then the value of that ray will be identical regardless of where along the line I sample.
AdrianM: Two additional notes: first I'm aware that this can be approximated easily, that's basically what a bloom shader does. Second, yes the result of this should be identical to a gravitational simulation, which makes me think perhaps this has already been solved.
Yaazarai: I think the guy who wrote Secret Life of Photons managed to do this with path tracing: https://benedikt-bitterli.me/tantalum/
Yaazarai: He used rasterized lines though.

AdrianM: didn't you have a demo for FFT lighting and shadows?

Alexander: two completely different approaches, both use fft and have different limitations
AdrianM: Wave propagation I've played around with a bit (though not with FFTs). How does wave optics work at a high level overview?
Alexander: it propagates wave fronts from slice to slice in space. it works, because in wave optics you can define the entire radiance field by a single slice that only captures complex phase at every point. this is much less information than geometrical optics where you need to store radiance for every point of slice and for every direction
AdrianM: That doesn't immediately make sense to me. I might have to do some thinking on that.
AdrianM: For wave propagation are you propagating those waves in the fourier domain with discrete time steps? Is that any different from simulating the waves in normal space?

Yaazarai: It would take some work but youcould rewrite the shader to use 2x density increase in planes.
Yaazarai: Hmmmmmmmm. I need to revisit axis-aligned planes. For some reason tracing is way faster.
Yaazarai: For reference on a 1024 x 1024 scene tracing standard HRC takes me like 100ms. But axis aligned planes takes 10ms.
RadianceCausality: axis-aligned planes?
Yaazarai: All probes are re-oriented towards the ray-direction.
Yaazarai: Like that.
RadianceCausality: huh
Yaazarai: Here my probes are grouped by `cascade interval` so for so c4 you get 16 probes on the y-axis as one group and they all have varying X-offsets to form the compelte planes.
Yaazarai: Then the tracing interval changes with plane angle--because distance between planes gets smaller with angle: `floor(intrv * cos(theta))`
Yaazarai: That performance difference is insane.
Yaazarai: TL;DR though the reason I stopped is its impossible to do proper ray extensions.
RadianceCausality: ray extensions > tracing anyways
RadianceCausality: but huh that is really weird
Yaazarai: Agreed, extensions are the best, but also artifacts.

Yaazarai: I still think its possible to fix with the new A/B-type merges I found, just can't figure out the weights.
Youssef Af: cant understand how that makes any difference in performance ?
Yaazarai: Same... I think it somehow just improves cache hits.
Youssef Af: axis-aligned means your frustums are rotated with a certain angle right ?
Yaazarai: Not frustums. Planes. You still have 4 frustums.
Yaazarai: I'm using the same texture memory layout and frustums as my Vanilla HRC too.
Youssef Af: with these planes you compute this frustum ?
Yaazarai: Because all of the ray directions stay the same.
Youssef Af: the lookup should be hell
Yaazarai: The math is trivial.
Yaazarai: Its just Vanilla HRC then apply an X-offset relative to the probe's y-position in its group depending on ray direction:
```glsl
vec2 getLineSlopeOffset(vec2 probe, float intrv, float slope) {
    float bias = step(0.0, slope);
    float ypos = mix(render_extent - probe.y, probe.y, bias);
    float skew = floor(abs(ypos * slope) + (bias * 0.5));
    return probe + vec2(mod(skew, intrv), 0.0);
}
```
Yaazarai: Group size on the Y-axis = interval.
Youssef Af: and this is faster ?
you should find which angle gives fastest speed
Yaazarai: Well idk cuz the tracing / interval length varies with angle too.
Yaazarai: But yes, much faster.
Youssef Af: oh so you make sure the rays reaches the other plane ?
Youssef Af: weird
Yaazarai: I didn't want them to overlap like Vanilla HRC.
Youssef Af: that working is strange, you will end up with a completly strange ray branching scheme
Yaazarai: Nah, branching is the same as Vanilla.
Yaazarai: Well kind of....
Yaazarai: +/- some distance because you would technically need to trace to the nearest cN+1 plane.
Yaazarai: Which I am not doing yet.
Youssef Af: but you are using different intervals we should visualize that at least for 45 degree
Yaazarai: Ehhh I doubt its worth it. The value of extensions is what mattered most with HRC and its not possible.
Yaazarai: All I wanna know is why its faster for tracing.
Yaazarai: Time would be better spent trying to fix the new A/B-type merges for Vanilla.

Yaazarai: @AdrianM the heat equation solution you use... Is it the same principle for generating a vector field for path finding?
AdrianM: What are you asking?
Yaazarai: For path finding you can generate a vector field--e.g. an image of vectors pointing towards the closest path to some point. Which i similar to heat dissperion, but much more trivial.
AdrianM: That only gives you a single point
Yaazarai: [image]
AdrianM: You need to have every single point be potentially influenced by every single other point
AdrianM: An N-to-N problem in the truest sense
Yaazarai: Right. However I only need a single point... In that case is it sitll the same complex problem or can it be simplified?
Yaazarai: You can also approximate a vector field as heat disspersion because the general vector direction can be approximated by the heat gradient in an area.
AdrianM: In that case I suspect you could massively simplify
AdrianM: I don't like averaging directions for larger blocks since in the case of something like a maze you can have many disconnected averages in close proximity.
Yaazarai: Makes sense. In the simplified case then, woudl scaling still be the same?
RadianceCausality: could go use walk-on-spheres
AdrianM: Walk on spheres, like ray marching, gives almost no benefit for dense scenes. It depends what you are using it for. If you want to path find a mostly empty space great, if you want to solve mazes then good luck.
AdrianM: I suspect you can get much better scaling for both 2D and 3D if all you want to solve is path finding to a single point.
Yaazarai: The bullshit I came up with a few years back isn't much better.... Essentially do a series of cardinal (NSEW) raymarches / SDFs.
AdrianM: As a side note, I was toying around with the idea of path finding Hamiltonian paths using these methods. It probably shouldn't be possible but if it is then that would open up some really interesting doors since Hamiltonian paths are thought to be a NP problem.
AdrianM: Is this path finding for a single point? I see a few different points there
Yaazarai: Single point at the center of the blue area.
Yaazarai: The gradiant represents the vector field.
Yaazarai: I never used it for obvious reasons.
AdrianM: Hmmmm
AdrianM: It's an interesting problem
Yaazarai: Its actually very fast, just janky.
Yaazarai: But I figured whatever math hellscape you came up with would be applicable to path finding.
AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: This is an example of the kinds of worst cases we need to be thinking of
Yaazarai: Yes and I'm not happy about it.
AdrianM: Unfortunately the only way I know to solve this in an arbirtrary manor is sequentially removing nodes
AdrianM: However in the case of diffusion each time we remove a node we need to redistrubute the weights between that node and every other node within the cascade block.
AdrianM: For path finding I suspect that operation will be simpler
AdrianM: So perhaps it will be the same number of sequential steps, but you can probably reduce the amount of parallel compute required for each step
Yaazarai: Could you do it faster by sacrificing quality? I don't care about accuracy as much because I would be using it more for organic path finding.
RadianceCausality: I swear I've seen some algorithms for hierarichal path finding which kinda do some sorta node compression thing
Yaazarai: This for example? https://gamedev.stackexchange.com/a/192186/42657
Yaazarai: I believe its also possible to do temporal reproejction on flow fields as well to simplify frame-by-frame updates.
AdrianM: A line of thought I was exploring was what if instead of a data structure that gives you an exact path for any point you instead had a data structure that you could query that would tell you if the point you've selected has a possible path to the desired end.
AdrianM: Then you can take a step and block the node behind you, ask if a path still exists, if it doesn't then you know you've taken a wrong step so go back. If it does then keep proceeding until you have a path.
AdrianM: But I say this as a hypothetical just to leave an open spot in your mind in case that kind of data structure turns out to be easier to build and query.
Yaazarai: Uhhhh I broke it. Accidentally displayed the cascade merging distribution.
Yaazarai: (trying to get that damn A/B-type merge thing working)
Yaazarai: And more interestingly....
Yaazarai: @AdrianM so this may sound silly, but without B-type rays... Just reformulating HRC as if it were path tracing works. Count total ray contributions from top-down and multiply-up the contribution of each ray at each cascade based on how much total cN+1 rays it merges with.
Yaazarai: No weighting at all by angle, but whatever--it works at least.
AdrianM: You are doing the merge like normal and then manually tracing the middle ray right?
Yaazarai: I haven't added the middle ray yet, but when I get there, yes.
Yaazarai: So far this is just Vanilla HRC.
Yaazarai: With weighting basedo npath-traced-esque averaging.
Yaazarai: [image]
Yaazarai: Like that.
AdrianM: The only downside of that approach is that ray has to be traced independently
Yaazarai: I mean, you have to do that anyways.
Yaazarai: Or store rays and merge it down independently.
AdrianM: Although you might be able to sample it from a higher cascade
Yaazarai: You can't because the ray needs to extend to infinity, not just the cascade interval.
AdrianM: Pick your poison I suppose, it is all the same result regardless
Yaazarai: Anyways, the general idea is that each ray represents some sum-total path radiance at its cascade level, as if it were path traced multiply times.
Yaazarai: So a green ray at c1 above represents 2x its traced radiance because it merges with 2 rays in c2 (yellow).
Yaazarai: @Alexander I think this is accurate right?
Alexander: radiance does not add, it's integrated over (so, averaged)
Alexander: during merging
Yaazarai: I'm averaging at the end.
Yaazarai: Right, but if you trace N rays along the exact same path, the radiance along that path is additive or well multiplicitive by N.
Alexander: not really, it's merged. each interval adds its radiance and attenuates the contribution of interval behind it with its transmittance
Yaazarai: No no, I get that. I'm talking about just the interval itself.

So for example with path tracing (monte-carlo for example) you cast some N amount of rays an infinite length then average their total contribution. Now assuming 2 rays perfectly overlap, the radiance they represent is exactly the same as if you traced the ray once and multiplied its output by 2.
Yaazarai: Unless I'm really really missing something, I am not sure how that would be incorrect.
Yaazarai: It works perfectly when there's almost no B-type rays. Then breaks because basically every position contains B-type rays.
Alexander: it's not multiplied by 2, it should be averaged out and divided by 2. the more rays you shoot in a given direction, the less contribution they should have
Yaazarai: Yes, that's what I mean. Its the same as if you did: `(trace(A, B)  * 2) / 2`. I know the multiply and divide here is redundant. But its important.
Yaazarai: @AdrianM I think what I did kind of works, but we have a bigger problem.

Left is without B-type rays. Note the over-contribution within the radiance field even without the B-type rays.
Yaazarai: So I think what I did kind of actuall works.
Yaazarai: And there's somehow overall within the rays themselves.
Yaazarai: So going back to doing just Vanilla HRC and removing near-plane interpolation on B-type merges.

You can see the gaps are created by B-type rays missing.
While the noise is created by ray-paths crossing between merges.
Yaazarai: Its essentially a dual-perspective problem.
Yaazarai: The same point is being seen from multiple perspectives across the X-axis.
Yaazarai: @AdrianM so the problem isn't entirely B-type merges. Its also the tracing function.
Yaazarai: This explains why RadianceCasualty gets smoother results when analytically tracing a point light.
Yaazarai: @RadianceCausality Isn't this sort of thing something DDA handles?
Yaazarai: Adjusting traced radiance relative to the exact ray path.
Alexander: that's the actual reason why bilinear fix works, not the handwavy explation "it makes rays connected". this is also why inner/outer parallax fixes work, even though their rays are not continuous
Yaazarai: Right its all perspective correction.
Yaazarai: Except that you can't really do that here.
Alexander: why
Yaazarai: Because HRC deliberately has no spatial interpolation. So instead I think I need to treat each pixel as a point-source and adjust its contribution along the exact ray path.
AdrianM: Does the right side work for any light x position / multiple light sources?
AdrianM: Also what you are referring to on the left as overcontribution you'll find of you average out those gaps is correct
AdrianM: But it still doesn't solve the issue that even correcting the rays doesn't allow us to correct the weights for more than a single x position
AdrianM: Also why are your lines all so checkerboardy are you doing ray extensions or something?

Alexander: the first thing i'd do is to replace any sdf raymarching, pixel stepping, dda, any brezenham or whatever else there is with an analytic ray-sphere and ray-box intersection, depending on what you're approximating and manually intersect the single emissive pixel with that. this is just much more reliable way that's guaranteed to not have artifacts
Alexander: because the last thing you want is to have some more implementation specific sdf raymarching stuff to be getting in your way

Alexander: i don't think at this point we even understand if pixels have to be boxes, circles or edge (surface elements) for HRC merging to work the best
Alexander: yeah and how about you trace a circle instead of box?
Alexander: or an edge in pixel center? or an edge between pixels?
Alexander: which one is correct? which one should it be?
Yaazarai: No idea, yeah.
Yaazarai: But yeah I figured after all this tracing pixels directly is most likely extremely suboptimal.
Alexander: i actually think that probes should be located in pixel corners, not in pixel centers, and occlusion should happen between pixel boundaries
RadianceCausality: That probably makes sense
Alexander: it's like finite element vs finite volume
Alexander: both can be made correct, but some work better than others for different tasks
Alexander: so you really want to have the control to be able to switch between them
Alexander: and anything that involves efficient raymarching always makes assumptions like "rays start from pixel centers" or "rays get occluded by geometry aligned with pixel boundaries"
Alexander: so unnecessary
Alexander: i imagine an image like this might accidentally be entirely 100% fixed if you switched from pixel-centered probes to pixel-corner probes
AdrianM: ?
Alexander: because some merge patterns might just happen to work if you flip stuff like that
AdrianM: I think the issue is a little deeper than that
Alexander: possible
AdrianM: You tried to get it to work with frustums, so you know that the weighting is perhaps impossible to correct
Alexander: but we shouldn't implicitly make any assumptions about how it should be laid out. it should be laid out in a way that works the best during merging. not the other way round, we shouldn't be constructing a merging scheme assuming our probes are centered in pixel centers
Alexander: which of these arrangements for a probe (orange) vs pixel (red) is correct?
Alexander: my point is, we don't know, and we need to figure out by seeing which one works better for merging
Alexander: we shouldn't assume that it's always where the probe is in the center of a pixel. it can be in the corner, it can be on the edge. it's possible that even probes need to be on horizontal edges and odd probes need to be on vertical edges. it's possible that cascades should alternate too.
Alexander: which location do you approximate with the dda implementation that you have? well, i don't know. but what i do know is that it had to make some implicit assumption there that you're not changing
Alexander: what happens with volumetric raymarching? some completely arbitrary fuck-all thing, nobody knows what happens, because it's not precise enough.
Alexander: we should be removing unknowns from the problem, not adding more
Alexander: keep abstracting away until we reduce the problem to something that can be solved, then un-abstract it back to something practical. that's why i really respect when @AdrianM has completely forsaken any lighting scenes in favour of just a single octant with a single source and manhattan distance metric. that was a good amount of reduction to the problem that we need. but since we still haven't solved it, we need to keep simplifying it by removing more unknowns.
ion: Not all worlds are constructed from pixels. E.g. if you run 3d sparce RC the world might consist of polygons. Maybe pixels should be abstracted away too. This could require having some form of antialiasing in the raytracing to be able to deal with smaller-then probe-spacing geometry. In my HRC merging experiments I am tracing a bunch of parallel rays per probe and direction and averaging them instead of just a single ray, so i don't have to worry about aliasing artefacts.
Yaazarai: Do you have any results to share? The thing about HRC is that it currently mostly feasible in 2D. In 2D no one uses geometry or BVHs for the most part beyond AABB.

Finding a wildly complex solution that requires converting all of your standard 2D sprite assets into BVH geometry kind of defeats the purpose. Solving the problem and solving the problem while being realistically usable are two separate endeavors.
Yaazarai: Anyways, that doesn't entirely matter I guess, the final solution will just kinda be whatever it is.
ion: Its in 2D and I made the implementation purely to experiment with merging patterns. I'm not planning to do anything with it other then reporting back here if I find out something interesting.

ion: I've never seriously looked at any existing implementations code, nor read the paper in detail. I am building it entirely on my own from my mental model of how it should work. I haven't yet come to the point where quality is comparable to what has been shown here by others. Or at least that's my impression. My merging is very much not done yet.
ion: I'm using a pixel world and my probes are aligned with pixels, but my goal is to build the system such that is doesnt depend on that.  Pixels are interpreted as squares. Here are two merging patterns I have so far and a reference where I just randomly cast rays from the pixel center. The sharper image shows the RC output directly, and the unsharp one shows it interpolated to pixel centers, because my direct RC output represents the light in the corners of pixels. The two light sources are 1 pixel each. They are 1 pixel apart horizontally, to test the extreme cases of maximal aligned and maximal unaligned merges (my terminiology for odd/even, cant ever remember which is which). To the left of each light are 2 black single pixel shadowcasters. There is no correction for the angle at the moment, so diagonals are too bright in the RC images. I suspect there also might be more reasons for that. The RC images look darker then the reference. My transparency handling is somehow broken, didn't have time to look more deeply into it, but there are no transparent things in these images.
ion: It's single threaded on the CPU, written in Julia.
ion: Seems I need to upscale the images for discord. One moment.
ion: [image]
ion: Here are 2 images showing the difference with and without AA for a single frustum. What seems to happen is that without AA the diagonal rays get twice as many hits on the single pixel light, becuase when seen from the diagonal a square is wider. Without AA this is an abrupt change, whereas with AA it changes more gradually. The only thing I do AA wise is to cast multiple rays instead of just one per probe*direction. They then get averaged to just have 1 ray per probe*direction as normal, so there could still be aliasing-like effects arising in the later steps.
ion: The first set of images was with AA. There as still some edges in there. I haven't investigated yet whether thats caused by the merging or because my "AA-resolution" wasnt high enough.
ion: A note about the artifacts in the merging patterns: I know that one pattern has some rays twice in there, and the other is having some rays only "half" in there, which explains at least some of the artefacts. Working on it.
ion: One thing that surprised me is that one of the merging patterns seems to never use rays in unaligned probes, which are half the probes. Haven't verified that yet, but if true  50% of the rays don't need to be traced.
ion: I'm not sure if i dont have to worry, but im trying to take it out as something to worry about.

ion: hmm, lets look at this cone stump
ion: If I extrapolate the sides i end up at the tip of the overall frustum. This angle defines the weight of the cone.
ion: But for b-type merges if i do the same I dont end up at the frustum tip. So there are now two possible definitions of the angle. I think the one where the angle is measured from the frustum tip is correct.
ion: Which can also be computed for b-rays
ion: But this assumes that the rays all hit at the same distance, and this distance is clearly defined (= no transparency)
ion: For the standard merge pattern its independend of distance.
Yaazarai: Correct that is the current problem with the added/subtracted B-type rays.
Yaazarai: Having weights dependent upon hit distance isnt really viable with volumetrics.
Yaazarai: Hmmm actually, this makes sense. I think maybe we can modify the weights to take advantage of this?

Instead of calculating the weight of each cone in cN, the weight of each cone is actually dependent upon where it lands in cN-1. So the weights need to actually be moved into the lower cascade.
Yaazarai: Then the weight can be calculated by the angular span it would represent when merged into the cascade below it.
Yaazarai: Like that.
Yaazarai: [image]
Yaazarai: Weight is calculated as the angular span of the cone formed by the interval of cN-1 + cone of cN+1.
ion: I wonder if it can be generalized to volumetrics. Not without artefacts I think, but maybe these artefacts wouldn't be too bad?
ion: How would the angles for the b-ray and the cone on the other side of the b-ray look like?
ion: There are a couple differences between your drawing and mine.
ion: Your angle lines end at ray ends, mine end at the midpoints between them because the rays represent area on both of their sides.
ion: My angle tip is at the tip of the overall frustum, not the current cascades "merging point" (I don't have a good word for this).
ion: It confuses me that we often draw the cones delineations where the rays are, when they actually should also include half of the gaps to the next ray.
Yaazarai: You cant compute the cone width from the tip of the frustum because you dont know how/where that cone ends up until you've merged all the way down.
Yaazarai: Its not really an option.

Yaazarai: You have to weight them separately.

Yaazarai: Imagine that the merge structure is discrete path tracing. You can track the number of rays in the merge structure and reconstruct the paths discretely by multiplying each cascade level up to represent the total number of rays in the merges above it.
Yaazarai: So now the rays arent treated as cones but as individual rays.
ion: But the rays "keep branching" until they hit something, so the number of rays depends on how far they travel before they hit. Except for b-rays of course. Maybe I'm misunderstanding something. (tried googling "discrete path tracing" but couldn't find a definition quickly)
Yaazarai: I know. So what you do is carry the total number of rays down the merge structure and add the B type rays to that total.
Yaazarai: Then divide the whole thing by the total rays that show up in c0.

ion: I suspect a fundamental problem of many attempts to solve this is that the weighing doesn't just need to change with each branching, but also between the branchings.
