Alexander: apparently i was normalizing the ray that i cast to intersect the pixel aabb
Alexander: so t.x-t.y was in pixels
Alexander: but i guess it should be a ratio of ray's total length?
Alexander: i don't even
Alexander: @fad what's the deal here?
Alexander: it feels like 1/length of the ray needs to be in the weight maybe?
Alexander: guys, i think i see it. it's called multisweeping. so you know line sweeping that casts one ray per pixel in one direction with O(1) cost per pixel? well, what multisweeping does is it casts _all_ rays for _all_ pixels in O(log N) cost per pixel.

this is literally what Dolkar was describing on GP. imagine for each NxN box on the screen. that box has NxN lines that can possibly cross it. so using that box and its neigbours we can in O(1) per pixel create a 2x larger box of size 2Nx2N by connecting 4 boxes together and precalculate all their rays
Alexander: on the illustration green is a ray in the large box that we want to approximate, two yellow paths is lines in its sub-boxes that we can split it into
Alexander: so you can see how this can be used to cast all rays from all pixels on the screen using log N image-space passes. the result of this is we entirely fill the line space, every pixel of it. which means we know the raycast result of all rays in the scene. now the crazy part is we can use the same exact aglorithm _in line space_ to gather fluence for all _pixels in real space_ because each in the line space corresponds to gathering fluence in the real space for a single pixel.
RadianceCausality: ...
RadianceCausality: this is something new?
literally the entire reason why I asked the question about line gathering twoish days ago

the problem I have is I'm unsure how to do merging
Alexander: the way to think about this is liek this: you though line sweeping is neat? well this subtracts one dimension from that
Alexander: nothing is new under the sane
Alexander: it's all the same lines and the same points
Alexander: the question is what you can do with them and what you can notice about them
RadianceCausality: although I'm fairly certain you still want to restrict to 45deg
Alexander: and noticing that multisweeping folds one dimension away relative to sweeping is actually huge
RadianceCausality: wdym multisweeping folding away one dimension?
RadianceCausality: it changes from O(N^3) to O(N^2 log N) but what?
Alexander: if you were to sweep all possible lines using line sweeping, it's O(N^3) total work
Alexander: yes exactly
RadianceCausality: we've had that since hrc extensions though
Alexander: well it folds one dimension into a log
RadianceCausality: it is worth checking if this results in acceptable error but I'm not sure it solves merging issues
Alexander: the thing is, this process is exact, the only source of inaccuracy is line aliasing
Alexander: so i thought about merging
Alexander: and i actually think there's a different way of solving merging
RadianceCausality: ?
Alexander: so imagine this stored value in one of the boxes
Alexander: it stores information about all lines that pass through these 2 segments
Alexander: now imagine instead of storing 1 value, we store 5 values, where each value only considers intersections in a certain height range:
Alexander: depending on which exact pixel we gather fluence for, we can use a different value
Alexander: now, what does it mean 5? how many do we actually need?
Alexander: i my point is, i think we need any value greater than 1?
Alexander: maybe even 2 will work?
RadianceCausality: I still think we shouldn't be considering lines at all
they're just a distraction, even if this stuff is neat
Alexander: what does htat mean
Alexander: it is not, because it defines all pixels that we can gather fluence at using them
RadianceCausality: we should be directly summing pixel values
or otherwise integrating over the volume

We don't need straight lines for stuff to look good, and lines are only necessary for occlusion anyways
Alexander: we should be directly summing pixel values in realspace to produce lines, we should be summing pixels in line space over lines to produce fluence
Alexander: this is exactly where non-straightness of lines comes into play
Alexander: essentially any rasterized line segment will belong to multiple rasterized lines. so we reuse it among _all_ lines that share it
Goobley: I am not visualising how this works at all, but it sounds good
RadianceCausality: ...I should try to check how well a HRC-extension based path tracer works
Alexander: we should be testing extensions and merging separately from each other
Alexander: the way to visualize it is all pixel-rasterized lines can share rows of pixels
Alexander: so for every segment of pixels like that, we cache its raymarched value
Alexander: and then apply it to every line that has a segment like that
Alexander: essentially it's like caching parts of dda and then reusing them between all lines that end up walking that same path
Alexander: the longer the path is, the fewer lines share it
Alexander: single pixel paths are shared by all lines that pass through that pixel
Goobley: Right, yeah, and that merging/extension process can be done in an efficient way?
Goobley: We obviously discussed things like that with sweeping and extensions before, but never put together a viable stratgy for building all the lines up, that I'm aware of
Alexander: i mean extensions is just addition, merging is just merging 2 intervals
Alexander: i want to see extensions working first in empty space with this. if this works, make merging work in empty space. then we can think of making occlusion work, which i feel should be possible, but i don't fully see it yet
Alexander: it also should really help to just visualize all rays that share a given segment
Alexander: and how it builds up
Goobley: I guess I see the concept, but if one really does generate all the intermediate N^2 paths in 2D, that seems like a lot?
Alexander: why do you think that's a lot? that's one viewport-sized image worth of rays to store
Alexander: oh yeah there are n^2 possible segments of every length
Goobley: Yeah, as you start merging up you have more and more
Goobley: Unless I'm missing something
Alexander: the number is the same regardless of box size
Goobley: oh. yeah.
Alexander: so we can just store log N total viewport-size images
Goobley: you got me
Goobley: Okay, nice that that's perfectly tractable
Alexander: it's so neat
Goobley: So it _should_ be lossless?
Alexander: it's not quite lossless because lines will be not quite fully straight
Alexander: but up to that it should be lossless i think?
Goobley: So long as you can select the right rays, which seems like there's a messy indexing scheme to be contrived
Alexander: it's not messy at all, because for each NxN block there are NxN lines passing through it
Alexander: you literally store them inside of blocks
Goobley: Yeah, storing them is fine, I was thinking about if I wanted to grab the (x -> y) ray on a level, I'd need to know where to find it, but I guess you just lay them out flat
Alexander: rays are just indexed by which pixels they connect on the top and on the bottom of every block
Goobley: So you end up with multiple possible path constructions too, as you extend
Alexander: how so?
Alexander: you mean line rasterization ambiguities?
Alexander: i want to just connect pixel centers

Alexander: yeah probably will need to average or pick one
Alexander: so far planning to pick one
Goobley: Yeah, I was thinking averaging might give you free "anti-aliasing" with occlusion
Goobley: Or it might be a mess
Alexander: yeah i want to implement the simplest possible version first
Alexander: i think i bit a bit too much with the whole basis function thing
Alexander: it's too complex to start
Alexander: should just see how it scales first and then make it more accurate
Goobley: So to get the actual full radiance at every world voxel you would need to keep the full log(N) chain around so you can reconstruct for each one, right?
Alexander: you can reconstruct fluence slowly (by iterating over N lines per pixels) using just the last cascade, that connects every point on the screen perimeter with every other point
Alexander: but the hope is that by storing log N cascades, we can gather fluence fast by doing the same exact process in the line space

Alexander: there are 2 ways of doing shadows: lossless and lossy
Alexander: lossless way is: for any shape in the line space that you store as a single value (average value of all lines in the bundle), you can actually store multiple values that correspond to different interval lenghts. similar to how HRC planes work. or you can capture them inside boxes. anyway, instead of storing them for infinite lines, you can store multiple values for that rhombus at different lengths
Alexander: the second way is you store a single value but you store it for a specific interval length (for example, when rays intersect, it's natural to store interval that starts at the intersection point and then spans a certain distance) and then you exploit overlapping regions: if two regions overlap and you know that one of them stores intervals at a larger offset than the other one, you can merge them in that order knowing that one will always occlude the other
Alexander: i think HRC relies on both during merging and extensions
Alexander: overall imo we should really study how HRC works on the line diagram, just to develop the feeling what a working scheme looks like

fad: idk what you're looking at, this is all you have to do```py
output = [0 for _ in range(n)]
radiance = 0
# compute left -> right sweep
for i in range(n):
  output[i] += radiance
  radiance = radiance * pixel[i].transparency + pixel[i].emissivity
radiance = 0
# compute right -> left sweep
# ...
```

fad: if you’re doing this over a 2D image, you can launch one thread per line, and with a few directions running at once, you’re at thousands of threads which is easily enough to saturate the GPU
AdrianM: It's still sequential though over the length of the line
fad: if you have enough work to saturate the GPU there is no benefit to parallelizing over each line
AdrianM: I suppose
fad: but if you really do only have 1 line (so you can’t parallelize across lines and have to actually parallelize within the single line), then the structure of this is essentially a prefix sum and there’s a lot of literature on how to make prefix sums fast
fad: so you can still get O(n) with a single line too, the code just won’t look as simple
AdrianM: I thought prefix sums are n log n
fad: luckily no, there are O(n) prefix scans suitable for the GPU
fad: you can even do it in a single pass, which i don’t get how, but it’s possible and is O(n)
https://research.nvidia.com/sites/default/files/pubs/2016-03_Single-pass-Parallel-Prefix/nvr-2016-002.pdf
fad: though i do know that technique can’t be implemented in most graphics APIs without UB because it relies on forward progress guarantees or something
fad: but it works in CUDA
Alexander: O(n) is absolutely nasty
Alexander: and even though on a good day O(n) it can actually deadlock
Alexander: especially if you run it on some other hardware that has different scheduling rules than nvidia
Alexander: it's super nailed to the way nvidia architecture works
fad: pretty cool that it’s possible at all on some hardware though
fad: and with it being a big player like nvidia it puts more pressure on others to eventually implement the necessary capabilities on their gpus as well
Alexander: it's not capabilities
Alexander: it's that there are nasty non user controllable stuff like 32 wide wave lanes that you have to hardcode and then on intel GPU's wave length is 4
Alexander: or you rely on the scheduler working in a very specific way, such as if one thread starts executing you rely on it finishing without losing the execution context
Alexander: the whole thing doesn't feel like a nice algorithm, it feels more like a hardware hack that :works_on_my_machine:
Randy: That just sounds like relying on known hardware behavior to do something serially that just so happens to be constructed in parallel
Goobley: It’s almost like there should be abstractions over hardware capabilities for most of the common operations of this type
Alexander: it's almost as if a certain company is not particularly interested in having abstractions that make it look like their hardware is kind of equivalent to others
Alexander: it's not too bad, but it's far from pretty imo
Randy: NVIDIA is heavily incentivized to make it as hard as possible to use any other product
Alexander: absolutely

AdrianM: Now what happens if we extend to further cascade?
AdrianM: It squishes our rhombus in line space as we go further way. You can almost thing of this as our rhombus becoming more 'line-line' as the geometric footprint gets stretched into a line.
AdrianM: We can think of each cascade as a different vertical line here defined by different x edge positions
AdrianM: The rhombus we get for that cascade forms a kind of basic function for that cascade
AdrianM: In a lossless world we want to convert from the rhombus of one cascade to the rhombus of the other cascade.
AdrianM: Which is effectively asking the question: how can we squish a rhombus without being allowed to scale along the x axis?
AdrianM: Unfortunately the answer is I think we can't at least not this way
AdrianM: By default we can't change the rhombus scew angle at all. But with half bundles we can split it in half
AdrianM: But we only get to split it in one direction
AdrianM: And unfortunately splitting it between those two corners rotates the wrong way

AdrianM: But it's the wrong way!

AdrianM: Actually we may need to solve that problem either way for this to be possible. If we fix one defining plane at x=0 then reproject the other, then our new definition is now based on penumbra. So to reproject for the next cascade we need to be able to reproject the penumbra. It's either that or keep the original definition and reproject from there each time. Which is basically the same as merging all higher cascade into the current cascade.
AdrianM: A penumbra is defined geometrically between two lines
AdrianM: It's basically the result of cutting an umbra in half
AdrianM: A penumbra is a triangle and an umbra is a rhombus
AdrianM: [image]

AdrianM: [image]
AdrianM: Because two of them are aligned to the same axis and we know that moving one of the definition planes in real space is a skew in linespace. Then we know that these two definitions share one defining plane.
AdrianM: In this case because the direction of that shared plane is horizontal we know that both can be defined with a plane at x=0

```glsl
float ray_len = length(ray_end - ray_start);
float step_len = ray_len / float(num_samples) * exp2(u_mip_level);
vec3 trans_step = pow(trans, vec3(step_len));
vec3 opacity = vec3(1.0) - trans_step;
vec3 rad = opacity * emiss * step_len;
```
Yaazarai: Tracing or extensions?
Yaazarai: Weighted rays for tracing is trivial. Extensions are the problem.

```glsl
vec2 ray_start = vec2(float(probe_x), float(gid.y));
float step_len = length(target_f - ray_start) * exp2(u_mip_level);
vec3 trans_step = pow(trans, vec3(step_len));
```
Yaazarai: Yeah thats why it doesn't work. You have to weight during tracing.

Yaazarai: These results are actually very very good for low res so far. We unfortunately don't have much to work with on the pixel level.
Yaazarai: Correct. The most basic tracing function traces exactly and only N pixels across a line of length N. The method I provided here adjusts the radiance/transmittance by increasing the influence of each sample depending on angle of the ray--since diagonal rays are technically longer than they should be.
Yaazarai: Reference Path Tracer: https://github.com/Yaazarai/PathTraced-Volumetrics
Yaazarai: DDA also does this, except it incorporates more samples to diagonal rays instead of weighting. This method is a much simpler alternative.

Yaazarai: When you set merging to the far plane (inteprolation of 1.0) there should be gaps between rays.
Yaazarai: No, you just need to look up the appropriate rays/cones from the correct probes.
Yaazarai: So you may have some off-by-one or rounding error somewhere.
Yaazarai: TL;DR of it is that Vanilla HRC merging can't correctly connect even planes so it has to interpolate the near and far plane. The near-plane causes some angular overlap and the far plane causes angular underlap. They average eachother out when interpolating.
Yaazarai: So your far-plane merging should have those gaps in the reference image above.

Smalltalk: Tried my hand at implementing bounce light. (I upped the intensity of my lights to make sure I could see the effect well enough). I just did this via a fragment shader that mixes together a bunch of textures.
```glsl
layout (set = 2, binding = 1) uniform sampler2D albedo_tex;
layout (set = 2, binding = 2) uniform sampler2D radiance_tex;
layout (set = 2, binding = 3) uniform sampler2D transmittance_tex;

layout (set = 3, binding = 1) uniform shd_uniforms {
    float u_bounce_scale;
};

vec4 shader(vec4 color, ShaderParams params)
{
    vec3 albedo = texture(albedo_tex, screen_uv).rgb;
    vec3 radiance = max(texture(radiance_tex, screen_uv).rgb, vec3(0.0));
    vec3 transmittance = texture(transmittance_tex, screen_uv).rgb;
    vec3 opacity = vec3(1.0) - transmittance;
    return vec4(albedo * radiance * opacity * u_bounce_scale, 0.0);
}
```
This is how I interpreted the paper. I'm just curious, would running multiple times a frame produce better results?

Smalltalk: Thought I'd share a proper implementation of multibounce. The previous one I shared was just accumulating the previous frame's radiance into my emission. This one actually runs the lighting pipeline 6 times per frame:
Alexander: it should result in the same image
Smalltalk: I think I must be misunderstanding something important then. My previous implementation was just combining albedo and radiance, and then adding that on top of the emissive texture every frame.
Alexander: what is "combining"
Alexander: also what is "adding on top"
Alexander: you should be keeping your direct and indirect lighting separate
Alexander: every frame you add indirect part of lighting to itself
Alexander: emissive stays constant
Alexander: when a ray hits a surface, to get its radiance you add its direct and indirect components
Smalltalk: That's really funny, I had this thought before trying out just running the lighting pipeline N times.
Alexander: you should still have a verification test like that just to compare manually running 10x passes vs accumulating over time
Smalltalk: I'm still seeing things not quite converge after 6 passes it seems. In theory, after 6 passes things should stop getting "brighter" right?
Alexander: they should only converge to the degree of the brightest albedo to the power of 6
Alexander: if your brightest albedo is around 1, it will keep bouncing forever
Smalltalk: That makes sense. I set it to 0.9, lol.
Alexander: that's why they paint living room ceiling and walls white most of the time
Smalltalk: 22 bounces
Alexander: you can pick albedo = 0.5, then it should add up to exactly twice that at infinity iterations
Alexander: https://tenor.com/view/flashbang-flash-cat-betterttv-gif-10690574645280585754
Smalltalk: This is super old, but, thank you for mentioning the kind-of interlacing of quadrants (Where you can process half of the pixels in merge because y overlaps.) It cut my frame time down by about 25% with no artifacts that I could see at all.
Smalltalk: Figured I'd share a bug I spent like 4-5 hours trying to figure out. I feel really stupid. I've had support in my lighting for emitters that don't cast shadows via a ternary:
```glsl
float dda_step = step_length * exp2(u_mip_level);
float trans_step = pow(trans, dda_step);
float opacity = 1.0 - trans_step;

vec3 rad = opacity > eps ? opacity * emiss : emiss * dda_step;
```
I recently started running into an issue where these emitters were producing basically no radiance, and couldn't figure out why. Here are my notes after going through step by step...
```
// When absorption is 0.0:
1. trans = dot(vec3(1.0), vec3(0.333)) = 0.999
2. trans as R16_FLOAT quantizes 0.999 -> 0.99902
3. For DIAGONAL RAYS (cascade 0 is only diagonal rays)
   dda_step   = sqrt(2);             // approx. 1.414
   trans      = 0.99902;             // from R16_FLOAT
   trans_step = pow(0.99902, 1.414); // 0.99862
   opacity    = 1.0 - 0.99862;       // 0.00138
4. 0.00138 > 0.001                   // TRUE
   rad = 0.00138 * emiss;            // 0.00138 * 3.0 = 0.00414
   // vs
   rad = emiss * dda_step            // 3.0 * 1.414 = 4.243
```
TLDR: Specify a good epsilon, not just 0.001 (Mine is still pretty close at 1.0/512.0 now). Don't just use 0.333 (I switched to 1.0/3.0)

RadianceCausality: Honestly you could probably start at an offset of 1 for everything
Vudewochatburg: Every cascade starts from plane 1 instead of 0?
Yaazarai: They start from 0, but what happens is when you output the frustums you offset 1px into each frustum to avoid overlap.

This means the plane where this method breaks is always out of bounds and never seen.
Yaazarai: Also you don't need the theoretical highest cascade. If your scene is 512x512 the highest cascade would be c9 with 1 plane and intervals of 512px.

This cascade only affects the very first plane and isn't necessary.

Vudewochatburg: Why do extensions need it?
Yaazarai: Hmmm, maybe you don't but you'll need to change how your extensions work.
Yaazarai: Also your rasterization not rastering to clean edges probably contributes, again for reference: https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1304556173671927878/1414202356702122068
Yaazarai: Vanilla HRC extensions assume volumetrics and use interpolation to get the virtual ray at the midpoint. If you have no volumetric data and you rasterize to hollow circles then your rays end up with transmittance values lower than fully opaque and can "see," through objects when they shouldn't.
Yaazarai: So what you need to do is either rasterize to full volume circles or change your extensions to choose left or right paths for rays (possibly alternating with each cascade) to avoid this.

Yaazarai: Yeah give that a trey, but just FYI you'll lose some of the reduced aliasing property of extensions.

gasnew: Hi! I'm new here and am a total graphics programming newbie. I'll also attach an image I found online that represents what I'm thinking of (the two lights on the right side)
gasnew: I can hack the desired effect by, e.g., surrounding three of four sides of an emissive material with a solid material, but I would be interested in a way to achieve beams of light emitting from individual pixels
Goobley: Would you want the diffuse haloing as well as the beam? The beam alone could be implemented by making emission dependent on ray angle (though this would make your raymarching step more expensive)
Alexander: is it srgb? looks very dim/fast falloff
fad: > an image I found online
fad: it’s just a reference they found online for the spotlight effect they want to achieve
gasnew: I'd rather not have the diffuse haloing actually, so this sounds perfect! I'm doing ray extensions and merging based on this implementation (https://github.com/Yaazarai/Volumetric-HRC). The two questions I have now: 1) Does it make sense to take beam angle and direction into account during ray extension and leave merging entirely untouched? I could sample a new beam-direction-and-angle texture as I go to reduce emissivity based on ray direction. 2) If so, is it sufficient to sample the beam texture at just the origin position of the current ray, or do I need to do anything more clever or involved?
gasnew: As a side note, please feel free to tell me if anything I'm saying is complete nonsense. I'm still in the very early stages of getting up to speed, so I expect to have sizeable knowledge gaps
gasnew: Thinking more on (2), that probably wouldn't work unless I were passing beam info up the cascades as I go since, for example, there might be a single-pixel beam light. I need all affected rays to somehow access that beam's data
Yaazarai: To have any emissive output dependant upon ray angle you'll need to manually trace some arbitrary amount of lower cascades, then extend the rest.
Yaazarai: Which honestly isn't difficult. The extensions shader can easily be modified to instead just trace the scene when at or below a certain cascade level.
gasnew: Ahh I think that makes sense. Is the tradeoff that I'll get higher angular resolution of beams the more lower cascades I trace manually at the cost of performance?
Yaazarai: Correct.
Yaazarai: You also have to factor in how wide that beam is, is it a beam from a point vs line, etc. There's also a small caveat of how this make be affected when spanning multiple frustums.
Yaazarai: You'll just need to test and see what works.
gasnew: Cool! That makes sense. I'll give it a go and see what I get! Thanks for the help, all
Kæl: I've been messing around with getting hard shadows to work in Vanilla RC and learned a lot. This seems to be a dominantly spatial resolution problem. I had zero improvement no matter how high I crank the angular resolution. But increasing number of probes at any given level of the hierarchy improves quality in relation to that level of probe's contribution. And interestingly it only seemed to matter increasing resolution laterally and not in the direction the rays are pointing.

I'm starting to think this is the secret to HRC's hard shadows. Is it literally that HRC probe resolution is constant except along the frustum direction? Because in VRC testing, that's the only thing that mattered too.
Kæl: Something like this?
Alexander: it's not a spatial resolution and not an angular resolution issue, it's how the scale relative to each other. for example, in vanilla 4x branching RC, spatial resolution scales the same exact way that 2x does, but the shadows are much sharper, because spatial resolution drops 2x slower relative to angular resolution
Alexander: same actually happens in HRC where spatial:angular resolution gets higher every cascade
Cody Bennett: On another note, I have been looking into discontinuity-aware interpolation to better represent hard shadows and mitigate ringing and light leaking.
Cody Bennett: Something like https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1944846.1944849.
Kæl: That explains my observations too, my angular exceeds my spatial. So in 3D, with quadtree branching scheme, but octree probe hierarchy, we have a 4x angular scaling but 1/2 spatial scaling up the hierarchy, so the angular scales 8x that of the spatial? Did I get the ratios correct?
Alexander: no, i measure scaling relative to what's considered "baseline" which is the penumbra condition, which defines the way spatial and angular resolution has to scale in order to represent penumbra of a fixed angular size. then you can have a higher spatial resolution per cascade (say, 2x higher) or angular resolution per cascade on top of that
Alexander: this scaling is the same in 2d or 3d, but memory requirements are different to achieve the same scaling ratio
Kæl: This has become a fascinating problem actually.

samlo: @Yaazarai I am wondering if you noticed a perf speedup doing all the frustums at once instead of serially? might require a compute shader and you'd use more memory but it seems like you could go a lot wider with this algorithm
Yaazarai: I got worse performance and idk why. Probably GameMakers fault.
Yaazarai: Give it a try and let me know.
Yaazarai: In theory it should be exactly the same, just more memory.
samlo: I would expect that if you could do more of it in one warp it'd be faster. But that's pretty context dependent
Yaazarai: To a point. HRC is memory bound not compute bound. So idk how much that'll help.
Yaazarai: Very little compute.
fad: did you use a profiler to figure that out?
Yaazarai: Nah, just kinda figured it out with intuition. I mean I was dealing with GameMaker, so profiling there sucks anyways.

TL;DR of it was that--using purely extensions--I managed to save some 50% performance on my renders by removing a pre-clear-texture command which did a bunch of over-writes on my textures. Mostly on the order of 4k resolution where bandwidth matters most.
Yaazarai: I'm sure if you did just tracing it'd be a different story.
fad: i remember back when i was profiling my line sweeping stuff, i was surprised to find that i was compute and not memory bound, even though previously i would’ve thought that each thread just iterating over pixels and reading and writing to them would’ve been memory bound, but turned out the actual iteration logic was taking longer than i thought
Yaazarai: You did some 1000 passes on that too didn't you?
Yaazarai: Well 1,000 directions IIRC from your last mention of it.
Yaazarai: If that's the case it may be possible to get better performance with compute over just fragment shaders--I mean entirely extra wasted pipeline steps, vertices, rasterization, etc.
fad: yeah 1000 sweep directions on a 1920x1080 image, i had each compute shader invocation do one line sweep
Yaazarai: Okay yeah, that's a far cry from what 88 passes I was doing with merging + extensions.
fad: with line sweeping you can’t really do it with fragment shaders, the whole advantage of line sweeping is that one thread does N reads and N writes (whereas with a fragment shaders you only get 1 write to a single pixel)
fad: the 1000 sweep directions were done in a single compute dispatch/pass btw
Yaazarai: Synchronized with atomics or what?
fad: yes, every thread atomicAdding to a main buffer
fad: and again surprisingly it wasn’t atomic/memory bound, it was compute
fad: i still don’t have an intuitive understanding of GPU performance lol
Yaazarai: Okay interesting, that changes things a lot.
Yaazarai: Same, I just know how GameMaker ruins things for me in stupid ways.
Yaazarai: I'll try out HRC with compute and see what comes up. Can't really do it all in one dispatch, but that shouldn't matter much.
Yaazarai: Also in terms of compute HRC is pretty wasteful in fragment shaders because 90% of the math is the same for every probe.
fad: btw, you NEED to it all in a single/few passes, otherwise there’s a high chance you won’t saturate the GPU with enough work. like, a horizontal sweep on a 1920×1080 image only requires dispatching 1080 threads, while GPUs are designed to handle millions of threads, since you normally have a thread per pixel. so that’s why it’s advantageous to launch all directions at once so that you have at least tens of thousands of threads for the GPU to process
Yaazarai: Yeah I figure I'd launch all 4 frustums + thread per-pixel for the whole scene.
fad: could be interesting to see if there is any performance benefit to precomputing those values on the CPU and passing them in via uniforms. it means more memory reads, though since they are uniform memory reads and not random, maybe it’ll still be fast
Yaazarai: Possibly yeah, could do a pre-compute table for per-ray-direction offsets and pre-computeded weights to eliminate atan()... Otherwise the rest of it is per-probe specific math.
Yaazarai: Also for funzies my GameMaker HRC implementation is 10x more wasteful by doing FP16 math for everything. (:
Yaazarai: So pretty much anything else will perform better by default, lmao.
fad: i think GPUs already have special faster instructions for computing values that are uniform across a warp, so it might not be as bad as you’re imagining. if the compiler knows at compile time that the values are the same across the warp then it will use those scalar instructions to compute it instead of the usual vector instructions
Yaazarai: Typical case of GPU smarter than me.
fad: there’s a good chance this isn’t GM-specific, i’ve heard tales of fp16 math not being a trivial win in all cases, as you have to write your shaders in a way that takes advantage of it (which i have no clue how to do)
Yaazarai: And I definitely did not do that.
Yaazarai: Guarenteed.
Yaazarai: Hmmmm what does bother me though is that my performance is relatively on par with the paper.
Yaazarai: Which IIRC does use compute.
fad: oh wait are you talking about how GM doesn’t have integer types? i misunderstood you as saying you tried swapping fp32 logic for fp16 and didn’t see any performance gain
Yaazarai: Oh yeah no. GLSL 1.0 has no int math or bitwise operators. It doesn't even have the % operator.
Yaazarai: It truly is the most language of all time.
Goobley: This is the sort of thing that has changed a lot over the past few generations. Atomic performance really improved unless you have crazy contention

samlo: finally getting around to implementing this but I'm getting unexpected results with my intuition, which is something like this. I think the idea of just adding to the cN radiance is correct but my direction/attenuation is wayyy off.

```c

if (mostDistantCascade) {
    float2 rayDir = coneCenter; // unsure about this, do i need to compute it for both the left and right side?
    float rayAngle = atan(rayDir.y / rayDir.x);
    float frustumAng[4] = { 0.0, -1.5707963, 3.1415926, 1.5707963 }; // offset the angle by frustum index
    rayAngle += frustumAng[_FrustumIndex];
    float2 wd = float2(cos(rayAngle), sin(rayAngle));
    float ndotl = saturate(dot(wd, lightDirection));
    finalRadiance += ndotl * lightColor * transmit; // just add to the output radiance!
}
```
samlo: ah, i think i need to scale by the cone width because its angular fluence, duh. but the attenuation is still wrong.
Yaazarai: Wdym attenuation? Don't not apply any falloff apart from cone weighting.
Yaazarai: Attenuation / falloff is a byproduct of cascading and merging.
samlo: the ndotl attenuation, for the sky light, I mean. I'm adding the "sky radiance" which is just dot(rayDir, lightDir) * lightColor into the distant radiance of the most distant cascade.

fad: i've been thinking about this (wanting an efficient way to additively render many line segments onto an image). i think it's simpler than the normal problems faced in HRC (how to make a single-pixel light have nice falloff and shadows) because in my setting you don't care about occlusion or raytracing - some process before you has already generated the line segments that should be splatted onto the screen, your job is only to do that (and you don't need to care about geometry that you have to occlude rays against, etc.). you also don't need to care about things like frustum-shaped intervals and can instead keep them as fixed-width parallelograms (i think), since there are no "source points" in this problem for light to emanate from and you don't have to make radiance falloff along an interval (which is what motivates frustums in regular HRC)

assuming the average length of a line segment is `O(w)` where `w`×`w` is the resolution of the image, using naive rasterization to draw all the lines will be `O(n*w)` where `n` is number of lines. the goal is to find something that's better than that, perhaps `O(n*log(w))` for writing the lines into a data structure and then `O(w^2*log(w))` for resolving the structure back into an image ready to be displayed on-screen. using the ideas from HRC cascade layout pretty much already achieves those time complexities, but of course the devil is in the details of what the best structure (cascade) layout and resolving (merging) process is that'll minimize how objectionable the artefacts are
Alexander: idk if you've been following that, but rasterizing a lot of line segments is mostly equivalent to implementing dual polygonal HRC gathering
Alexander: which is a process we don't have an efficient implementation for yet
Alexander: did you follow the primal vs dual line of research that we had in RC?
fad: vaguely
fad: traversing the line and inserting across different cascades works. you need to touch O(log(length of line)) probes. then you just merge down like in primal HRC. it works but it produces aliased lines. i'm guessing that wouldn't work well for dual
Alexander: aliased lines vs antialiased lines vs polygons they all compare the same way how polygonal compares to ray-based. dual implementation based on lines should have the same accuracy as primal using rays
Alexander: if primal works with rays, dual should work about the same way with lines
fad: i have no understanding of how occlusion works in dual
fad: luckily in my problem i don't care about occlusion which makes it simpler
Alexander: occlusion in dual gathering works the same way how occlusion works in primal extensions. occlusion in dual extensions works the same way how occlusion works in primal gathering
fad: in this line splatting thing i also don't really have the notion of a "probe" that intervals emanate out from in different directions. i just have standalone intervals by themselves. maybe it's not too different in practice/just an implementation detail, but it changes my mental model of the problem and makes it a bit easier to understand
Alexander: in sparse you have to use different probe structures for gathering and for extensions
Alexander: because you extend intervals around dense points and then you gather intervals that focus on dense points
fad: i don't understand how extensions work in sparse but i should ask about that in the proper channel
Alexander: well we don't have an implementation yet, but more of a plan
Alexander: i see how they can work in theory, but i don't see how to implement that in a nice way
fad: another difference is that in RC (first image), the interval you merge is from a greater distance along the ray. like if you are c0 red intervals, then you would look for c1 blue intervals that continue on from where your red interval ended. (black dashed line show cascade boundaries, black dot is the pixel we are gathering for)

whereas in line splatting (second image), you are instead "merging" directly in place. like the blue c2 segment gets added to the two red c1 segments which then each add to the four purple c0 segments  ("merging" in quotation because there's not really any transparency involved, so the merge operation is just adding a+b)
Alexander: that's what i mean by "extend around dense points": you extend both as your diagram on the right, but then _assign_ those intervals towards probes that are at a corresponding offset away, if those probes are dense
fad: i think this is actually a pretty big distinction. in RC you have to make sure the directions are somewhat aligned between different cascades when you're merging/extending them, that's why we had the whole foray into #linespace-exploration, trying to figure out the criteria for whether it was safe to merge two interval bundles and trying to figure out what the correct weights are, etc.

in the line splatting problem, that actually doesn't matter at all. we don't care about the direction the "radiance" is flowing, all we care is that a line's value is deposited to the correct pixels. when merging down, we can just directly visually compare whether the interval bundles overlap well in realspace before merging down, we don't have to think about whether they correctly overlap in linespace
Alexander: i think you'd have to care that in order for the result to be sampled uniformly
Alexander: in some sense
fad: i don't think you have to think about linespace at all. the merging weights just depend on whether the parallelogram shapes in each cascade overlap nicely in realspace
Alexander: i'm not sure why you're bringing up the linespace here, because we do everything in polygonal etc without invoking linespace at all
Alexander: unless it provided you with some insight? not sure
fad: even in polygonal if you didn't explicitly think about linespace, you're still thinking about the direction that radiance is flowing in within each polygon. you wouldn't think to merge one polygon with another polygon just because it connects spatially in realspace, you'd also make sure the radiance is flowing in the same direction - if one has radiance flowing in a direction perpendicular to the other then you wouldn't try merging them, because the linespace regions covered by each of those polygons would be completely disconnected
Alexander: here's an example of what i mean to assign these two red intervals to their corresponding probes. red and orange crosses are 2 probes that the two red intervals are assigned to
Alexander: essentially we just extend lines, but then assign those lines to probes that they belong to
Alexander: in dual you literally just connect polygons that touch each end to end
Alexander: they just happen to have a similar direction so it's ok to merge them
Alexander: their linespace representations aren't completely disconnected though, they're close
fad: here is an extreme example of what i mean: in RC, if the blue interval was a regular interval carrying radiance going towards the top right, and the two red intervals were wonky short-but-fat intervals that carried radiance going upwards, then you wouldn't try merging the two red intervals in order to reconstruct the blue interval. that would make no sense

but in my line splatting problem, it's actually ok to do this, because all we care about is making sure the blue shape resolves its value into smaller shapes that cover the same pixels. the shapes have no direction associated with them like intervals in RC do, so those blue and red arrows are meaningless for line splatting
Alexander: you need that for occlusion
fad: direction of the intervals is important in RC because that's the direction that shadows/occlusion is propagated in
fad: yeah i was typing that out
Alexander: but anything that splats lines should care about that
fad: in this linesplatting problem, there is no occlusion. so we don't have to bother with "directions". we only care about shapes

fad: and HRC lines will be even more artefacty than hardware rasterized aliased lines. because at least with hardware rasterized aliased lines, the space of lines is still ≈continuous. but with HRC lines, there are only like O(n^2) total lines so the space of all lines is discrete

fad: i was going to say, maybe that means you can additively render triangles faster than O(pixels covered) time too, but actually that’s a way easier problem than additively rendering lines. you can additively render a triangle in O(triangle perimeter) time by iterating over its edges and splatting the derivative of that edge to a buffer. then at the end you can integrate your image to render the filled triangles
fad: though i guess you could get even crazier and do it in O(log(perimeter)) time by using this log n line trick to splat the edge derivatives lol
Alexander: i mean.. can we render lines by splatting two "gradients" at every edge?

fad: if you were only rendering lines going in one direction then it would be easy. say horizontal lines only. then for all lines, splat +1 and -1 at the start and end pixels of the line respectively. then finally integrate over the rows of the image
fad: no clue how to make that work for multiple line directions
fad: unless you mean something different by splatting gradients at every edge?
Alexander: i guess i don't know how to do integration of that sort fast
Alexander: it's not an easy problem, i think
Alexander: nah they won't overlap well
Alexander: same here
Alexander: if you have 2 overlapping triangles then what even happens?
fad: here is an alternative explanation

imagine rendering a single constant-color triangle to an image

now take the partial derivative  of that resulting image in the x direction. i.e. d[x,y] = image[x,y] - image[x-1,y]

notice how d only has O(triangle perimeter) pixels filled in. so it’s actually faster to render the derivative by rasterizing the triangle edges than rendering the original image which requires filling all the triangle interior 

you can recover the original image by doing prefix sums across the rows of d


since additively rendering triangles is a linear operation, multiple triangles don’t interfere with each other
Alexander: well same can be done with lines
fad: this works for any constant-color shape btw
Alexander: just instead of rasterizing their perimeter we could rasterize just their end points
fad: but perimeter of a line is equal to length of line, so no win
Alexander: yes but treat "perimeter" as just end points
fad: well now you can’t just do prefix sums across x-direction like you could in the triangle case
fad: idk what you would do to recover it
Alexander: you can't it'd be more complex ye
Alexander: it's more like.. a way to encode arbitrarily many lines
Alexander: in O(N^2) storage
Alexander: finite lines too
fad: are you sure? what are you planning to store at each pixel value?
fad: here i used it to render circular bokeh blur in O(radius) time instead of O(radius^2) https://compute.toys/view/663

not really useful for depth of field though because idk how to make it interact with a depth map and have proper occlusion etc
fad: if the circle radius is O(n) where n is the width of the image, then the entire algorithm has runtime O(n^3). which is somehow slower than doing FFT, multiplying by sinc (which achieves circular bokeh), then doing IFFT, since that would be O(n² log n). man, why does FFT have to be so boring and always take the cake 🙄

fad: what’s this in response to?
fad: this actually makes me wonder: in HRC we go one extreme where we only share radiance along the direction parallel to the ray, no perpendicular sharing, which gives much stricter correctness guarantees - but on the other extreme, VRC shares less radiance parallel to the ray and shares more perpendicular to the ray, and it shows you don’t need to be that strictly correct in how you share radiance to still give meaningful renders. so what if we explored something in between? what if you shared as much radiance parallel to the ray as HRC but also still shared radiance perpendicular like in VRC?? that should give quality and efficiency better than VRC (because we share more parallel to the ray) while giving less aliasing artefacts than HRC (because we share perpendicular now)
fad: like we’ve explored the two extremes (HRC vs VRC), now what if you blend them
fad: HRC probes in C0 can “directly access” arbitrary CN as long as that C0 probe is close to a power of 2 boundary, that’s why HRC draws lines. VRC doesn’t exploit this at all so only draws a blob in the vicinity of the original probe. seems VRC is not sharing info as much as it could’ve
fad: i want to see a version of RC where enabling a single probe interval in a higher cascade splats a cone
fad: also makes sense that this is what you’d likely see in (sparse) 3D RC. an interval can be shared by a circular region on a surface. can’t do much better than that i think
fad: but in 2D, we can share to much more than just a circular region
fad: hence my desire to see a cone in 2D

fad: are you alluding to the idea that if a dual implementation produces X shape then that means a corresponding primal implementation would have to raytrace/extend an X shape? because i don’t think that’s the case since in vanilla RC we are never explicitly raytracing/extending circular blobs yet we end up rasterizing circular blobs
Alexander: in vanilla RC we absolutely approximate "gathering radiance from polygons", but since those polygons are all 1:2 pixels thick, we approximate them with rays
Alexander: what dual RC rasterizes as luminance in polygons, primal RC collects as radiance from polygons
Alexander: so in primal "rasterizing a shape" means we're collecting radiance from that shape and write the result into one interval. in dual "rasterizing a shape" means we're reading a value from the interval, then splatting fluence with that value over that shape onto the final image
fad: i think i’m confusing myself by conflating terminology between “rasterizing” a shape in the sense that HRC rasterizes lines and VRC rasterizes circular blobs when you enable 1 interval, vs the array of shapes that dual produces to be sent to a rasterizer
Alexander: i guess we can say that one HRC interval merges down to a line and VRC interval merges down to a blob
Alexander: but they collect gather radiance from polygons (that we approximate as rays) when working normally, and collecting all pixels in a polygon is similar to rasterization.
fad: perhaps replace “merges down” with “resolves”, “merging” typically involves an occlusion/transparency strategy that we’re ignoring for now. so
- an interval in HRC resolves to a line
- an interval in VRC resolves to a blob
Alexander: sure maybe
Alexander: so the idea is that if we construct a method that can resolve into a cone naturally, it should hopefully be possible to represent sharper cones than the lines that HRC can represent if we give them the same amount of storage
ion: I imagine down the line it would be useful to have a parameter to control the width of such a cone, to trade of shadow sharpness against memory/performance.
Alexander: also the min and max width of the cone
Alexander: @fad i think i see a path to solving your problem with rasterizing finite lines. imagine we have a bunch of nested grids like mips. so for example a mip 2 pixel is made of 4x4 mip 0 pixels. now, i think we know it is possible to do accurate line transformation as long as the line is infinite, we can do radon transform for that and fft and stuff. but the thing is, any finite line can be broken down into a quadtree of its segments where each segment is fully occupied, like on the image
Alexander: there are total logn segments per line, transforming all levels of the dense quadtree with a radon transform is N^2 log^2 N which is fine
Alexander: so the question can be just how do we render antialiased lines that span from edge to edge, which is just an easier problem to think about
fad: damnnnn that’s a good insight
fad: trying to render infinite lines seems like a much more fun problem to think about too
fad: always good when you can simplify the problem
Alexander: yeah it should keep overall complexity mostly the same
Alexander: log here log there nobody cares
fad: truth
Alexander: do you see a path to rendering antialiased infinite lines?
fad: no, i haven’t thought about the problem before
Alexander: but like.. you can't even encode them as NxN raster image
Alexander: because that doesn't support subpixel start/end points
Alexander: maybe you can do integer end points but then lines are antialiased? not sure if that's useful
fad: but it should lend itself better to things like radon transform that only operate on infinite lines. like you could just plot a pixel per line and then do radon transform which is a well established algorithm. now i’m not sure how nicely antialiased the lines will be from that, but at least it’s an existing algorithm to start work from
Alexander: if you have one pixel per line then line end points are still discrete
fad: like, maybe plotting antialiased lines just corresponds to doing some sort of bilinear splat for the pixel before you do the radon transform
Alexander: not sure if you consider that good enough
fad: but as you say the lines are still discrete so you’re essentially just blurring and not really antialiasing
Alexander: usually when you splat subpixel points you don't do bilinear splatting, you find which pixel you're in and increase its value
fad: yeah because normally you’re treating your point as infinitely small and trying to figure out what “bucket” to put it in
Alexander: maybe what you can do is when you splat a linespace pixel, you say that you render all lines that correspond to that pixel
Alexander: so at least if you add a bunch of them together they fill the space uniformly
fad: true 🧠
Alexander: line bundles
Alexander: again
Alexander: there has to be an fft way to do this
fad: fast radon transform does involve fft. but the thing that kind of concerns me is that radon transform involves doing polar coordinate transform :KEKW: surely that isn’t optimal for our case. these are just my immediate thoughts, i haven’t researched or properly thought about it yet, maybe there’s a way to render infinite antialiased lines efficiently without radon transform
Alexander: i'm sorry
Alexander: if it has fft, it's optimal
fad: new way to do single-pixel point splats just dropped:

1. FFT your image to work in frequency domain
2. for each point, add to the frequency domain image the appropriate sinc filter that corresponds to the point's single-pixel box (O(width^2) per point)
3. inverse FFT to reveal your splatted points

it's O(numPoints * width^2), optimal because it has FFT
Alexander: tru
Alexander: but also subpixel points
Alexander: it's like an sdf, allows subpixel point positions
ion: is it certain, that this  isn't enough? Maybe if the lines are "blurred" the right way this doesn't matter? I'm not trying to advocate the opposite, just am not 100% sure about it yet.
fad: tbh probably not in realistic scenes
fad: i guess the most important property is that uniform distribution of input lines fills pixels uniformly in the output
fad: i came up with another idea and i think you can do it in `O(sqrt(N*L)*W)` time for `N` line segments of average length `L` on a `W`×`W` image where `N*L > W`, by doing the endpoint derivative idea with multiple sweep directions (specifically `O(sqrt(N*L)/W)` sweep directions). there's a chance i did something wrong with my analysis but i think it's correct
fad: kinda seems like an impossible time complexity at first glance (because how can you have `sqrt(N)` growth? or why isn't there a `W^2` term if you're doing sweeps?), but it makes a little more sense when you see the condition that it only works for `N*L > W` (because otherwise the number of sweep directions `O(sqrt(N*L)/W)` would be less than 1 which doesn't make sense)
Alexander: how do you encode endpoint derivatives?
Alexander: i was thinking. maybe there's a way to encode half-planes?
Alexander: because a line is two half-planes
RadianceCausality: Pinned a message.
RadianceCausality: that might work but it'd only allow infinite lines right?
RadianceCausality: because a line / rectangle would be an intersection of 4 half-planes to cap the edges but then you have overlap and you can't take min because then you lose linearity
RadianceCausality: so you'd want to do quarter planes
fad: i'm thinking the same way as how you do it for triangles. take x derivative of the final target image

so if you wanted to render this red line, then instead simply splat +1 at the start of the line and -1 at the end of the line, then when you prefix sum over rows it will resolve to the red target line
fad: for slanted lines, you just repeat the above process for each run of horizontal pixels
fad: this will obviously be very inefficient for diagonal lines since then you will just be splatting as many +1s and -1s as there are pixels in the original line, so you get no performance win. that's where the idea of doing multiple sweep directions comes in, that way you don't deal with diagonal lines and your worst case becomes closer to the above image with an almost horizontal but not quite line
Alexander: hmmm
Alexander: so lines at an angle are more expensive
Alexander: i wonder if that's good enough
Alexander: so you want to have a bunch of sweep directions where zero gradient is considered to be at an angle
Alexander: and you define angles relative to that
fad: wdym "zero gradient"?
Alexander: well you define the "straight" direction relative to some other line
fad: ah yes exactly
Alexander: i wonder what the optimal number of directions is then
fad: that's what i'm writing up an explanation for now, it's how i arrived at `sqrt(N*L)*W` i mentioned before
Alexander: i wonder if there's a way to expand anything of this sort to antialiased lines
Alexander: really kind of tempted to say that lines themselves maybe can be antialiased but their end points just have to be integer
fad: if you do `D` sweep directions, then in the worst case you will have to process something like `L*sin(π/(2*D))` horizontal runs per line of length `L`. so then time complexity becomes `O(N*L*sin(π/(2*D)))` for splatting the endpoint derivatives, and then `O(W^2*D)` for the `D` fullscreen sweeps you'll have to do. so total time complexity `O(N*L*sin(π/(2*D)) + W^2*D)`

we can assume `D` is large enough that `π/(2*D)` is small so `sin(π/(2*D)) ≈ π/(2*D)` (small angle approximation). so the time complexity is `O(N*L*π/(2*D) + W^2*D) = O(N*L/D + W^2*D)` (because you can remove the `π/2` constant for big-O notation)

so now we want to find the number of sweep directions `D` that minimizes `N*L/D+W^2*D`. you can do that by setting the derivative w.r.t. `D` of that expression to 0 and solving for `D`:
```
0 = d/dD (N*L/D + W^2*D)
  = -N*L/D^2 + W^2
⇒ D = sqrt(N*L)/W
```(or really, `D` is `O(sqrt(N*L)/W)` because we removed the constants)
now plug that back into our time complexity, we get `O(N*L/(sqrt(N*L)/W) + W^2*sqrt(N*L)/W)`, i.e., `O(sqrt(N*L)*W)`
Alexander: yeah makes sense
Alexander: i like the derivative part, makes sense
Alexander: so the minimum is reached when the two terms are proportional to each other
fad: i think you can and it would involve splatting not just first derivatives but also second derivatives
fad: because for an anitaliased line, each horizontal run basically looks like a piecewise linear function that ramps up and then ramps down
fad: these green numbers is the second derivative splat
fad: when you're prefix summing for each row you would do this:```py
deriv2 = 0
deriv1 = 0
value = 0
for x in range(0, width):
    deriv2 += splat_buffer[x,y]
    deriv1 += deriv2
    value += deriv1
    result[x,y] = value
```
fad: and it'd naturally recreate those antialiased ramps
fad: admittedly, this is not the type of antialiasing you want for my caustics path tracer though. in that case you'd want each pixel to be coloured according to the length of line that passes through it
Alexander: but that doesn't match what antialiasing looks like as line vs aabb intersection length
Alexander: that's actually a pretty nonstandard case of AA, now that i think about it
fad: yeah doing that type of antialiasing (line vs aabb intersection length) would be terrible for UI and anything directly user-facing, but it is what you need for 2D path tracing where the idea is you have so many lines that they all average out
Alexander: still might be interesting to implement
Alexander: to see how it performs in practice
Alexander: so the way to optimize it curiously would be to make both passes have equal time
Alexander: you keep increasing the number of directions until sweeping has the same cost as writing lines
fad: kind of makes sense
Alexander: i wonder if there's some similar maths happening in 4x RC where the optimal choice is for all cascades to have the same cost
fad: but i feel like doing this algorithm in practice has some massive constant hiding behind the big-O notation or something, `O(sqrt(N*L)*W)` seems too OP
Alexander: well it only seems OP for low values of N*L
fad: even for big N*L it's pretty amazing that it grows by its square root
Alexander: i guess D can never be less than 1
fad: i guess you can assume `L = O(W)`, so time complexity simplifies to `O(sqrt(N) * W^1.5)`
Alexander: so the cost can never be less than `O(4*W*W)` because you need at least 4 directions
Alexander: i guess maybe 2
Alexander: but having just 2 sweeps is a good cost why not
Alexander: alright well this seems to be pretty straightforward to try
Alexander: you can just implement it and see how it goes
Alexander: if it works we'll make RC out of it
fad: minimum is 2 sweep directions because you need a horizontal and vertical sweep

the cost for two sweep directions would be `O(N*L + W^2)` (`N*L)` because worst case is diagonal lines where you need to splat derivatives for every pixel along the line basically, and `W^2` because you need to do fullscreen sweeps)
fad: so really 2 sweep directions is just worse than directly rasterizing the lines which would be `O(N*L)` without the `W^2` sweep term
fad: any constant number of sweep directions has the same problem. you need to make number of sweep directions dynamically depend on `N`,`L`,`W` in order to get good time complexity
Alexander: for RC we know that N*L ~ W^2
Alexander: because we end up rasterizing basically all discrete lines and there's total W^2 of them
Alexander: wait no
Alexander: it's W^3
Alexander: because it's W^2 lines each length of W
fad: so `N*L ~ W^3`
Alexander: when you get into fractional powers
Alexander: you know something weird is going on
fad: and `D = sqrt(N*L)/W ≈ sqrt(W)`
fad: at least that's bigger than 1
Alexander: D=sqrt(W) then?
Alexander: ok yeah i mean makes sense
Alexander: for 1024x1024 it's 32 directions
Alexander: makes sense
Alexander: you can do 32 sweeps in realtime
fad: so `N = W^2`, `L=W`
then our time complexity derived [here](https://discord.com/channels/1277053173764460625/1304556173671927878/1486151316907622452) becomes `O(sqrt(W^2 * W) * W)` = `O(W^2.5)` aka `O(W^2 sqrt(W))`
fad: strictly worse than `O(W^2 log W)`
Alexander: so RC wins over sweeps again?

fad: if your scene only has perfectly diffuse surfaces, then i think what you’re describing is equivalent to [radiosity](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiosity_(computer_graphics)>). but that becomes way more complicated as soon as you start having non-diffuse surfaces

Alexander: no there has to be a way to cast all rays at the same time, but you need to multiply each ray by BRDF
Alexander: essentially you're gathering all rays hitting a given surface patch and calculating all rays that they result in
fad: idk how that would work, when you cast all rays at the same time, multiple different rays may end up hitting the same pixel but that pixel will emit different radiance in different directions because of brdf
fad: so it’s not just that you multiply the gather by the brdf, you also need to multiply the scatter by the brdf
Alexander: you iterate over all gathered rays and over all scattered rays and multiply each pair by its brdf
